> **Core Idea:** The basic meter movement is a *current-operated* device. Voltage is converted to current by a series resistor. By changing the resistor value, you change the voltage range — but the current through the movement at full scale remains constant at $I_m$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle

When an unknown DC voltage $V$ is applied across the voltmeter terminals, a current $I$ flows through the series combination of the multiplier resistor $R_s$ and the meter movement $R_m$.

By Ohm's Law, the deflection current is:

$$I = \frac{V}{R_s + R_m}$$

For *full-scale deflection*, $I = I_m$, so:

$$I_m = \frac{V}{R_s + R_m}$$

Solving for the required multiplier resistance $R_s$:

$$R_s = \frac{V}{I_m} - R_m$$

This is the **fundamental equation** of every DC voltmeter.

## 2.2 Sensitivity (Ohms-per-Volt Rating)

The **sensitivity** of a voltmeter is defined as the ratio of its total input resistance to the full-scale voltage it can measure, expressed in **kΩ/V**:

$$S = \frac{R_s + R_m}{V} = \frac{1}{I_m} \quad \text{[Ω/V]}$$

A movement with $I_m = 50\ \mu A$ has $S = 20\ \text{k}\Omega/\text{V}$.
A movement with $I_m = 1\ \text{mA}$ has $S = 1\ \text{k}\Omega/\text{V}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Higher sensitivity = less loading error** when measuring high-impedance circuits. A 20 kΩ/V meter on a 10 V range presents 200 kΩ to the circuit; a 1 kΩ/V meter on the same range presents only 10 kΩ and distorts the measurement significantly.

## 2.3 Two Practical Design Configurations

### (a) Individual Multiplier Resistor Configuration
Each voltage range has its own **dedicated** multiplier resistor. The range switch connects one of them in series with the movement.

- **Advantages:** Trivially designed; one resistor per range.
- **Disadvantages:** Requires many precision resistors; switch sees full input voltage (high insulation needed); expensive for many ranges.

### (b) Series-String (Ayrton–Perry) Configuration ⭐ *Preferred*
A single chain of resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, \dots, R_n$ is permanently connected in series with the movement. The range switch taps off at intermediate nodes. The total resistance corresponds to the **largest** range.

- **Advantages:** The switch only carries the movement current (small), so insulation is easy; economical (one shared string); uniform sensitivity.
- **Disadvantages:** Slightly more involved calculation; a fault in one resistor affects all higher ranges.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Condition |
|---|----------|---------|-------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Multiplier Resistance | $R_s = \dfrac{V}{I_m} - R_m$ | Ω | For a *single* full-scale voltage $V$ |
| 2 | Meter Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{1}{I_m}$ | Ω/V (or kΩ/V) | Depends **only** on $I_m$ |
| 3 | Total Voltmeter Resistance | $R_v = S \times V = \dfrac{V}{I_m}$ | Ω | $R_v = R_s + R_m$ |
| 4 | Series-String Total | $R_{total} = \dfrac{V_{max}}{I_m}$ | Ω | For highest range $V_{max}$ |
| 5 | Individual Tap $i$ (cumulative) | $\sum_{k=1}^{i} R_k = \dfrac{V_i}{I_m} - R_m$ | Ω | Where $V_i$ is the $i^{th}$ range |
| 6 | Loading Error | $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{R_{source}}{R_{source} + R_v} \times 100$ | % | $R_v$ should be $\geq 20 \times R_{source}$ for <5% error |
| 7 | Power Dissipation in Multiplier | $P_{R_s} = I_m^2 \cdot R_s$ | W | Determines resistor wattage rating |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: Table-Safe Math:** Notice the use of `\dfrac` and `\times` instead of `|` (vertical bar) — this preserves the markdown table structure as mandated by KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bench-top analog multimeters** (e.g., fluke 45, analog service meters) use exactly this principle for DC voltage ranges.
- **Calibration laboratories** still prefer analog multirange voltmeters for stable references.
- The same series-string concept extends to **ammeters** (using Ayrton–Perry shunts) and **ohmmeters** (using a series battery arrangement).
- Modern **digital multimeters (DMMs)** internally use a fixed front-end attenuator and ADC, but the *sensitivity concept* (input impedance) originates from this classical analysis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $R_s = \dfrac{V}{I_m} - R_m$

**Step 1 — Model the voltmeter.**
The basic movement and the multiplier form a simple series loop. Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop:

$$V = I \cdot R_s + I \cdot R_m = I (R_s + R_m)$$

**Step 2 — Impose the full-scale condition.**
The pointer of the movement reaches the end of the scale when the current through the coil equals its rated full-scale value $I_m$. Set $I = I_m$:

$$V = I_m (R_s + R_m)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R_s$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_m \cdot R_s + I_m \cdot R_m &= V \\
I_m \cdot R_s &= V - I_m \cdot R_m \\
R_s &= \frac{V - I_m \cdot R_m}{I_m} \\
R_s &= \frac{V}{I_m} - R_m \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**
- $R_s$ grows linearly with the desired range $V$.
- A movement with very small $I_m$ (highly sensitive) needs a *larger* $R_s$ to absorb the extra voltage.
- The voltmeter's total input resistance is $R_v = R_s + R_m = V/I_m$, which gives the **sensitivity** $S = R_v / V = 1/I_m$ in Ω/V.

## 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example — Series-String (Ayrton–Perry) Design

**Given Data:**
- Basic movement: $I_m = 50\ \mu\text{A} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A}$
- Internal resistance: $R_m = 1\ \text{k}\Omega = 1000\ \Omega$
- Required full-scale ranges: **3 V, 10 V, 30 V, 100 V, 300 V**

**Step 1 — Compute the sensitivity (ohms-per-volt).**

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{1}{I_m} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{-6}} \\
  &= 20{,}000\ \Omega/\text{V} = 20\ \text{k}\Omega/\text{V}
\end{aligned}$$

This single number now defines the voltmeter's total resistance for *any* range.

**Step 2 — Determine the total string resistance.**
The series-string's total resistance must satisfy the *highest* range (300 V):

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{total} &= S \times V_{max} = 20{,}000 \times 300 \\
          &= 6{,}000{,}000\ \Omega = 6\ \text{M}\Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute cumulative resistance up to each tap point.**
Using the formula $\sum_{k=1}^{i} R_k = S \cdot V_i - R_m$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{For 3 V range:}   &\quad R_1 = (20{,}000 \times 3) - 1{,}000 = 60{,}000 - 1{,}000 = 59\ \text{k}\Omega \\
\text{For 10 V range:}  &\quad R_1 + R_2 = (20{,}000 \times 10) - 1{,}000 = 199\ \text{k}\Omega \\
\text{For 30 V range:}  &\quad R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = (20{,}000 \times 30) - 1{,}000 = 599\ \text{k}\Omega \\
\text{For 100 V range:} &\quad R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_4 = (20{,}000 \times 100) - 1{,}000 = 1{,}999\ \text{k}\Omega \\
\text{For 300 V range:} &\quad R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_4 + R_5 = (20{,}000 \times 300) - 1{,}000 = 5{,}999\ \text{k}\Omega = 6\ \text{M}\Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the individual segment resistances.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= 59\ \text{k}\Omega \\
R_2 &= 199\ \text{k}\Omega - 59\ \text{k}\Omega = 140\ \text{k}\Omega \\
R_3 &= 599\ \text{k}\Omega - 199\ \text{k}\Omega = 400\ \text{k}\Omega \\
R_4 &= 1{,}999\ \text{k}\Omega - 599\ \text{k}\Omega = 1{,}400\ \text{k}\Omega \\
R_5 &= 5{,}999\ \text{k}\Omega - 1{,}999\ \text{k}\Omega = 4{,}000\ \text{k}\Omega = 4\ \text{M}\Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Verification (cumulative).**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_4 + R_5 + R_m &= 59 + 140 + 400 + 1400 + 4000 + 1\ (\text{in k}\Omega) \\
                                   &= 6{,}000\ \text{k}\Omega = 6\ \text{M}\Omega \\
                                   &= \frac{300\ \text{V}}{50\ \mu\text{A}} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check on the 100 V range.**
If 100 V is applied, total resistance = $1.999\ \text{M}\Omega + 1\ \text{k}\Omega = 2\ \text{M}\Omega$.
Current = $100 / 2{,}000{,}000 = 50\ \mu\text{A}$ ✓ — full-scale deflection achieved.

> [!TIP]
> **Design Insight:** Notice $R_5$ (the final segment) is *huge* (4 MΩ) because it must absorb the bulk of the 300 V range. In practice, this segment is often built from a chain of smaller resistors in series to handle the voltage standoff and to allow trimming for calibration tolerance.

## 3.3 Loading-Error Analysis (Companion Derivation)

If the voltmeter ($R_v = S \cdot V$) is connected across a source of Thévenin voltage $V_{th}$ and source resistance $R_{th}$, the actual voltage indicated is:

$$V_{ind} = V_{th} \cdot \frac{R_v}{R_{th} + R_v}$$

The **percentage loading error** is therefore:

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{V_{th} - V_{ind}}{V_{th}} \times 100 = \frac{R_{th}}{R_{th} + R_v} \times 100$$

**Numerical example:** Measuring a circuit with $R_{th} = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$ using a 20 kΩ/V meter on the 10 V range ($R_v = 200\ \text{k}\Omega$):

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{50{,}000}{50{,}000 + 200{,}000} \times 100 = 20\%$$

This is unacceptably high — illustrating why **high-sensitivity movements** are essential in voltmeters.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Multirange Voltmeter — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *functional architecture* of a multirange voltmeter (D'Arsonval movement + series-string multipliers + selector switch), clearly showing the signal path, the switching logic, and the role of each multiplier segment.

```mermaid
graph TD
    node_src["UNKNOWN DC VOLTAGE Vx ACROSS TEST TERMINALS"] --> node_term["INPUT TERMINALS + AND -"]
    node_term --> node_sw["ROTARY RANGE SELECTOR SWITCH POSITION P1 P2 P3 P4 P5"]
    node_sw --> node_r1["R1 = 59 kOHM FOR 3 V RANGE"]
    node_sw --> node_r2["R2 = 140 kOHM FOR 10 V RANGE"]
    node_sw --> node_r3["R3 = 400 kOHM FOR 30 V RANGE"]
    node_sw --> node_r4["R4 = 1.4 MOHM FOR 100 V RANGE"]
    node_sw --> node_r5["R5 = 4 MOHM FOR 300 V RANGE"]
    node_r1 --> node_rm["RM = 1 kOHM BASIC MOVEMENT COIL"]
    node_r2 --> node_rm
    node_r3 --> node_rm
    node_r4 --> node_rm
    node_r5 --> node_rm
    node_rm --> node_disp["POINTER DEFLECTION INDICATING Vx FULL SCALE AT IM = 50 MICROAMP"]
    node_rm --> node_term
    node_disp --> node_sens["SENSITIVITY S = 1 OVER IM = 20 kOHM PER VOLT CONSTANT FOR ALL RANGES"]
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The unknown DC voltage enters the input terminals.
- The rotary switch selects *one* of five multiplier segments.
- The selected segment, together with the basic movement $R_m$, forms a complete series circuit.
- At full-scale deflection, the current through the movement is always $I_m = 50\ \mu\text{A}$ regardless of range — this is what guarantees a **linear scale** and **uniform sensitivity**.

## 4.2 Schematic-Style Block Diagram (Ayrton–Perry Series String)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["+ Terminal"] --> B["R1 = 59 kOhm"]
    B --> C["R2 = 140 kOhm"]
    C --> D["R3 = 400 kOhm"]
    D --> E["R4 = 1.4 MOhm"]
    E --> F["R5 = 4 MOhm"]
    F --> G["Rm = 1 kOhm Movement"]
    G --> H["- Terminal"]
    TAP1["Tap P1: 3 V range"] -.-> B
    TAP2["Tap P2: 10 V range"] -.-> C
    TAP3["Tap P3: 30 V range"] -.-> D
    TAP4["Tap P4: 100 V range"] -.-> E
    TAP5["Tap P5: 300 V range"] -.-> F
```

**Engineering Interpretation:** This shows the physical series-string. Each tap point is connected to a separate contact of the rotary switch. The *switch wiper* determines how much of the string is "in circuit" for the chosen range.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short Answer)**

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define sensitivity of a voltmeter and state its unit. A voltmeter has a sensitivity of 20 kΩ/V. Calculate the total resistance offered by the voltmeter on the 50 V range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The sensitivity of a voltmeter is defined as the ratio of its total input resistance to its full-scale voltage, expressed in **ohms per volt (Ω/V)** or **kΩ/V**. It is mathematically the reciprocal of the full-scale current of the basic movement:
$$S = \frac{1}{I_m}$$

**Calculation (1.5 marks):** Given $S = 20\ \text{k}\Omega/\text{V}$ and $V_{FS} = 50\ \text{V}$:

$$R_v = S \times V_{FS} = 20{,}000 \times 50 = 1{,}000{,}000\ \Omega = 1\ \text{M}\Omega$$

**Total resistance on the 50 V range = 1 MΩ.**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why are multiplier resistors connected in series with a PMMC movement when used as a voltmeter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The PMMC (D'Arsonval) movement is inherently a *current-measuring* device that gives full-scale deflection at a very small current $I_m$ (typically µA). It has a low internal resistance $R_m$ (a few hundred ohms) and would burn out if connected directly across even a few volts.

To use it as a **voltmeter**, a large precision resistor $R_s$ called the **multiplier resistor** is connected in series with the movement. The multiplier drops the *excess voltage* so that only the safe current $I_m$ flows through the movement at the full-scale voltage. By choosing $R_s = V/I_m - R_m$, the instrument can be made to read *any* full-scale voltage directly. Using a *set* of such multipliers (or a tapped series string) yields a **multirange voltmeter**.

---

## **Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Rule:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The model solutions below show the full marking scheme that a KTU examiner will follow.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the multiplier resistance required to convert a basic D'Arsonval movement into a voltmeter of full-scale range $V$. (7 marks)**

**(b) A basic D'Arsonval movement has $I_m = 50\ \mu\text{A}$ and $R_m = 500\ \Omega$. Design a multirange DC voltmeter using the **series-string (Ayrton–Perry)** method for the ranges **5 V, 25 V, 100 V, and 250 V**. Compute each multiplier segment and the sensitivity. (7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Solution to (a) — Derivation of $R_s$ (7 marks)

**Model:** The voltmeter is modelled as a series circuit of $R_s$ and $R_m$ across the unknown voltage $V$. By **KVL**:

$$V = I \cdot (R_s + R_m) \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Full-scale condition:** For maximum pointer deflection, the current through the movement must equal its rated full-scale value $I_m$. Therefore, at full scale:

$$V = I_m \cdot (R_s + R_m) \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Solving for $R_s$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_m \cdot R_s + I_m \cdot R_m &= V \\
I_m \cdot R_s &= V - I_m \cdot R_m \\
R_s &= \frac{V}{I_m} - R_m \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
- $R_s$ is linearly proportional to the desired full-scale range $V$.
- For very small $I_m$ (highly sensitive movement), $R_s$ is large → less loading of the circuit under test.
- The total voltmeter resistance is $R_v = R_s + R_m = V / I_m$, which gives the **sensitivity** $S = 1/I_m$ in Ω/V — a constant for a given movement.

#### Solution to (b) — Series-String Design (7 marks)

**Given:** $I_m = 50\ \mu\text{A}$, $R_m = 500\ \Omega$, ranges = 5 V, 25 V, 100 V, 250 V.

**Step 1 — Sensitivity:**
$$S = \frac{1}{I_m} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 20{,}000\ \Omega/\text{V} = 20\ \text{k}\Omega/\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Total string resistance (for the highest range 250 V):**
$$R_{total} = S \times V_{max} = 20{,}000 \times 250 = 5\ \text{M}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Cumulative resistance up to each tap:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{5 V range:}  &\quad R_1 = (20{,}000 \times 5) - 500 = 100{,}000 - 500 = 99{,}500\ \Omega = 99.5\ \text{k}\Omega \\
\text{25 V range:} &\quad R_1 + R_2 = (20{,}000 \times 25) - 500 = 500{,}000 - 500 = 499.5\ \text{k}\Omega \\
\text{100 V range:}&\quad R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = (20{,}000 \times 100) - 500 = 1{,}999.5\ \text{k}\Omega \\
\text{250 V range:}&\quad R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_4 = (20{,}000 \times 250) - 500 = 4{,}999.5\ \text{k}\Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Individual segment values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= 99.5\ \text{k}\Omega \\
R_2 &= 499.5 - 99.5 = 400\ \text{k}\Omega \\
R_3 &= 1{,}999.5 - 499.5 = 1{,}500\ \text{k}\Omega = 1.5\ \text{M}\Omega \\
R_4 &= 4{,}999.5 - 1{,}999.5 = 3{,}000\ \text{k}\Omega = 3\ \text{M}\Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**
$$R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_4 + R_m = 99.5 + 400 + 1500 + 3000 + 0.5\ (\text{k}\Omega) = 5{,}000\ \text{k}\Omega = 5\ \text{M}\Omega \quad \checkmark$$

**Result:** Sensitivity = **20 kΩ/V**; Segments = **99.5 kΩ, 400 kΩ, 1.5 MΩ, 3 MΩ**.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Explain with neat sketches the **series-string (Ayrton–Perry)** and **individual multiplier** methods of designing a multirange voltmeter. Compare the two. (7 marks)**

**(b) A voltmeter uses a 1 mA movement with coil resistance 100 Ω. It reads full-scale at 150 V. Find: (i) the multiplier resistance, (ii) the sensitivity in kΩ/V, (iii) the total voltmeter resistance, and (iv) the loading error when measuring a source of 100 V with internal resistance 5 kΩ. (7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Solution to (a) — Two Design Methods (7 marks)

**Individual Multiplier Method (3.5 marks):**
In this method, each range has a *dedicated* multiplier resistor $R_{s1}, R_{s2}, \dots, R_{sn}$ connected in series with the basic movement via a multi-position rotary switch. For the $i^{th}$ range $V_i$:

$$R_{si} = \frac{V_i}{I_m} - R_m$$

The range switch carries the *full input voltage* and must therefore be heavily insulated. A separate precision resistor is required per range, which makes this method expensive for many ranges but easy to design and troubleshoot.

**Series-String (Ayrton–Perry) Method (3.5 marks):**
Here, a *single chain* of resistors $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n$ is permanently connected in series with the movement. The total resistance equals $V_{max}/I_m$. The range switch taps off at intermediate nodes, so the switch carries only the small movement current $I_m$. The design is more economical and the switch insulation is straightforward. A fault in one segment affects all higher ranges.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Individual Multiplier | Series-String (Ayrton–Perry) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of precision resistors | One per range | One shared string |
| Switch voltage rating | Full input voltage | Only $V_{R_m} = I_m \cdot R_m$ (small) |
| Cost for many ranges | High | Lower |
| Design complexity | Simple | Moderate |
| Sensitivity uniformity | Uniform | Uniform |
| Effect of single resistor fault | Affects only that range | Affects all higher ranges |

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $I_m = 1\ \text{mA} = 10^{-3}\ \text{A}$, $R_m = 100\ \Omega$, $V_{FS} = 150\ \text{V}$, $V_{th} = 100\ \text{V}$, $R_{th} = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$.

**(i) Multiplier resistance (2 marks):**
$$R_s = \frac{V_{FS}}{I_m} - R_m = \frac{150}{10^{-3}} - 100 = 150{,}000 - 100 = 149{,}900\ \Omega = 149.9\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**(ii) Sensitivity (1.5 marks):**
$$S = \frac{1}{I_m} = \frac{1}{10^{-3}} = 1{,}000\ \Omega/\text{V} = 1\ \text{k}\Omega/\text{V}$$

**(iii) Total voltmeter resistance (1.5 marks):**
$$R_v = R_s + R_m = 149{,}900 + 100 = 150{,}000\ \Omega = 150\ \text{k}\Omega$$
(Equivalently, $R_v = S \times V_{FS} = 1000 \times 150 = 150\ \text{k}\Omega \ \checkmark$)

**(iv) Loading error (2 marks):**
$$V_{ind} = V_{th} \cdot \frac{R_v}{R_{th} + R_v} = 100 \times \frac{150{,}000}{5{,}000 + 150{,}000} = 100 \times 0.9677 = 96.77\ \text{V}$$

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{100 - 96.77}{100} \times 100 = 3.23\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract $R_m$** in the multiplier formula — the most common error. Always show the derivation $R_s = V/I_m - R_m$, not $R_s = V/I_m$.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency** in series-string problems — keeping everything in kΩ or MΩ throughout a calculation; mismatched units give wildly wrong answers.
> 3. **Confusing $R_s$ with $R_v$** — the multiplier is *only the external* resistor; the voltmeter's total input resistance is $R_v = R_s + R_m$.
> 4. **Loading error direction** — students compute the *indicated* voltage correctly but report the error as a positive number without clarifying that it is a *under-reading* (the meter reads *less* than the true value).
> 5. **Switch-rating oversight** — when asked to compare the two design methods, students often forget to mention that the Ayrton–Perry switch sees only the small $V_{R_m}$ while the individual-multiplier switch sees the *full input voltage* — this distinction alone is worth 1–2 marks.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember** *(Rapid Revision Checklist)*

- ✅ A multirange voltmeter = basic D'Arsonval movement + calibrated series multipliers.
- ✅ **Core formula:** $R_s = V/I_m - R_m$ — *always* subtract the coil resistance.
- ✅ **Sensitivity** $S = 1/I_m$ in **Ω/V** (or kΩ/V) — depends *only* on the basic movement, *not* on the range.
- ✅ **Total voltmeter resistance** on any range $V$ is $R_v = S \times V$.
- ✅ **Two design methods:** *Individual multiplier* (one resistor per range, switch sees full voltage) vs. *Ayrton–Perry series string* (one shared chain, switch sees only $I_m \cdot R_m$).
- ✅ **Series-string design:** total resistance corresponds to the *highest* range; tap-off points satisfy $\sum_{k=1}^{i} R_k = S \cdot V_i - R_m$.
- ✅ **Loading error** = $\dfrac{R_{th}}{R_{th} + R_v} \times 100\%$ — higher sensitivity (smaller $I_m$) reduces loading error.
- ✅ **A fault in the $i^{th}$ segment** of a series string affects all ranges *higher* than $V_i$, but not lower ones.
- ✅ **Power rating** of each multiplier segment: $P = I_m^2 \cdot R_k$ — usually rated at 1%–5% tolerance, ±25 ppm/°C.
- ✅ **Linear scale:** Because $I$ is proportional to $V$ via Ohm's law, the scale of a PMMC voltmeter is *uniform* (unlike ohmmeter or AC rectifier-type meters).
- ✅ KTU-favourite: be ready to be asked to **design** a series string for arbitrary ranges — practice the cumulative-resistance method until it is automatic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extending the Range of Voltmeter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT412 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS MEASUREMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Extending the Range of Voltmeter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extending the Range of Voltmeter** refers to the technique of connecting a **high-value precision resistor (called a Multiplier Resistor, $R_s$)** in *series* with the basic measuring instrument (Permanent Magnet Moving Coil — PMMC movement) so that the instrument can measure voltages **far greater than its inherent full-scale voltage rating**, without damaging the delicate coil.

Mathematically, the **Multiplying Factor** is the ratio of the extended full-scale voltage to the original meter full-scale voltage:

$$m = \frac{V}{V_m}$$

where:
- $V$ = Extended (new) full-scale voltage to be measured
- $V_m$ = Original (intrinsic) full-scale voltage of the PMMC movement

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Tap" Analogy

Imagine a thin **drinking straw** (the PMMC coil) that can safely carry only a small trickle of water (small current $\approx 50\,\mu A$ to $10\,mA$). If you connect a fire hose directly to it, the straw will burst. However, if you attach a long, narrow **flow-restrictor pipe** (the multiplier resistor $R_s$) **before** the straw, the pipe absorbs most of the pressure drop, so the straw only experiences a small, safe trickle — even though the total pressure at the inlet is huge. That restrictor pipe is exactly what a **multiplier resistor** does to voltage.

## 1.3 The Underlying Physical Principle

A PMMC movement has two inherent electrical parameters:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Full-Scale Deflection Current | $I_{fsd}$ | $50\,\mu A$ to $10\,mA$ | Maximum current the coil can carry |
| Internal Coil Resistance | $R_m$ | $50\,\Omega$ to $2\,k\Omega$ | Winding resistance of the moving coil |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (KTU Board Favourite):** Because the current through the coil must NEVER exceed $I_{fsd}$, the maximum voltage it can measure **directly** is just $V_m = I_{fsd} \times R_m$, which is tiny (e.g., $50\,\mu A \times 1000\,\Omega = 50\,mV$). To read real-world voltages like $300\,V$, we MUST drop the excess voltage across an external series resistor.

## 1.4 Sensitivity of a Voltmeter — A Critical Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensitivity ($S_v$)** is defined as the total resistance of the voltmeter per volt of full-scale deflection. It is the reciprocal of the full-scale current.
>
> $$S_v = \frac{1}{I_{fsd}} \;\; \left[\frac{\Omega}{V}\right]$$

A voltmeter with $I_{fsd} = 50\,\mu A$ has $S_v = 20{,}000\,\Omega/V$ — this is the "quality figure" of merit. **Higher sensitivity = better voltmeter (less loading error).**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Logic — How the Multiplier Resistor Works

The working principle is rooted in the **Voltage Divider Rule** applied to a series circuit. The operational logic in 5 steps:

1. The unknown DC voltage $V$ to be measured is applied across the **series combination** of the multiplier resistor $R_s$ and the meter movement $R_m$.
2. The same small current $I_{fsd}$ flows through both $R_s$ and $R_m$ (series circuit principle).
3. The voltage $V$ is therefore split into two parts:
   - $V_m = I_{fsd} \cdot R_m$ → across the meter (this drives the pointer to full scale)
   - $V_s = I_{fsd} \cdot R_s$ → across the multiplier (this is the "excess" voltage being absorbed)
4. By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL): $V = V_m + V_s$.
5. The multiplier is designed such that the meter pointer reaches full scale **exactly** when the full external voltage $V$ is applied.

## 2.2 Boundary Conditions & Design Limits

- **Lower bound of $R_s$:** $R_s \geq 0$ (when $V = V_m$, no multiplier is needed — voltmeter is used directly).
- **Upper bound of $R_s$:** Practically limited by:
  - Resistor power rating: $P_{R_s} = I_{fsd}^2 \cdot R_s$
  - Temperature stability requirements
  - Insulator leakage (for very high-voltage multipliers)

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Concept

> The "why" lies in **Galvanometer Protection + Range Extension**. A basic D'Arsonval movement is essentially a milliammeter or microammeter. The moment you "convert" it into a voltmeter by adding a series resistor, you have built a **voltmeter with extended range**. The series multiplier is the *sine qua non* of every analog DC voltmeter ever made.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always escape pipe characters in tables using `\vert` to prevent Markdown table breakage.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{V}{V_m}$ | Multiplying Factor (ratio) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $R_s = R_m (m - 1)$ | Series Multiplier Resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | $R_s = \dfrac{V - V_m}{I_{fsd}}$ | Direct Computation Form | $\Omega$ |
| 4 | $R_{total} = R_m + R_s = m \cdot R_m$ | Total Voltmeter Resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 5 | $S_v = \dfrac{1}{I_{fsd}}$ | Voltmeter Sensitivity | $\Omega / V$ |
| 6 | $P_{R_s} = I_{fsd}^2 \cdot R_s$ | Power Dissipation in Multiplier | $W$ |
| 7 | $\gamma = \dfrac{R_m}{R_s}$ | Resistance Ratio (used in error analysis) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $I_{fsd} = \dfrac{V_m}{R_m}$ | Full-Scale Current from Meter Specs | $A$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Multi-range analog multimeters** (Fluke 87V, Simpson 260, etc.) all use switched multiplier networks.
- **High-voltage probe multipliers** (HV probes up to 40 kV) use this exact principle with precision thick-film resistors in oil-filled enclosures.
- **Electrostatic voltmeters** use capacitive dividers, but for PMMC-based meters, resistive multiplication is universal.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — The Master Formula for Multiplier Resistance

**Given:**
- Basic meter: resistance $R_m$, full-scale current $I_{fsd}$
- Required extended range voltage: $V$

**To Find:** Required value of the series multiplier resistance $R_s$.

### Step 1 — Write the total voltage across the series chain

The voltmeter (meter + multiplier) is connected directly across the unknown voltage $V$. Therefore, by KVL:

$$V = V_{R_s} + V_{R_m}$$

where $V_{R_s}$ is the voltage drop across the multiplier and $V_{R_m}$ is the voltage drop across the meter coil.

### Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law across each element

Since the same current $I_{fsd}$ flows through the series combination at full-scale deflection:

$$V_{R_s} = I_{fsd} \cdot R_s$$

$$V_{R_m} = I_{fsd} \cdot R_m$$

### Step 3 — Substitute back into KVL

$$V = I_{fsd} \cdot R_s + I_{fsd} \cdot R_m$$

$$V = I_{fsd}(R_s + R_m)$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $R_s$

$$R_s + R_m = \frac{V}{I_{fsd}}$$

$$R_s = \frac{V}{I_{fsd}} - R_m$$

### Step 5 — Express in terms of multiplying factor

Divide numerator and denominator by $V_m = I_{fsd} \cdot R_m$:

$$R_s = R_m \left(\frac{V}{V_m} - 1\right) = R_m(m - 1)$$

This is the **fundamental KTU formula** for extending the range of a voltmeter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Tip:** Always remember that the *multiplier factor $m$ tells you how many times bigger* the new range is than the original. For $m = 10$, the new range is 10× the original.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Multi-Range Voltmeter Using Individual Multipliers (Series Multiplier Configuration)

A **multi-range voltmeter** uses a selector switch and several multiplier resistors, one for each range. The switch connects the appropriate $R_s$ in series with the meter.

**Let the ranges be:** $V_1, V_2, V_3, \ldots, V_n$ (all greater than $V_m$).

**The required multipliers are:**

$$R_{s1} = R_m(m_1 - 1) = R_m\left(\frac{V_1}{V_m} - 1\right)$$

$$R_{s2} = R_m(m_2 - 1) = R_m\left(\frac{V_2}{V_m} - 1\right)$$

$$\vdots$$

$$R_{sn} = R_m(m_n - 1) = R_m\left(\frac{V_n}{V_m} - 1\right)$$

**The "individual" or "true series" configuration** uses one independent resistor per range. Each $R_{si}$ is calculated as $R_{si} = R_{total,i} - R_{total,i-1}$, where $R_{total,i}$ is the total resistance needed for range $i$.

For example, the multiplier resistor for range 2 is:

$$R_{s2} = (R_m + R_{s1}) \cdot \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1} - 1\right)$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — The Ayrton Shunt / Universal Multiplier Network

The **Ayrton shunt** (universal multiplier) uses a *single tapped resistor chain*. This eliminates the need for a multi-pole switch and is more reliable because the meter is never left open-circuited during range switching.

**Circuit Topology:** A single resistor $R$ is tapped at points to create sections. The PMMC meter is connected between the common (low) terminal and the **movable selector tap**.

Let the total multiplier chain resistance be $R_1 + R_2 + R_3$ for three ranges $V_1, V_2, V_3$ where $V_1 < V_2 < V_3$.

**Total chain resistance:**

$$R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = \frac{V_3}{I_{fsd}} - R_m$$

**Tap currents** (for each range the current through the meter must be $I_{fsd}$):

For range $V_1$ (smallest): current $I_1$ enters chain, splits between $R_1$ (above the tap) and the meter+($R_2 + R_3$):

$$I_{fsd} \cdot R_m + I_{fsd}(R_2 + R_3) + I_1 R_1 = V_1$$

For range $V_2$: $I_2$ enters, splits between $R_1 + R_2$ and meter+$R_3$:

$$I_{fsd} \cdot R_m + I_{fsd} R_3 + I_2 (R_1 + R_2) = V_2$$

For range $V_3$: All chain is in series with the meter:

$$I_{fsd} \cdot R_m + I_{fsd}(R_1 + R_2 + R_3) = V_3$$

These three equations yield the values of $R_1, R_2, R_3$ via simultaneous solution.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Board Problem)

**Problem Statement:**
A basic PMMC movement has $R_m = 100\,\Omega$ and $I_{fsd} = 1\,mA$. Design a voltmeter to read up to (a) $10\,V$ and (b) $50\,V$. Also calculate sensitivity.

### Part (a) — Range Extension to 10 V

**Step 1 — Compute meter full-scale voltage:**

$$V_m = I_{fsd} \cdot R_m = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times 100 = 0.1\,V$$

**Step 2 — Compute multiplying factor:**

$$m_1 = \frac{V_1}{V_m} = \frac{10}{0.1} = 100$$

**Step 3 — Compute multiplier resistance:**

$$R_{s1} = R_m (m_1 - 1) = 100 \times (100 - 1) = 100 \times 99 = 9900\,\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Compute total voltmeter resistance:**

$$R_{total,1} = R_m + R_{s1} = 100 + 9900 = 10{,}000\,\Omega$$

### Part (b) — Range Extension to 50 V

**Step 1 — Multiplying factor:**

$$m_2 = \frac{V_2}{V_m} = \frac{50}{0.1} = 500$$

**Step 2 — Total resistance needed:**

$$R_{total,2} = m_2 \cdot R_m = 500 \times 100 = 50{,}000\,\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Individual multiplier section** (when $R_{s1}$ is already in place):

$$R_{s2} = R_{total,2} - R_{total,1} = 50{,}000 - 10{,}000 = 40{,}000\,\Omega$$

### Sensitivity

$$S_v = \frac{1}{I_{fsd}} = \frac{1}{10^{-3}} = 1000\,\frac{\Omega}{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification check:** $S_v \times V_1 = 1000 \times 10 = 10{,}000\,\Omega = R_{total,1}$ ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Basic Voltmeter with Single Multiplier Resistor

The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of the basic PMMC voltmeter with a series multiplier.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["UNKNOWN VOLTAGE V"] --> nodeB["SERIES MULTIPLIER RESISTOR Rs"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["PMMC METER COIL Rm"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["FULL SCALE DEFLECTION POINTER"]
    nodeE["Ifsd flows through Rs and Rm"] -.-> nodeB
    nodeF["Vm = Ifsd x Rm"] -.-> nodeC
    nodeG["Vs = Ifsd x Rs"] -.-> nodeB
    nodeH["V = Vm + Vs by KVL"] -.-> nodeA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The unknown voltage $V$ is applied across the *series combination* of the multiplier $R_s$ and the meter coil $R_m$. The dashed annotations show the key analytical relationships.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Multi-Range Voltmeter (Individual Series Multipliers)

This is the block architecture for a 3-range voltmeter using **individual true series multipliers**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeTerm["COMMON TERMINAL"] --> nodeSw["RANGE SELECTOR SWITCH S1"]
    nodeSw --> nodeR1["R_S1 for Range V1"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeR2["R_S2 for Range V2"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeR3["R_S3 for Range V3"]
    nodeR3 --> nodeMeter["PMMC METER Rm Ifsd"]
    nodeMeter --> nodeRet["RETURN TO COMMON"]
    nodeR1alt["V1 RANGE INPUT"] -.-> nodeR1
    nodeR2alt["V2 RANGE INPUT"] -.-> nodeR2
    nodeR3alt["V3 RANGE INPUT"] -.-> nodeR3
    nodeLbl["V1 < V2 < V3 Increasing Ranges"] -.-> nodeSw
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Ayrton Shunt / Universal Multiplier Network

This block diagram shows the **universal multiplier topology** with a single tapped resistor chain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn["INPUT V"] --> nodeA1["SECTION R1"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA2["SECTION R2"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3["SECTION R3"]
    nodeA3 --> nodeGnd["COMMON GROUND"]
    nodeTap["MOVABLE SELECTOR TAP"] -.-> nodeA1
    nodeTap -.-> nodeA2
    nodeTap -.-> nodeA3
    nodeMeter["PMMC METER Rm"] --- nodeTap
    nodeMeter --- nodeGnd
    nodeLbl["Single resistor chain, no open circuit risk"] -.-> nodeA1
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Process Flowchart for Solving Voltmeter Design Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["START: Given Rm and Ifsd"] --> nodeV1["Compute Vm = Ifsd x Rm"]
    nodeV1 --> nodeV2["Compute m = V_desired / Vm"]
    nodeV2 --> nodeV3["Compute Rs = Rm x m minus 1"]
    nodeV3 --> nodeV4["Compute R_total = Rm + Rs"]
    nodeV4 --> nodeV5["Compute Sensitivity Sv = 1 over Ifsd"]
    nodeV5 --> nodeV6["Compute Power P_Rs = Ifsd squared x Rs"]
    nodeV6 --> nodeEnd["END: Design Complete"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always present your final design in the form of a *circuit diagram* drawn in your answer script. A clearly labeled circuit (showing $V$ applied across the series chain) is worth 2–3 marks by itself.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is a multiplier resistor? Derive the expression for the multiplier resistance used to extend the range of a voltmeter.**

**Model Answer:**

A **multiplier resistor** ($R_s$) is a high-precision, high-stability resistor connected in *series* with a PMMC movement to extend its measurable voltage range.

**Derivation:** When voltage $V$ is applied across the series combination of $R_s$ and $R_m$, the same current $I_{fsd}$ flows through both (series circuit). The voltage across the meter is $V_m = I_{fsd} \cdot R_m$. By KVL:

$$V = I_{fsd} \cdot R_s + I_{fsd} \cdot R_m$$

Solving:

$$R_s = \frac{V - V_m}{I_{fsd}} = R_m \left(\frac{V}{V_m} - 1\right) = R_m(m - 1)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $R_s = R_m \cdot m$ instead of $R_s = R_m(m-1)$. The $(-1)$ term is critical — the meter resistance itself is part of the total; you only need the *excess*.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define voltmeter sensitivity. Why is high sensitivity desirable in a voltmeter?**

**Model Answer:**

**Voltmeter sensitivity** ($S_v$) is the total resistance of the voltmeter per volt of full-scale deflection:

$$S_v = \frac{1}{I_{fsd}} \quad \left[\frac{\Omega}{V}\right]$$

**Why high sensitivity is desirable:** A voltmeter is always connected in **parallel** with the circuit under test. If the voltmeter's resistance is comparable to the source resistance, it draws current and disturbs (loads) the circuit. A *high-sensitivity* voltmeter has high total resistance, draws negligible current, and thus causes **minimum loading error**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Confusing voltmeter sensitivity (Ω/V) with galvanometer sensitivity (mm/μA). They are different concepts; sensitivity of a *voltmeter* is always expressed in Ω/V.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question Choice 1(A) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

**Q: A basic PMMC movement has an internal resistance of $R_m = 50\,\Omega$ and a full-scale deflection current of $I_{fsd} = 5\,mA$.**
- **(a)** Design a multiplier resistor to extend the range of this voltmeter to **0–50 V**. Compute the total voltmeter resistance, sensitivity, and the power dissipated in the multiplier. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** Now design a **multi-range voltmeter** with ranges **0–10 V, 0–50 V, and 0–100 V** using individual series multipliers. Compute the value of each multiplier resistor. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1 — Compute the intrinsic meter voltage:**

$$V_m = I_{fsd} \cdot R_m = (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 50 = 0.25\,V$$

**Step 2 — Compute the multiplying factor for 0–50 V range:**

$$m = \frac{V}{V_m} = \frac{50}{0.25} = 200$$

**Step 3 — Compute the multiplier resistance:**

$$R_s = R_m(m - 1) = 50 \times (200 - 1) = 50 \times 199 = 9950\,\Omega \approx 9.95\,k\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Compute total voltmeter resistance:**

$$R_{total} = R_m + R_s = 50 + 9950 = 10{,}000\,\Omega = 10\,k\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Compute sensitivity:**

$$S_v = \frac{1}{I_{fsd}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 200\,\frac{\Omega}{V}$$

**Verification:** $S_v \times V = 200 \times 50 = 10{,}000\,\Omega$ ✓

**Step 6 — Compute power dissipated in the multiplier at full scale:**

$$P_{R_s} = I_{fsd}^2 \cdot R_s = (5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 9950 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 9950 = 0.24875\,W$$

A standard **0.5 W** or **1 W** resistor would be chosen as a safety margin.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

For a **true series** multi-range configuration, the multipliers are computed sequentially:

**Range 1: 0–10 V**

$$R_{total,1} = \frac{V_1}{I_{fsd}} = \frac{10}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 2000\,\Omega$$

$$R_{s1} = R_{total,1} - R_m = 2000 - 50 = 1950\,\Omega$$

**Range 2: 0–50 V**

$$R_{total,2} = \frac{V_2}{I_{fsd}} = \frac{50}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 10{,}000\,\Omega$$

$$R_{s2} = R_{total,2} - R_{total,1} = 10{,}000 - 2000 = 8000\,\Omega$$

**Range 3: 0–100 V**

$$R_{total,3} = \frac{V_3}{I_{fsd}} = \frac{100}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 20{,}000\,\Omega$$

$$R_{s3} = R_{total,3} - R_{total,2} = 20{,}000 - 10{,}000 = 10{,}000\,\Omega$$

**Tabulated Final Design:**

| Range | Total Resistance | Multiplier Resistor | Power in Multiplier |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0–10 V | $2\,k\Omega$ | $R_{s1} = 1.95\,k\Omega$ | $48.75\,mW$ |
| 0–50 V | $10\,k\Omega$ | $R_{s2} = 8\,k\Omega$ | $200\,mW$ |
| 0–100 V | $20\,k\Omega$ | $R_{s3} = 10\,k\Omega$ | $250\,mW$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (KTU Examiner Pattern):** A frequent mistake is to compute $R_{s2}$ and $R_{s3}$ as the *total* resistance for that range, rather than the *additional* section beyond the previous range. In the *individual series multiplier* configuration, each $R_{si}$ is the **incremental** resistance, NOT the total. Marks are deducted heavily for this.

---

### Question Choice 1(B) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]`

**Q: A basic D'Arsonval movement has a full-scale deflection current of $I_{fsd} = 1\,mA$ and an internal resistance of $R_m = 200\,\Omega$.**
- **(a)** Determine the value of the multiplier resistance required to extend the range to **0–100 V**. Also calculate the sensitivity of the voltmeter. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** Compare the **Ayrton shunt (universal multiplier)** configuration with the **individual series multiplier** configuration for a multi-range voltmeter. State the advantages of each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1 — Meter full-scale voltage:**

$$V_m = I_{fsd} \cdot R_m = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times 200 = 0.2\,V$$

**Step 2 — Multiplying factor:**

$$m = \frac{V}{V_m} = \frac{100}{0.2} = 500$$

**Step 3 — Multiplier resistance:**

$$R_s = R_m(m - 1) = 200 \times (500 - 1) = 200 \times 499 = 99{,}800\,\Omega \approx 99.8\,k\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Total voltmeter resistance:**

$$R_{total} = R_m + R_s = 200 + 99{,}800 = 100{,}000\,\Omega = 100\,k\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Sensitivity:**

$$S_v = \frac{1}{I_{fsd}} = \frac{1}{10^{-3}} = 1000\,\frac{\Omega}{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check:** $S_v \times V = 1000 \times 100 = 100{,}000\,\Omega = R_{total}$ ✓

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Individual Series Multiplier | Ayrton Shunt (Universal Multiplier) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of resistors | One per range | Single tapped resistor chain |
| Switch complexity | Multi-pole switch required | Single-pole selector tap |
| Open-circuit risk | Yes — meter can be left open during switching | **No** — meter is always connected |
| Range-to-range isolation | Complete (no interaction) | Tap positions are interdependent |
| Manufacturing tolerance impact | Each resistor is independent | Errors accumulate across the chain |
| Cost | Higher (more components) | Lower (single resistor) |
| Reliability | Less reliable (switch contacts critical) | **More reliable** |
| Accuracy | **Higher** | Slightly lower |
| Use case | Precision laboratory instruments | Commercial multimeters, rugged field use |

**Verdict:** The Ayrton shunt is preferred for **rugged, low-cost, multi-range instruments**; the individual series multiplier is preferred for **high-precision reference voltmeters**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** When answering comparison questions, students often give *vague* advantages like "Ayrton shunt is better." This loses marks. The examiner expects **specific, paired, contrasting points** with engineering justification (e.g., open-circuit protection during switching).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Bullet Form**

- [x] **Multiplier Resistor** is connected in **series** (not parallel) with the PMMC movement.
- [x] The fundamental formula is: $R_s = R_m(m-1)$, where $m = V/V_m$.
- [x] The **multiplying factor** $m$ is **dimensionless** and indicates the "fold" range extension.
- [x] **Sensitivity** $S_v = 1/I_{fsd}$ measured in $\Omega/V$ — higher is better.
- [x] **Higher sensitivity ⇒ less loading error** (because the voltmeter draws less current from the circuit under test).
- [x] Total voltmeter resistance = $m \times R_m$.
- [x] **Power rating** of the multiplier must be ≥ $I_{fsd}^2 \times R_s$ with a safety factor of 2–4×.
- [x] For **multi-range** voltmeters, prefer **individual series multipliers** for accuracy, **Ayrton shunt** for reliability and switching safety.
- [x] The **Ayrton shunt** is essentially a tapped voltage divider; the meter is never disconnected from the chain.
- [x] Common error: forgetting the **(-1)** in $R_s = R_m(m-1)$ — this is a 2-mark deduction every time.
- [x] Common error: confusing $R_{si}$ (incremental) with $R_{total,i}$ (absolute) in multi-range design.
- [x] For AC voltmeter extension, the multiplier must be non-inductive (use **Manganin** or **Constantan** wire wound non-inductively, or thick-film/cermet resistors) to avoid frequency-dependent errors.
- [x] **Loading error** in a DC voltmeter: $\varepsilon = \frac{R_{th}}{R_{th} + R_V}$, where $R_{th}$ is the Thevenin resistance of the source and $R_V$ is the voltmeter resistance. Minimized by high $R_V$ (i.e., high sensitivity).
- [x] **Temperature compensation** is built into the multiplier by using a resistor with a *negative temperature coefficient* in series or a thermistor shunt — a hot topic in KTU Module 1 follow-ups.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — Topic: Extending the Range of Voltmeter**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (NEP 2020) Outcome-Based Education Standards.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transistor Voltmeter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT412 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS MEASUREMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transistor Voltmeter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Transistor Voltmeter (TVM)** is an electronic voltmeter that employs one or more transistors in its signal-conditioning stages (typically as an attenuator, impedance-matching stage, or amplifier) to achieve **high input impedance, high sensitivity, low loading effect, and wide frequency range** while measuring DC, AC, or complex voltage waveforms. It overcomes the inherent limitations of conventional moving-coil voltmeters, such as low sensitivity (typically **20 kΩ/V**) and inability to measure high-frequency signals.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, a TVM is classified as an **active electronic voltmeter** because the transistor operates in its active region, drawing negligible power from the circuit under test while providing current or voltage amplification before the indicating meter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Transistor Voltmeters are categorized under *Electronic Instruments* and form the foundation for understanding *DC Amplifier-based Voltmeters, AC VTVMs, and Chopper-stabilized Voltmeters*.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you want to measure the **water pressure (voltage)** inside a very thin, sensitive pipe (high-impedance source) using a heavy mechanical pressure gauge. The moment you screw the gauge on, the pressure drops (loading effect) because the gauge draws water (current).

Now imagine replacing the heavy gauge with a **sensitive electronic sensor** that:
1. **Doesn't disturb the flow** (high input impedance),
2. **Magnifies the tiny reading internally** (transistor amplification),
3. **Displays the amplified value** on a small, clear screen.

That electronic sensor is exactly what a transistor does in a TVM. The transistor's **base-emitter junction** acts as a high-impedance probe, and the **collector-emitter current** acts as the amplified output driving the meter — without ever "draining" the source.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Input impedance of a TVM**: typically **1 MΩ to 10 MΩ** (compared to 20 kΩ/V for VOM)
- **Sensitivity**: typically **1 MΩ/V to 10 MΩ/V**
- **Frequency range**: **20 Hz to 5 MHz** (for AC TVMs)
- **Power consumption**: a few **milliwatts**
- **Transistor operating region**: Active region (V_CE > 0, V_BE ≈ **0.7 V** for Si, **0.3 V** for Ge)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction:** In a TVM, the transistor is **never used in saturation or cutoff** during normal measurement — it remains in the **linear active region** so that the output is a faithful, amplified replica of the input voltage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Logic — Why and How

A TVM functions in **three distinct blocks** that work in cascade:

**Block 1 — Attenuator / Probe Stage**
- Provides range selection and initial impedance matching.
- For high-voltage measurements, a **resistive voltage divider** is used.
- For low-voltage measurements, the divider is bypassed for direct coupling.
- *Why?* To protect the delicate transistor input stage from over-voltage damage.

**Block 2 — Transistor Amplifier Stage**
- The heart of the TVM. Configured as **Common Emitter (CE)** for voltage gain, or **Common Collector (CC) — Emitter Follower** for impedance matching.
- *Why CE?* Provides high voltage gain ($A_v$) — essential for measuring millivolt-level signals.
- *Why CC?* Provides high current gain and impedance transformation — essential for matching a high-impedance source to a low-impedance meter.

**Block 3 — Detector / Indicating Stage**
- For DC TVM: a moving-coil D'Arsonval meter directly reads the amplified collector current.
- For AC TVM: a **rectifier (diode)** converts the AC signal to a proportional DC value, which is then either amplified again or directly indicated.
- *Why rectify first?* A standard D'Arsonval movement cannot respond to AC at frequencies above a few Hz due to mechanical inertia.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Configuration | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Voltage gain of CE stage (approx.) | $A_v = h_{fe} \cdot R_C \big/ h_{ie}$ | Dimensionless, $h_{ie}$ in Ω, $R_C$ in Ω |
| 2 | Input impedance of CE stage (approx.) | $Z_{in} = h_{ie}$ | Ω — valid when $R_B \gg h_{ie}$ |
| 3 | Input impedance of Emitter Follower | $Z_{in} = h_{ie} + (h_{fe}+1)R_E$ | Ω — very high, suitable as probe |
| 4 | Voltage gain of Emitter Follower | $A_v = (h_{fe}+1)R_E \big/ \left[h_{ie} + (h_{fe}+1)R_E\right]$ | $\approx 1$ (slightly less than unity) |
| 5 | Current gain of Emitter Follower | $A_i = (h_{fe}+1)$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Overall TVM sensitivity (with rectifier) | $S_{TVM} = S_{galvanometer} \cdot A_v \cdot \eta_{rect}$ | Ω/V; $\eta_{rect}$ = rectifier efficiency |
| 7 | Meter current (CE stage) | $I_m = I_C = h_{fe} \cdot I_B$ | A — direct reading |
| 8 | Loading error | $\%\text{Error} = \left[R_S \big/ (R_S + R_{in})\right] \times 100$ | $R_S$ = source resistance, $R_{in}$ = TVM input impedance |
| 9 | Half-wave rectifier efficiency | $\eta = 0.406$ | For sinusoidal input |
| 10 | Full-wave rectifier efficiency | $\eta = 0.812$ | For sinusoidal input |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$h$-parameter reminder:** $h_{ie}$ is the input impedance with output shorted (typical value: **1 kΩ to 4 kΩ**), and $h_{fe}$ is the forward current gain (typical value: **50 to 300**). These are small-signal AC parameters evaluated at the **Q-point (quiescent operating point)**.

### Real-World Utility in Engineering
- **Laboratory bench meters** (Fluke 8000A series) — the original TVM architecture.
- **Analog oscilloscope input stages** — emitter follower buffers to drive the vertical amplifier.
- **Biomedical instrumentation** — ECG, EEG pre-amplifiers where loading a patient is unacceptable.
- **Audio industry** — high-fidelity VU meters and broadcast line monitors.
- **Industrial process control** — transducer signal conditioning (thermocouples, strain gauges).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Input Impedance of an Emitter-Follower TVM Stage

Consider a PNP transistor in common-collector (emitter follower) configuration with emitter resistor $R_E$ and the source $V_S$ feeding the base through source resistance $R_S$.

**Step 1 — Apply KVL to the Base–Emitter loop**

$$V_{in} = I_b \cdot h_{ie} + V_{be} + I_e \cdot R_E$$

**Step 2 — Relate emitter current to base current using the fundamental transistor current relation**

$$I_e = (h_{fe} + 1) \cdot I_b$$

This is the defining relationship: the emitter current is the sum of base and collector currents.

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression for $I_e$ into the KVL equation**

The voltage drop across the emitter resistor becomes $I_e \cdot R_E = (h_{fe}+1) I_b \cdot R_E$.

The voltage across the base–emitter junction, $V_{be}$, is implicitly absorbed into the $h_{ie}$ term during hybrid-model derivation (it is the input voltage that drives $I_b$ through $h_{ie}$).

$$V_{in} = I_b \cdot h_{ie} + (h_{fe}+1) I_b \cdot R_E$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $I_b$ to obtain a clean expression**

$$V_{in} = I_b \cdot \left[ h_{ie} + (h_{fe}+1) R_E \right]$$

**Step 5 — Apply the definition of input impedance**

By Ohm's law applied at the input port, $Z_{in} = V_{in} / I_b$.

$$Z_{in} = h_{ie} + (h_{fe}+1) R_E$$

**Step 6 — Approximation for high $h_{fe}$**

Since $h_{fe} \gg 1$ for most modern silicon transistors ($h_{fe} \geq 100$), the term $h_{ie}$ is negligible compared to $(h_{fe}+1)R_E$.

$$Z_{in} \approx (h_{fe}+1) R_E$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity check:** If $h_{fe} = 100$ and $R_E = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, then $Z_{in} \approx 101 \times 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 1.01 \text{ M}\Omega$ — confirming the high input impedance expected of a TVM probe.

---

### Derivation 2: Voltage Gain of a Common-Emitter TVM Stage

Consider an NPN transistor in CE configuration with collector load $R_C$ and unbypassed source resistance $R_S$ feeding the base.

**Step 1 — Write the input loop equation using the hybrid-π model**

$$V_{in} = I_b \cdot h_{ie} + (h_{fe}+1) I_b \cdot R_E \quad \text{(if } R_E \text{ is unbypassed; usually it is bypassed by } C_E\text{)}$$

For the standard bypassed case (most TVM designs):

$$V_{in} = I_b \cdot h_{ie}$$

**Step 2 — Write the output loop equation**

$$V_{out} = - I_c \cdot R_C = - h_{fe} I_b \cdot R_C$$

The negative sign reflects the **180° phase inversion** intrinsic to the CE configuration — a defining feature KTU examiners frequently test.

**Step 3 — Form the voltage gain ratio $A_v = V_{out} / V_{in}$**

$$A_v = \frac{-h_{fe} I_b R_C}{I_b h_{ie}} = -\frac{h_{fe} R_C}{h_{ie}}$$

**Step 4 — Include the source resistance for a more realistic TVM model**

If the source has internal resistance $R_S$, the effective input is reduced to $V_{in}' = V_S \cdot Z_{in} / (R_S + Z_{in})$. The overall gain becomes:

$$A_{v,\text{overall}} = -\frac{h_{fe} R_C}{h_{ie}} \cdot \frac{Z_{in}}{R_S + Z_{in}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for TVM design:** The factor $\frac{Z_{in}}{R_S + Z_{in}}$ represents the **voltage divider** formed by the source and the TVM. For the TVM to be accurate, we require $Z_{in} \gg R_S$, which is precisely the role of the emitter-follower buffer preceding the CE stage.

---

### Derivation 3: Overall Sensitivity of an AC TVM with Rectifier

For an AC voltmeter using a TVM front-end, the signal path is: **Source → Attenuator → AC Amplifier → Rectifier → DC Meter**.

**Step 1 — Establish the meter deflection equation for a moving-coil galvanometer**

$$I_m = k \cdot \theta$$

where $I_m$ is the meter current, $\theta$ is the deflection, and $k$ is the galvanometer constant.

**Step 2 — Compute the RMS meter current after rectification and amplification**

The AC amplifier delivers $V_{out,AC} = A_v \cdot V_{in,AC}$ to the rectifier. The rectifier (full-wave bridge) produces a DC equivalent:

$$V_{DC} = \eta \cdot V_{out,AC} = 0.812 \cdot A_v \cdot V_{in,AC}$$

The meter current becomes:

$$I_m = \frac{V_{DC}}{R_m} = \frac{0.812 \cdot A_v \cdot V_{in,AC}}{R_m}$$

**Step 3 — Define the sensitivity as $S = R_{in} / V_{FS}$ (Ω/V) where $V_{FS}$ produces full-scale deflection**

For a full-scale meter current $I_{FS}$ at input voltage $V_{FS}$:

$$S_{TVM} = \frac{R_{in}}{V_{FS}} = \frac{R_{in} \cdot R_m}{0.812 \cdot A_v}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key insight for KTU problems:** Increasing the amplifier gain $A_v$ *decreases* the value of $V_{FS}$ required for full-scale, thereby *increasing* the sensitivity $S_{TVM}$. This is why a 1 mV full-scale TVM requires a gain of several thousand — and why multi-stage amplifiers are common.

---

### Analytical Matrix — TVM Circuit Variants

| Configuration | Gain ($A_v$) | Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$) | Typical Use in TVM |
|---------------|--------------|----------------------------|--------------------|
| **Common Emitter (CE)** | High (50–500) | Moderate ($h_{ie}$ ≈ 1–4 kΩ) | Voltage amplification stage |
| **Common Collector (CC) / Emitter Follower** | $\approx 1$ | Very high (MΩ range) | Input buffer / probe |
| **Balanced Bridge (differential pair)** | Determined by $\Delta R / R$ | High (matched pair) | Drift-free DC TVM |
| **Cascode (CE + CB)** | Moderate, wideband | High | RF / high-frequency TVM |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram A — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Transistor Voltmeter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT TERMINALS] --> B[RANGE SELECTOR SWITCH]
    B --> C[ATTENUATOR NETWORK]
    C --> D[INPUT BUFFER EMITTER FOLLOWER]
    D --> E[VOLTAGE AMPLIFIER CE STAGE]
    E --> F{AC OR DC DETECTION}
    F --> G[RECTIFIER BRIDGE FOR AC]
    F --> H[DIRECT COUPLING FOR DC]
    G --> I[DC METER MOVEMENT]
    H --> I
    I --> J[CALIBRATED SCALE]
    I --> K[FEEDBACK NETWORK FOR LINEARITY]
    K --> E
    D -.-> K
```

### Diagram B — Signal-Flow Hierarchy and Power Domains

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SIGPATH[SIGNAL PATH]
        S1[STEP 1 PROBE HIGH Z INPUT] --> S2[STEP 2 ATTENUATE TO SAFE LEVEL]
        S2 --> S3[STEP 3 BUFFER IMPEDANCE MATCH]
        S3 --> S4[STEP 4 AMPLIFY TO USABLE LEVEL]
        S4 --> S5[STEP 5 RECTIFY OR DIRECT READ]
        S5 --> S6[STEP 6 DRIVE METER MOVEMENT]
    end
    subgraph POWSUP[POWER SUPPLY DOMAIN]
        P1[REGULATED DC SUPPLY PLUS MINUS 15V] --> S3
        P1 --> S4
    end
    subgraph CALIB[CALIBRATION LOOP]
        C1[FEEDBACK RESISTOR NETWORK] --> S4
        C2[ZERO ADJUST POTENTIOMETER] --> S3
    end
```

### Diagram C — Functional Roles of Each Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PROBE[PROBE STAGE]:::stage1 --> BUFF[BUFFER STAGE]:::stage2
    BUFF --> GAIN[GAIN STAGE]:::stage3
    GAIN --> DET[DETECTOR STAGE]:::stage4
    DET --> METER[INDICATING METER]:::stage5
    classDef stage1 fill:#fde0c5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef stage2 fill:#c5e0fd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef stage3 fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef stage4 fill:#fdd5f5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef stage5 fill:#fff5c5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### Diagram D — Decision Flow for Choosing TVM Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{WHAT IS THE INPUT SIGNAL TYPE} -->|DC ONLY| Q2{IS DRIFT CRITICAL}
    Q1 -->|AC OR MIXED| Q3{IS FREQUENCY ABOVE 1 MHZ}
    Q2 -->|YES| R1[USE BALANCED BRIDGE TVM WITH MATCHED PAIR]
    Q2 -->|NO| R2[USE SIMPLE CE STAGE WITH THERMAL COMPENSATION]
    Q3 -->|YES| R4[USE CASCODE OR FET INPUT TVM]
    Q3 -->|NO| R3[USE EMITTER FOLLOWER PLUS CE PLUS RECTIFIER]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The arrows indicate **signal flow direction**, not physical wiring. The **solid arrows** in Diagram B represent the main signal path; the **dashed arrows** represent feedback paths that improve linearity and temperature stability. The color-coded classes in Diagram C visually distinguish the *functional role* of each block.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper]
**Define a Transistor Voltmeter. List any four advantages it offers over a conventional moving-coil voltmeter.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Transistor Voltmeter (TVM) is an **electronic voltmeter that uses one or more transistors** in its measuring circuit to amplify the input signal before it is indicated on a moving-coil meter, thereby achieving high input impedance and high sensitivity.

**Four advantages over a conventional VOM:**
1. **High input impedance** (MΩ range) → minimizes loading error on the circuit under test.
2. **High sensitivity** (MΩ/V) → can measure millivolt-level signals.
3. **Wide frequency range** (20 Hz to several MHz) for AC measurements.
4. **Low power consumption** because the active transistor stage requires only a small bias current.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks for advantages.)*

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper]
**Why is an emitter-follower configuration often used as the input stage of a TVM?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An emitter follower is used as the input stage because:
1. It provides **very high input impedance**, given by $Z_{in} = h_{ie} + (h_{fe}+1)R_E$, which can be in the **MΩ range**.
2. This high $Z_{in}$ ensures **negligible loading effect** on the source, allowing accurate measurement of high-impedance circuits.
3. Although its voltage gain is approximately **unity**, it provides **high current gain** $(h_{fe}+1)$, making it an ideal **impedance transformer** between the source and the next amplifying stage.

*(Award 1 mark per point; award 1 additional mark for the correct $Z_{in}$ formula.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, adapted]
**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of an **emitter-follower Transistor Voltmeter (TVM) input stage** and explain its operation. Derive the expression for its **input impedance** and **voltage gain**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A TVM uses an emitter follower with $h_{fe} = 120$, $h_{ie} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $R_E = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$. The source has an internal resistance $R_S = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$ and an open-circuit voltage $V_S = 1.5 \text{ V}$. Calculate: (i) the input impedance seen by the source, (ii) the actual voltage reaching the base, and (iii) the output voltage across $R_E$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks)**

The emitter-follower TVM stage is a **common-collector** configuration. The input signal is applied between **base and ground**, and the output is taken across the **emitter resistor $R_E$** (which is common to both input and output loops — hence "common collector").

**Operation:**
- When $V_{in}$ increases, base current $I_b$ increases.
- This increases emitter current $I_e = (h_{fe}+1) I_b$.
- The voltage drop across $R_E$ increases, causing $V_{out}$ to rise in **phase** with $V_{in}$.
- The output "follows" the input, but offset by $V_{be} \approx 0.7 \text{ V}$.

**Derivation of $Z_{in}$** (already shown in Section 3):

$$Z_{in} = h_{ie} + (h_{fe}+1) R_E$$

**Derivation of $A_v$:**

$$V_{out} = I_e R_E = (h_{fe}+1) I_b R_E$$

$$V_{in} = I_b h_{ie} + (h_{fe}+1) I_b R_E$$

$$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{(h_{fe}+1) R_E}{h_{ie} + (h_{fe}+1) R_E} \approx 1 \text{ for } h_{fe} R_E \gg h_{ie}$$

*Mark split: 1 mark for diagram, 2 marks for explanation, 2 marks for $Z_{in}$ derivation, 2 marks for $A_v$ derivation.*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:** $h_{fe} = 120$, $h_{ie} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_E = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_S = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_S = 1.5 \text{ V}$.

**(i) Input impedance:**

$$Z_{in} = h_{ie} + (h_{fe}+1) R_E = 2 \text{ k}\Omega + (121)(5 \text{ k}\Omega) = 2 + 605 = 607 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**(ii) Actual voltage at the base (voltage divider rule):**

$$V_b = V_S \cdot \frac{Z_{in}}{R_S + Z_{in}} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{607 \text{ k}\Omega}{50 \text{ k}\Omega + 607 \text{ k}\Omega} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{607}{657}$$

$$V_b = 1.5 \times 0.9239 = 1.386 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Output voltage across $R_E$:**

$$V_{out} = A_v \cdot V_b = \left[\frac{605}{607}\right] \times 1.386 = 0.9967 \times 1.386 \approx 1.381 \text{ V}$$

*Mark split: 2 marks for (i), 3 marks for (ii) including voltage divider setup, 2 marks for (iii).*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common mistakes students make:**
> 1. **Forgetting the +1 in $(h_{fe}+1)$:** Many students write $h_{fe} R_E$ instead of $(h_{fe}+1)R_E$. While $h_{fe}=120$ makes this a small numerical error, it is a *conceptual* error that loses **1 full mark** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Ignoring the voltage divider effect of $R_S$:** Students often assume $V_b = V_S$ directly. This is only valid when $Z_{in} \to \infty$. With $R_S = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $Z_{in} = 607 \text{ k}\Omega$, the divider ratio is **0.924**, and ignoring it costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Sign errors in CE stage derivation:** Always retain the **negative sign** for CE voltage gain — it represents the 180° phase inversion. Losing this sign costs **1 mark** in the derivation step.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — May 2023, adapted]
**(a)** Explain the working of a **balanced-bridge transistor voltmeter** with a neat circuit diagram. State the **null-balance condition** and discuss how it achieves **drift-free measurement**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **CE amplifier stage** of a TVM has the following parameters: $h_{ie} = 2.5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $h_{fe} = 150$, $R_C = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, and the meter resistance $R_m = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$. Calculate: (i) the voltage gain, (ii) the meter current when the input is 10 mV RMS (assume the input is first amplified and then rectified using a full-wave bridge with $\eta = 0.812$), and (iii) the overall sensitivity in Ω/V. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks)**

A balanced-bridge TVM uses **two matched transistors** $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ arranged in a **Wheatstone-bridge configuration** at their collectors, with the meter movement as the bridge galvanometer.

- $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ share the same base-bias network so that at **zero input voltage**, both collector currents are equal ($I_{C1} = I_{C2}$), and the bridge is balanced → **zero meter deflection**.
- When an input $V_{in}$ is applied to $Q_1$'s base, $I_{C1}$ changes while $I_{C2}$ remains (approximately) constant.
- The bridge becomes unbalanced, and the meter reads a **deflection proportional to $V_{in}$**.

**Null-balance condition:**

$$I_{C1} \cdot R_{C1} = I_{C2} \cdot R_{C2}$$

when $R_{C1} = R_{C2}$ and the transistors are matched, this simplifies to $I_{C1} = I_{C2}$ at null.

**Drift-free operation:** Since both transistors experience the **same temperature changes**, any thermal drift in $I_{C1}$ is **mirrored** in $I_{C2}$, and the *difference* (which the meter measures) remains nearly constant. This **common-mode rejection** is the key advantage.

*Mark split: 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for working, 1 mark for null condition, 2 marks for drift analysis.*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:** $h_{ie} = 2.5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $h_{fe} = 150$, $R_C = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_m = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{in} = 10 \text{ mV (RMS)}$, $\eta = 0.812$.

**(i) Voltage gain of CE stage:**

$$A_v = \frac{h_{fe} R_C}{h_{ie}} = \frac{150 \times 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega}{2.5 \text{ k}\Omega} = \frac{705}{2.5} = 282$$

**(ii) Meter current:**

Step 1 — AC voltage at collector (RMS):

$$V_{C,RMS} = A_v \cdot V_{in} = 282 \times 10 \text{ mV} = 2.82 \text{ V (RMS)}$$

Step 2 — After full-wave rectification, the equivalent DC voltage (using rectifier efficiency $\eta = 0.812$):

$$V_{DC} = \eta \cdot V_{C,RMS} = 0.812 \times 2.82 = 2.29 \text{ V}$$

Step 3 — Meter current (assuming the DC voltage drives the meter directly):

$$I_m = \frac{V_{DC}}{R_m} = \frac{2.29 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ k}\Omega} = 2.29 \text{ mA}$$

**(iii) Overall sensitivity in Ω/V:**

The full-scale voltage is $V_{FS} = 10 \text{ mV} = 0.01 \text{ V}$ (we are using this as the maximum the meter is calibrated for). With $R_{in} \approx h_{ie} = 2.5 \text{ k}\Omega$ (neglecting $R_B$ for the CE stage):

$$S_{TVM} = \frac{R_{in}}{V_{FS}} = \frac{2.5 \text{ k}\Omega}{0.01 \text{ V}} = 250 \text{ k}\Omega/\text{V}$$

*Mark split: 2 marks for (i), 3 marks for (ii) with full rectification logic, 2 marks for (iii).*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (b):**
> 1. **Mistaking $\eta$ for peak factor:** Students often confuse rectifier efficiency with form factor or peak factor. Remember: $\eta = V_{DC}/V_{RMS}$ for full-wave = **0.812**, for half-wave = **0.406**.
> 2. **Sign in CE gain:** The gain magnitude is 282; do not introduce a negative sign unless the problem explicitly asks about phase.
> 3. **Units in sensitivity:** The answer **must** be in Ω/V. A common error is to write "250 kΩ" instead of "250 kΩ/V", costing the unit mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **TVM = Active electronic voltmeter** using a transistor in the active region to provide gain or impedance matching.
- **Three main blocks:** Attenuator → Amplifier → Detector/Meter.
- **Two dominant topologies:** Emitter Follower (input) + CE Stage (gain) + Rectifier (for AC).
- **Input impedance of emitter follower:** $Z_{in} = h_{ie} + (h_{fe}+1)R_E \approx (h_{fe}+1)R_E$ — this is the key to high-impedance probing.
- **Voltage gain of CE stage:** $A_v = -h_{fe} R_C / h_{ie}$ — note the **negative sign** = 180° phase inversion.
- **Voltage gain of emitter follower:** $A_v \approx 1$ (slightly less) — used as buffer, not for gain.
- **Rectifier efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{half-wave}} = 0.406$; $\eta_{\text{full-wave}} = 0.812$.
- **Balanced-bridge TVM** uses two matched transistors to cancel thermal drift via common-mode rejection.
- **Sensitivity** $S = R_{in}/V_{FS}$ in **Ω/V** — high sensitivity arises from high $R_{in}$ and low $V_{FS}$.
- **Loading error** $\% = R_S/(R_S + R_{in}) \times 100$ — minimized by maximizing $R_{in}$.
- **Typical TVM specs to memorize:** $R_{in} = 1\text{–}10 \text{ M}\Omega$, $S = 1\text{–}10 \text{ M}\Omega/\text{V}$, bandwidth $20 \text{ Hz to } 5 \text{ MHz}$.
- **For KTU numerical problems:** Always state assumptions (e.g., "assuming unbypassed $R_E$ is bypassed by $C_E$", "neglecting $R_B$ effect").
- **Examiner's mantra:** Show all formula substitutions with units; never skip the voltage-divider step; always verify final answers with dimension checks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chopper Type DC Voltmeter, True RMS Voltmeter, Wattmeter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT412 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS MEASUREMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Chopper Type DC Voltmeter, True RMS Voltmeter, Wattmeter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Chopper Type DC Voltmeter, True RMS Voltmeter & Wattmeter

## 1.1 Chopper Type DC Voltmeter

### 1.1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chopper Type DC Voltmeter (Chopper-Stabilized Voltmeter):** An electronic voltmeter that converts a low-value or slowly varying DC input signal into an AC signal using a **chopper**, amplifies the AC signal using a stable AC amplifier, and then rectifies and filters it back to DC. The output DC is proportional to the original input DC. This technique combines the **drift-free nature of DC measurement** with the **high gain and stability of AC amplification**.

The block diagram of a chopper-stabilized amplifier (and hence the chopper voltmeter) consists of:
**Chopper → AC Amplifier → Rectifier/Phase-Sensitive Detector → Smoothing Filter → Output DC Meter**

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Bucket-Brigade":**
> Imagine you want to measure the level of water in a still pond (a slowly varying DC signal). The water is so still that your ruler cannot read a tiny change. So, a friend with a bucket keeps **dipping the bucket in and pouring it into a measuring cup** repeatedly (chopping = converting DC to pulses). Now, the cup shows clear, easy-to-measure amounts. You then estimate the pond's level from the cup readings.
>
> - The **bucket** is the **chopper** (chopping the DC into pulses).
> - The **measuring cup** is the **AC amplifier** (easy to amplify pulses).
> - The **poured-back water** is the **rectified output** feeding the meter.
>
> This is exactly what a chopper voltmeter does: it converts DC to AC (chopping), amplifies the AC precisely, and converts it back to DC for display. Drift in DC amplifiers is the enemy; AC amplifiers have **near-zero drift**.

### 1.1.3 Standard Metrics

- **Chopping Frequency** = typically **50 Hz to 400 Hz** (often line-synchronized).
- **Input Range** = **µV to mV** (microvolts to millivolts) for chopper amplifiers.
- **Chopper (Modulator)** = mechanical reed-chopper, photo-chopper, or FET chopper.
- **Output Deflection** = proportional to **mean (DC) value of input**, since the input is DC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Chopper type voltmeters are introduced in KTU Module-1 specifically to overcome the **drift problem** of direct-coupled (DC) amplifiers while retaining the DC measurement capability.

---

## 1.2 True RMS Voltmeter

### 1.2.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **True RMS (Root-Mean-Square) Voltmeter:** An electronic voltmeter whose deflection is proportional to the **heating effect (effective power)** of the input waveform — i.e., to its **true root-mean-square (RMS) value**, *independent of the waveform shape*. Unlike average-responding or peak-responding voltmeters, a true RMS voltmeter provides correct readings for sine, square, triangular, distorted, noise-laden, and pulsed waveforms.

The mathematical definition of true RMS of a periodic signal $v(t)$ is:

$$V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\, dt}$$

where $T$ is the period. **A true RMS voltmeter computes (or thermally obtains) this integral faithfully**, then displays $V_{\text{rms}}$.

### 1.2.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Heating Value of a River":**
> Imagine two rivers flowing at your house. The first is a calm river (DC). The second pulses violently, sometimes drying up, sometimes flooding (AC waveform). A hydro-generator on each river produces useful energy that depends on **the actual water-power over time**, not on peak or average. The True-RMS voltmeter is exactly this **"energy meter"** for electrical signals — it measures the *actual heating power* delivered to a resistor.
>
> - A **peak-responding** meter would overestimate the AC river (looking at flood peaks).
> - An **average-responding** meter would underestimate the AC river (ignoring flood surges).
> - A **true RMS** meter would give the *correct* equivalent DC power — just as a **calorimeter** measures real heat regardless of waveform.

### 1.2.3 Standard Metrics

- **Crest Factor** = $\frac{V_{\text{peak}}}{V_{\text{rms}}}$ — true RMS meters must handle crest factors up to **3 to 5** (typical) or higher.
- **Sine-Wave Form Factor** = **1.1107** (ratio of RMS to average for pure sine).
- **Two Common Methods** = (a) **Thermocouple/Thermal Converter method** (b) **Electronic Squaring-and-Averaging method** (using log-antilog, or analog multipliers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module-1, the focus is on the **electronic / thermal method of achieving true RMS conversion** and the concept of form factor / crest factor as applied to non-sinusoidal waveforms.

---

## 1.3 Wattmeter (Electrodynamometer Type)

### 1.3.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wattmeter:** An instrument designed to measure **average active power** ($P$) flowing in an electrical circuit. The **Electrodynamometer Wattmeter** is the standard AC/DC transfer instrument for power. It has two coils:
> - **Current Coil (CC)** — fixed (stationary), connected **in series** with the load.
> - **Potential Coil (PC) / Voltage Coil** — moving, connected **in parallel** with the load, usually with a high-value multiplier resistor.
>
> The instantaneous torque is $T_i \propto i_1(t) \cdot i_2(t)$, and the pointer deflection is proportional to the **average product** of the two currents, which equals the **average power**.

For sinusoidal AC, the wattmeter reading is:

$$P = V_{\text{rms}} I_{\text{rms}} \cos\phi$$

where $\phi$ is the phase angle between the voltage coil current and the load current.

### 1.3.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Two Friends Multiplying on a Seesaw":**
> Imagine two friends sitting on opposite ends of a seesaw.
> - **Friend A** represents **current** $i_1(t)$ — push him harder when current is high.
> - **Friend B** represents **voltage** $i_2(t)$ — push him harder when voltage is high.
> - The **average tilt** of the seesaw over time is the **product of their pushes averaged out — that is POWER**.
>
> When current and voltage are in phase (purely resistive load), the seesaw tilts one way persistently (real power consumed). When they are 90° out of phase (purely reactive load), the seesaw goes back and forth perfectly (zero average power — no real consumption). The electrodynamometer wattmeter *physically realizes this multiplication* using electromagnetic forces.

### 1.3.3 Standard Metrics

- **Range** = milliwatts to megawatts (with multipliers/CTs/PTs).
- **Frequency Range** = typically **DC to 400 Hz** (or up to 2.5 kHz for special designs).
- **Accuracy Class** = **0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0** (per IS-1248 / IEC 60051).
- **Deflecting Torque** = $T_d \propto M(\theta) \cdot I_1 I_2 \cos\phi$, where $M(\theta)$ is the mutual inductance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU syllabus emphasizes (i) the **multiplier-based wattmeter for range extension**, (ii) **compensating coil for power loss in potential circuit**, and (iii) **errors in electrodynamometer wattmeters** (pressure coil inductance, capacitance, stray fields, temperature).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chopper Type DC Voltmeter — Operational Logic

**Step-by-step operating principle:**

1. **Chopper (Modulator)** — A chopper is a switch (electromechanical, photo-electric, or FET) that opens and closes at a fixed **chopping frequency** $f_c$ (say 50–400 Hz). It samples the input DC voltage $V_{\text{in}}$ and produces a **square-wave-like AC signal** whose amplitude is proportional to $V_{\text{in}}$.
2. **AC Amplifier** — The chopped AC signal is amplified by a **drift-free AC amplifier** (typically with $R-C$ or transformer coupling). AC amplifiers have **no 1/f noise or thermal drift** problems that plague DC amplifiers.
