## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bulleted Summary — Read This 5 Minutes Before the Exam:**

### ⚡ DC Motor: Core Concepts
- ⭐ DC motor works on **Lorentz Force Principle**: $F = BIL$.
- ⭐ Direction of rotation is determined by **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**.
- ⭐ **Back EMF** ($E_b$) opposes applied voltage — formula: $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$.
- ⭐ **Armature Torque** formula: $T_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \Phi Z I_a \cdot \dfrac{P}{A}$.
- ⭐ **Voltage Equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (motor convention).
- ⭐ **Power Equation:** $V I_a = E_b I_a + I_a^2 R_a$ — Electrical Input = Mechanical Power + Armature Copper Loss.
- ⭐ **Mechanical Power Developed:** $P_m = E_b I_a = T_a \omega$ where $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$.

### ⚡ Commutation: Core Concepts
- ⭐ **Commutation** = reversal of current in an armature coil as it crosses the **MNA**.
- ⭐ Achieved mechanically by the **commutator-brush assembly**.
- ⭐ **Time of Commutation:** $T_c = \dfrac{30}{CN}$ (very short — 0.5 to 2 ms).
- ⭐ **Reactance Voltage:** $E_r = L \cdot \dfrac{dI}{dt}$ — opposes current reversal.
- ⭐ **Ideal Commutation:** Linear current reversal → no sparking.
- ⭐ **Delayed Commutation:** Caused by self-inductance → sparking at trailing brush tip.

### ⚡ Methods to Improve Commutation (KTU Frequently Asked)
- ⭐ **Method 1 — Resistance Commutation:** High-resistance **carbon brushes** redirect current.
- ⭐ **Method 2 — EMF Commutation (Interpoles):** Most widely used. **Polarity of interpole = Polarity of NEXT main pole in direction of rotation.** Winding in series with armature.
- ⭐ **Method 3 — Compensating Windings:** Embedded in pole faces; neutralize armature reaction.

### ⚡ Winding Type Quick Reference
- ⭐ **Lap Winding:** $A = P$ — Used in high-current machines.
- ⭐ **Wave Winding:** $A = 2$ — Used in high-voltage machines.
- ⭐ **Number of Commutator Segments** = Number of coils = $\dfrac{Z}{2}$.

### ⚡ KTU Numerical Solving Strategy
- ⭐ Always **state assumptions** clearly (lap vs wave, $A$ value).
- ⭐ Always **check dimensional consistency** ($E_b < V$ for a motor).
- ⭐ Use $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ for power calculations.
- ⭐ Box all final answers with units.
- ⭐ For commutation problems, always mention **interpole polarity rule** for full marks.

### ⚡ Common Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing **interpole polarity** rule (most common error).
- ❌ Forgetting that back EMF **opposes** applied voltage (Lenz's Law).
- ❌ Using $A = 2$ for lap wound machines.
- ❌ Mixing up $E_b = V + I_a R_a$ (generator) with $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ (motor).
- ❌ Skipping the **commutation period** definition in commutation questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Back EMF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT412 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS MEASUREMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Back EMF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Back EMF (Counter EMF / Counter Electromotive Force)** in a DC motor is the electromagnetic force (EMF) induced in the rotating armature conductors due to the motion of these conductors through the main magnetic field. By **Lenz's Law**, this induced EMF always opposes the cause that produces it, i.e., it opposes the applied supply voltage. It is denoted by the symbol **$E_b$** (or **$E_{back}$**) and is measured in **Volts (V)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Back EMF is *not* a loss; it is a natural consequence of electromechanical energy conversion. It is the mechanism that allows a motor to "self-regulate" its speed and current draw.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **rowing a boat upstream** against a fast-flowing river:

- The force of the river pushing you backward = **Back EMF ($E_b$)**
- Your effort (paddling force) = **Supply Voltage ($V$)**
- The energy that actually moves the boat forward (net useful work) = **Net Voltage ($V - E_b$)**

If the river suddenly slows down (load on motor reduces), less backward force acts on your boat — you accelerate! Similarly, if the load on the motor decreases, the back EMF reduces, net voltage rises, and the motor speeds up. This elegant feedback mechanism is the heart of DC motor operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A DC motor running on no-load has maximum back EMF (almost equal to $V$). A DC motor under heavy load has reduced back EMF, allowing more armature current to flow to produce the required torque.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Back EMF | $E_b$ | Volts (V) |
| Flux per pole | $\Phi$ | Weber (Wb) |
| Armature speed | $N$ | RPM (Revolutions per Minute) |
| Number of poles | $P$ | — (dimensionless) |
| Number of parallel paths | $A$ | — (dimensionless) |
| Specific machine constant | $P/(60A)$ | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physical Origin of Back EMF — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Supply Voltage $V$** is applied across the armature terminals → current $I_a$ flows through the armature winding.
2. The armature winding is placed in a **stationary magnetic field** produced by the field poles (flux $\Phi$ per pole).
3. The current-carrying conductors experience a **Lorentz Force ($F = BIL$)** → producing torque → armature begins to **rotate**.
4. Once rotating, these same conductors now **cut the magnetic flux lines** → by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, an EMF is induced in them.
5. By **Lenz's Law**, this induced EMF must **oppose the supply voltage** (the very cause of rotation) → hence the name **"Back" EMF**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60\,A}$ | Fundamental back EMF equation | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $E_b = k\,\Phi\,N$ | Simplified: $k = \dfrac{PZ}{60A}$ (machine constant) | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Voltage equation of DC motor | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $I_a = \dfrac{V - E_b}{R_a}$ | Armature current (Armature reaction neglected) | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | Rearranged: Computing $E_b$ from measurements | Volts (V) |
| 6 | $P_m = E_b I_a$ | Mechanical power developed by armature | Watts (W) |
| 7 | $P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Armature copper loss | Watts (W) |
| 8 | $P_{in} = V I_a$ | Electrical input power to armature | Watts (W) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **Wave-wound** armature: $A = 2$. For a **Lap-wound** armature: $A = P$. This distinction is **frequently tested** in KTU university exams.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Speed Regulation:** Since $N \propto (V - I_aR_a)/\Phi$, back EMF dictates the speed-torque characteristic. This is exploited in **DC servo motors, electric vehicle traction, and industrial drives**.
- **Self-Protection Mechanism:** During start-up, $N = 0 \Rightarrow E_b = 0$, so a dangerously high inrush current $I_a = V/R_a$ would flow. **Starters** (3-point, 4-point) limit this current by inserting external resistance — a direct application of back EMF analysis.
- **Generator Mode:** In a DC *generator*, the induced EMF is the *source* EMF, and the same physics applies in reverse. The duality makes the back EMF concept fundamental to all rotating machines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Back EMF Equation ($E_b = P\Phi NZ / 60A$)

We start from **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**: the EMF induced in a single conductor of length $l$ moving with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a magnetic field of flux density $B$ is:

$$e = B \cdot l \cdot v$$

**Step 1: Express the velocity of armature conductors.**

The armature rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute.

- Revolutions per second = $N/60$
- Distance traveled by a conductor on the armature periphery in one revolution = $\pi D$, where $D$ is the armature diameter.
- Therefore, linear velocity $v = \pi D \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$.

**Step 2: Express the magnetic flux density $B$.**

The total flux per pole is $\Phi$ Wb, distributed over the pole arc area $A_{pole}$. The average flux density under the pole face is $B = \Phi / A_{pole}$. For derivation purposes, we use the equivalent relation that the **average flux cut per pole per revolution** is $\Phi$.

**Step 3: EMF generated per conductor per revolution.**

In one revolution, each conductor cuts flux equal to $P \times \Phi$ (since the conductor passes under $P$ poles).

**Step 4: EMF per conductor per revolution (in Volts).**

Flux cut per second by one conductor = $P\Phi \times \dfrac{N}{60}$.

By Faraday's law, EMF per conductor:

$$e_{\text{conductor}} = \dfrac{P\Phi N}{60} \text{ Volts}$$

**Step 5: Multiply by the total number of conductors $Z$.**

The total induced EMF across the armature (before considering parallel paths) is:

$$E_{\text{total}} = Z \cdot \dfrac{P\Phi N}{60}$$

**Step 6: Divide by the number of parallel paths $A$.**

In a DC machine, the $Z$ conductors are distributed among $A$ parallel paths. The conductors in each path are in **series**, so the EMF per path is the terminal EMF. Since there are $A$ such paths in parallel, the net terminal EMF is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b &= \dfrac{Z}{A} \cdot \dfrac{P\Phi N}{60} \\
E_b &= \dfrac{P\,\Phi\,N\,Z}{60\,A} \text{ Volts}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of Units:** $\dfrac{\text{poles} \times \text{Wb} \times \text{rev/min} \times \text{conductors}}{\text{paths} \times \text{seconds/min}}$ = $\dfrac{\text{Wb} \cdot \text{conductors}}{\text{paths} \cdot \text{seconds}}$ = Volts. ✔ Dimensionally consistent.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Voltage Equation of a DC Motor

Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** to the armature circuit:

- The applied voltage $V$ drives the current $I_a$ through the armature.
- It must overcome (i) the ohmic drop $I_a R_a$ across the armature resistance.
- It must also overcome (ii) the back EMF $E_b$ (which acts as a load/source in the armature loop, opposing $V$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= E_b + I_a R_a \\
\Rightarrow I_a &= \dfrac{V - E_b}{R_a}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Power Flow Derivation

Multiplying the voltage equation by $I_a$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V I_a &= E_b I_a + I_a^2 R_a \\
P_{\text{input}} &= P_{\text{mechanical developed}} + P_{\text{copper loss}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The term $E_b I_a$ represents the **gross mechanical power developed** in the armature, which is then partially lost as friction, windage, and core losses, and the remainder appears as **shaft output power**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Back EMF Feedback Loop in a DC Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Supply Voltage V] --> B[Armature Terminals]
    B --> C[Armature Winding Ra]
    C --> D[Armature Current Ia]
    D --> E[Force on Conductors F=BIL]
    E --> F[Mechanical Torque T]
    F --> G[Armature Rotation N]
    G --> H[Conductor cuts main flux Phi]
    H --> I[Induced Back EMF Eb]
    I --> J[Opposes Applied Voltage V]
    J --> K[Net Driving Voltage V minus Eb]
    K --> D
    F --> L[Shaft Load Torque TL]
    L --> M[Steady State Equilibrium]
    M --> G
```

### 4.2 Voltage Distribution Across Armature (Equivalent Circuit)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A((+ V)) -->|V| B[Armature Resistance Ra]
    B -->|IaRa Drop| C((Node B))
    C -->|Eb Opposing| D((- Back EMF Source))
    D -->|Return Path| E((0 V Reference))
    A --> E
```

### 4.3 No-Load vs Loaded Condition (Operational State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Starting: N equals 0
    Starting --> Acceleration: Eb small, Ia large
    Acceleration --> NoLoad: Torque balanced
    NoLoad --> Loaded: External load applied
    Loaded --> Deceleration: Back EMF drops
    Deceleration --> NewSteadyState: Ia rises, torque rises
    NewSteadyState --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Physical Layout of a DC Motor Armature (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MagneticField["Stationary Magnetic Field - Flux Phi per pole"]
        N1[N Pole]
        S1[S Pole]
        N2[N Pole]
        S2[S Pole]
    end
    subgraph RotatingArmature["Armature Conductors - Rotating at N RPM"]
        C1[Conductor 1]
        C2[Conductor 2]
        C3[Conductor Z]
    end
    N1 --> C1
    C1 --> S1
    S1 --> C2
    C2 --> N2
    N2 --> C3
    C3 --> S2
    C3 -.Induces Eb.-> EbNode[Back EMF across brushes]
    C1 -.Induces Eb.-> EbNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> The induced EMF in each conductor is summed across the series-connected conductors of one parallel path. There are $A$ such paths in parallel, giving the terminal voltage $E_b$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define back EMF in a DC motor. Why is it called a "back" EMF?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 Marks):** Back EMF is the EMF induced in the rotating armature conductors of a DC motor due to their motion through the main magnetic field. It is given by $E_b = \dfrac{P\Phi NZ}{60A}$.
- **"Back" Justification (1 Mark):** By Lenz's Law, this induced EMF opposes the applied supply voltage, hence it is called "back" (or counter) EMF.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional]**
*A 4-pole, wave-wound DC shunt motor has 600 conductors and runs at 1000 RPM. The flux per pole is 0.02 Wb. Calculate the back EMF.*

**Model Solution (3 Marks):**
Given: $P = 4$, $A = 2$ (wave), $Z = 600$, $N = 1000$ RPM, $\Phi = 0.02$ Wb.

$$
E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60\,A} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.02 \times 1000 \times 600}{60 \times 2}
$$

$$
E_b = \dfrac{48\,000}{120} = 400 \text{ V}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that for a **wave-wound** machine, $A = 2$ *regardless* of the number of poles. Marking penalty of **1 mark** is typical for this error.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**
*Derive the expression for the back EMF in a DC machine. A 6-pole, lap-wound DC motor has 720 conductors. The armature resistance is $0.1\,\Omega$ and it draws 80 A from a 400 V supply. If the flux per pole is 0.05 Wb, find: (i) the back EMF, and (ii) the speed of the motor in RPM.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (carried for context, 7 marks available for the problem):**
The back EMF in a DC machine is given by:

$$E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60\,A}$$

**Given Data:**
- $P = 6$ (poles)
- Lap-wound $\Rightarrow A = P = 6$
- $Z = 720$ (conductors)
- $R_a = 0.1\,\Omega$
- $I_a = 80$ A
- $V = 400$ V
- $\Phi = 0.05$ Wb

**(i) Back EMF Calculation (3 Marks):**

$$
E_b = V - I_a R_a = 400 - (80 \times 0.1) = 400 - 8 = 392 \text{ V}
$$

**(ii) Speed Calculation (4 Marks):**

Rearranging the back EMF equation for $N$:

$$
N = \dfrac{E_b \times 60 \times A}{P \times \Phi \times Z}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{392 \times 60 \times 6}{6 \times 0.05 \times 720}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{141\,120}{216} = 653.33 \text{ RPM}
$$

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the role of back EMF in determining the speed and armature current of a DC motor under varying load conditions. Derive the relationship $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ and discuss what happens at the instant of starting.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):** Refer to the functional architecture in **Section 4.1** of these notes. Key blocks: Supply → Armature Resistance → Current → Torque → Rotation → Flux Cutting → Back EMF → Opposition.

**Derivation of Voltage Equation (2 Marks):** By applying KVL to the armature circuit, where the back EMF $E_b$ acts in opposition to the applied voltage $V$ and the ohmic drop is $I_a R_a$:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Role in Speed and Current (2 Marks):**
- $I_a = (V - E_b)/R_a$ — as load increases, the motor slows, $E_b$ drops, and $I_a$ rises automatically to produce more torque, maintaining equilibrium.
- $N \propto (V - I_aR_a)/\Phi$ — speed is directly governed by the net effective voltage.

**Starting Instant (1 Mark):** At $t = 0$, $N = 0 \Rightarrow E_b = 0$. Therefore $I_{a,\text{start}} = V/R_a$, which is **dangerously high** (typically 10–20 times the rated current). Hence starters are mandatory.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks, Internal Choice)

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternative Set]**
*A 4-pole, wave-wound DC series motor has 800 armature conductors. When connected to a 250 V supply, it takes 40 A and runs at 1200 RPM. The armature resistance is $0.5\,\Omega$ and the field winding resistance is $0.3\,\Omega$. Calculate: (i) the back EMF, (ii) the flux per pole, and (iii) the mechanical power developed.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $P = 4$, $A = 2$ (wave), $Z = 800$, $V = 250$ V, $I_a = 40$ A, $N = 1200$ RPM
- $R_a = 0.5\,\Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.3\,\Omega$

**(i) Back EMF (2 Marks):**

For a series motor, total voltage drop is across $R_a + R_{se}$:

$$
E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se}) = 250 - 40 \times (0.5 + 0.3) = 250 - 32 = 218 \text{ V}
$$

**(ii) Flux per Pole (3 Marks):**

$$
\Phi = \dfrac{E_b \times 60 \times A}{P \times N \times Z} = \dfrac{218 \times 60 \times 2}{4 \times 1200 \times 800}
$$

$$
\Phi = \dfrac{26\,160}{3\,840\,000} = 6.8125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb} = 6.81 \text{ mWb}
$$

**(iii) Mechanical Power Developed (2 Marks):**

$$
P_m = E_b \times I_a = 218 \times 40 = 8720 \text{ W} = 8.72 \text{ kW}
$$

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**
*Explain the significance of back EMF in a DC motor. A DC motor has an armature resistance of $0.2\,\Omega$ and is supplied with 220 V. On full load, the armature current is 50 A. If the flux is suddenly reduced to 80% of its original value, calculate the new armature current and the new speed, assuming torque remains constant.* **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Significance of Back EMF (2 Marks):**
Back EMF is the regulating parameter of a DC motor. It provides automatic current limiting, enables self-adjustment of speed under load, and is the direct measure of the electromechanical energy conversion process.

**Initial Condition Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$
E_{b1} = V - I_{a1} R_a = 220 - (50 \times 0.2) = 220 - 10 = 210 \text{ V}
$$

Initial speed $N_1$ satisfies $E_{b1} = k \Phi_1 N_1$.

**After Flux Reduction (3 Marks):**

Let $\Phi_2 = 0.8 \Phi_1$. Torque $T \propto \Phi I_a = $ constant $\Rightarrow I_{a2} \Phi_2 = I_{a1} \Phi_1$:

$$
I_{a2} = \dfrac{I_{a1} \Phi_1}{0.8 \Phi_1} = \dfrac{50}{0.8} = 62.5 \text{ A}
$$

New back EMF:

$$
E_{b2} = V - I_{a2} R_a = 220 - (62.5 \times 0.2) = 220 - 12.5 = 207.5 \text{ V}
$$

Speed ratio:

$$
\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{E_{b2}/\Phi_2}{E_{b1}/\Phi_1} = \dfrac{207.5/0.8}{210/1} = \dfrac{259.375}{210} = 1.235
$$

$$
N_2 = 1.235 \times N_1
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting $R_{se}$ in series motors** — In a series motor, the total resistance drop is across *both* $R_a$ and $R_{se}$. Common deduction: **1 mark**.
> 2. **Using $A = P$ for wave winding** — Wave winding *always* has $A = 2$. Common deduction: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Missing units in final answer** — KTU examiners frequently deduct **0.5 mark** for omitting "V", "RPM", or "Wb".
> 4. **Confusing $E_b$ with $V$** — Never treat the supply voltage as the induced EMF. They are equal *only* at no-load with negligible drops.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Back EMF** $E_b = \dfrac{P\Phi NZ}{60A}$ is induced in the *rotating* armature; it **opposes** the supply voltage by Lenz's Law.
- ✅ **Voltage equation:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ — the *fundamental governing equation* of a DC motor.
- ✅ **Wave winding:** $A = 2$ (always). **Lap winding:** $A = P$.
- ✅ At **start-up**, $N = 0 \Rightarrow E_b = 0 \Rightarrow I_a = V/R_a$ (very high) — this is why **starters are mandatory**.
- ✅ **No-load:** $E_b$ is maximum (close to $V$); current is small. **Full load:** $E_b$ drops slightly, current rises to produce higher torque.
- ✅ **Mechanical power developed:** $P_m = E_b I_a$. **Shaft output:** $P_m$ minus mechanical (friction, windage) and core losses.
- ✅ **Machine constant** $k = PZ/(60A)$ is a fixed design parameter; $E_b = k\Phi N$.
- ✅ **Speed formula:** $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{k\Phi}$ — increase $V$ or decrease $\Phi$ to increase speed.
- ✅ Back EMF is the **physical link** between the electrical input and mechanical output — without it, energy conversion would be impossible.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEIT412 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS MEASUREMENTS & MACHINES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Speed Equation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Speed Equation** of a DC machine is the fundamental mathematical relationship that expresses the **rotational speed (N)** of the armature in revolutions per minute (rpm) as a function of the **supply voltage (V)**, **armature circuit drop (I_aR_a)**, **flux per pole (Φ)**, and the **machine constructional constants** (number of poles P, number of conductors Z, and number of parallel paths A). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – DC Machines):** > The Speed Equation is derived by combining the **Back EMF Equation** (E_b) with the **Voltage Equation** of the armature circuit. It is the foundational relation used to study **speed control methods** (armature voltage control and flux/field control) — a guaranteed 14-mark question in KTU University Examinations. Formally, for a **DC Motor**: $$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{k \Phi}$$ where the machine constant is given by: $$k = \frac{P \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tap Water & Bucket" Intuition Imagine you are filling a bucket with water: - **Water pressure from the tap (V)** → This is the **supply voltage** pushing current into the motor. - **Friction in the pipe (I_a R_a)** → This is the **voltage drop** lost as heat in the armature resistance; it steals useful pressure. - **Weight of water already in the bucket (Back EMF E_b)** → This is the **opposition** the rotating armature generates; it grows as the bucket (motor) spins faster. - **Size of the bucket opening (Φ — flux)** → A **larger magnetic flux** is like a narrower opening; it restricts flow and limits the maximum speed. - **How fast you fill the bucket (N)** → This is the **motor speed**. > When you increase the tap pressure (V), the bucket fills faster (motor speeds up). > When you partially close the opening (weaken Φ by reducing field current), the bucket fills faster too (motor speeds up). > When the pipe is clogged (R_a increases), the bucket fills slowly (motor slows down). This is exactly how the **Speed Equation** governs real DC motors. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants Used:** > - $\Phi$ — Flux per pole, measured in **Weber (Wb)** > - $N$ — Speed, measured in **revolutions per minute (rpm)** > - $E_b$ — Back EMF, measured in **Volts (V)** > - $V$ — Supply voltage, measured in **Volts (V)** > - $I_a$ — Armature current, measured in **Amperes (A)** > - $R_a$ — Armature resistance, measured in **Ohms (Ω)** > - $P$ — Number of poles (dimensionless integer) > - $Z$ — Total number of armature conductors (dimensionless integer) > - $A$ — Number of parallel paths (A = P for wave winding, A = 2 for lap winding) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Foundation The Speed Equation is **not a standalone formula** — it is the **culmination of two fundamental DC machine equations**. Understanding the *why* behind each is critical for KTU problems. ### Step 1: The Voltage Equation (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law on Armature Circuit) When a DC voltage $V$ is applied to the armature terminals, a part of it is dropped across the armature resistance $R_a$ (causing $I_a^2 R_a$ copper loss), and the remainder is **opposed by the Back EMF** $E_b$ generated due to armature rotation in the magnetic field. $$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$ Rearranging: $$E_b = V - I_a R_a$$ ### Step 2: The Back EMF Equation (Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction) According to Faraday's law, the EMF induced in a single conductor cutting magnetic flux is $e = \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$. When integrated over a full rotation and summed over all conductors in all parallel paths, the average EMF generated in the armature is: $$E_b = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$$ where: - $\frac{N}{60}$ converts rpm to revolutions per second. - The factor $\frac{P}{A}$ accounts for the winding configuration. ### Step 3: Equating Both Expressions for $E_b$ The **Back EMF** is a single physical quantity, so: $$\frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A} = V - I_a R_a$$ ### Step 4: Solving for Speed N $$\boxed{N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi} \cdot \frac{60 \cdot A}{P \cdot Z}}$$ This is the **Speed Equation of a DC Motor**. ### Step 5: The Simplified Form Defining the **machine constant** as: $$k = \frac{60 \cdot A}{P \cdot Z} \quad \text{(units: rev/V-min per Wb)}$$ the equation becomes the elegant form: $$N = k \cdot \frac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi}$$ ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Speed Control From the speed equation, **three** variables can be manipulated to change the speed: | Variable | Method of Variation | Effect on Speed | |----------|---------------------|-----------------| | $V$ | Variable DC supply (e.g., chopper, Ward-Leonard) | $N \uparrow$ as $V \uparrow$ | | $\Phi$ | Field current control via field rheostat | $N \uparrow$ as $\Phi \downarrow$ (above base speed) | | $R_a$ | External resistance in series (only at starting) | $N \downarrow$ as $R_{ext} \uparrow$ (lossy method) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** The phrase *"The speed of a DC motor is directly proportional to the back EMF and inversely proportional to the flux"* is a **guaranteed 3-mark question**. Memorize the **direction of proportionality**, not just the formula. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Units | Application | |---|--------------|-------------------|-------|-------------| | 1 | Voltage Equation | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | V, V, A·Ω | KVL on armature | | 2 | Back EMF Equation | $E_b = \dfrac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$ | V, Wb, rpm | Faraday's Law | | 3 | **Speed Equation (DC Motor)** | $N = \dfrac{(V - I_a R_a) \cdot 60 A}{P \Phi Z}$ | rpm | **Core topic** | | 4 | Speed Constant | $k = \dfrac{60 A}{P Z}$ | rev/(V·min·Wb) | Simplified form | | 5 | Synchronous Speed (AC) | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm | Induction/Synchronous motor | | 6 | Slip Relation | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N}{N_s}$ | dimensionless | Induction motor speed | | 7 | Per-Phase EMF (AC) | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \Phi \, T_p \, K_w$ | V | AC machines | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Conditions & Critical Cases:** > - At **no-load**, $I_a \to 0$, so $E_b \approx V$ → No-load speed is $N_0 = \dfrac{60 A V}{P \Phi Z}$. > - At **standstill/start**, $N = 0$, so $E_b = 0$ → Starting current $I_{st} = \dfrac{V}{R_a}$ (very high, hence starters needed). > - For a **shunt motor**, $\Phi$ is approximately constant (since field current is nearly constant) → $N \propto V - I_a R_a$. > - For a **series motor**, $\Phi \propto I_a$ → $N \propto \dfrac{1}{I_a}$ (inverse square law at low loads). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The Speed Equation is the **design cornerstone** of every variable-speed industrial drive: - **Electric vehicles (EVs):** Tesla, Tata Nexon EV — use precisely controlled $V$ via inverters to regulate $N$. - **Cranes and elevators:** Series motor's inverse-speed characteristic (from the equation) provides high starting torque naturally. - **Rolling mills and paper machines:** Ward-Leonard systems vary $V$ over a wide range for fine speed control. - **Robotic servo motors:** Modern BLDC motors (electronically commutated) use the *same* speed equation digitally via PWM. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Speed Equation ### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Two Independent Physical Laws **Law 1 (Circuit Law — Kirchhoff's Voltage Law):** Applied to the armature loop when the motor draws current $I_a$ from supply $V$: $$V = I_a R_a + E_b$$ **Law 2 (Field Law — Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction):** A conductor of length $l$ moving with velocity $v$ perpendicular to flux density $B$ generates an EMF $e = B l v$. ### 3.1.2 Detailed Derivation of Back EMF **Step 1:** Consider one armature conductor. During one full revolution, it cuts a flux equal to $P \Phi$ (since it passes under $P$ poles, each contributing $\Phi$). **Step 2:** Time taken for one revolution: $$t = \frac{60}{N} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$ **Step 3:** Average rate of flux cutting per conductor (which equals the average EMF per conductor): $$e_{avg} = \frac{P \Phi}{t} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60} \quad \text{(Volts per conductor)}$$ **Step 4:** Total number of conductors is $Z$. These are distributed in **A parallel paths**, so the number of conductors in **series per path** is $\dfrac{Z}{A}$. **Step 5:** Since EMFs add in series within one path, the total armature EMF is: $$E_b = e_{avg} \times \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60} \cdot \frac{Z}{A}$$ $$E_b = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$$ ### 3.1.3 Equating and Isolating N **Step 6:** From the voltage equation: $$E_b = V - I_a R_a$$ **Step 7:** Setting the two expressions for $E_b$ equal: $$\frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A} = V - I_a R_a$$ **Step 8:** Multiplying both sides by $\dfrac{60 A}{P \Phi Z}$: $$\boxed{N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi} \cdot \frac{60 A}{P Z}}$$ This is the **full, un-simplified Speed Equation of a DC Motor**. ### 3.1.4 Common Simplification (Lap vs Wave Winding) For a **Lap Winding**: $A = P$ $$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi} \cdot \frac{60}{Z} \quad \text{(lap)}$$ For a **Wave Winding**: $A = 2$ (always) $$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi} \cdot \frac{120}{P Z} \quad \text{(wave)}$$ ## 3.2 Derivative Relationships and Important Variations ### 3.2.1 Speed vs. Armature Voltage (Constant Flux) Holding $\Phi$ constant and differentiating with respect to $V$: $$\frac{\partial N}{\partial V} = \frac{60 A}{P \Phi Z} = \text{constant}$$ This **linearity** is what makes armature voltage control so desirable in industrial drives. ### 3.2.2 No-Load Speed At no-load, $I_a \approx 0$ (small value needed only to overcome friction): $$N_0 = \frac{60 A V}{P \Phi Z}$$ ### 3.2.3 Speed Regulation $$\text{Regulation} = \frac{N_0 - N_{fl}}{N_{fl}} \times 100\%$$ A **shunt motor** has good (small) regulation because $\Phi$ is essentially constant. A **series motor** has poor regulation (speed varies widely with load). ## 3.3 Numerical Problem Walkthrough (KTU Typical Style) > **Problem:** A 4-pole, 250 V DC shunt motor has 800 conductors wave-wound. The armature resistance is 0.2 Ω and the flux per pole is 30 mWb. If the motor takes an armature current of 50 A, find the speed. **Solution:** **Given:** - $P = 4$, $Z = 800$, $A = 2$ (wave), $V = 250$ V, $R_a = 0.2$ Ω, $\Phi = 30 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb, $I_a = 50$ A **Step 1:** Compute Back EMF. $$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 250 - (50)(0.2) = 250 - 10 = 240 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the Speed Equation. $$N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z}$$ $$N = \frac{240 \times 60 \times 2}{4 \times 30 \times 10^{-3} \times 800}$$ **Step 3:** Numerator. $$240 \times 60 \times 2 = 28800$$ **Step 4:** Denominator. $$4 \times 30 \times 10^{-3} \times 800 = 4 \times 24 = 96$$ **Step 5:** Final calculation. $$N = \frac{28800}{96} = 300 \text{ rpm}$$ **Answer:** The motor runs at **300 rpm**. > [!TIP] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always carry units in intermediate steps. The examiner awards **1 mark** just for the correct substitution step. Skipping steps will cost partial marks. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | DC Shunt Motor | DC Series Motor | DC Compound Motor | |----------|----------------|-----------------|-------------------| | Flux $\Phi$ | Approximately constant | $\Phi \propto I_a$ | Partial series effect | | Speed equation reduces to | $N \propto (V - I_aR_a)$ | $N \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{I_a}}$ (at low loads) | Intermediate | | No-load speed | Finite (safe) | Dangerously high (no-load) | Finite | | Speed regulation | Good (~5–10%) | Poor (~25%) | Moderate | | Typical use | Lathes, fans, pumps | Cranes, traction, EVs | Presses, elevators | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — How the Speed Equation is Built ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Supply Voltage V applied to Armature] --> B[Voltage Equation: V = Eb + IaRa] A --> C[Faradays Law: Conductor cuts flux] C --> D[Back EMF Equation: Eb = PPhiNZ / 60A] B --> E[Equate both expressions for Eb] D --> E E --> F[Isolate Speed N] F --> G[Speed Equation: N = V minus IaRa times 60A over PPhiZ] G --> H{Manipulate Variables} H -->|Increase V| I[Speed Increases - Armature Control] H -->|Decrease Phi| J[Speed Increases - Field Weakening] H -->|Increase Ra| K[Speed Decreases - Rheostatic Control] style A fill:#e1f5ff style G fill:#ffe1e1 style H fill:#fff4e1 style I fill:#d4f4dd style J fill:#d4f4dd style K fill:#f4d4d4 ``` ## 4.2 DC Motor Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph LR DC[DC Supply V] -->|Ia| ARM[Armature Winding
Resistance Ra] ARM -->|Back EMF Eb| FB[Opposes Supply V] DC -->|If| FLD[Field Winding
Produces Flux Phi] FLD -->|Phi| MFR[Magnetic Field in Air Gap] ARM -->|Conducts current in MFR| TRQ[Electromagnetic Torque T] MFR -->|Flux linking| ARM ARM -->|Rotates at N rpm| SHAFT[Mechanical Output Shaft] ARM -->|Induces Eb| FB style DC fill:#cce5ff style ARM fill:#ffd9b3 style FLD fill:#d4f4dd style SHAFT fill:#ffccff ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Variables Affecting Motor Speed ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUTS[Independent Variables] V1[Supply Voltage V] RA1[Armature Resistance Ra] PHI1[Flux per Pole Phi] end subgraph EQ[Speed Equation] SE[N = V minus IaRa times 60A over PPhiZ] end subgraph OUTPUT[Dependent Variable] N1[Motor Speed N in rpm] end V1 --> SE RA1 --> SE PHI1 --> SE SE --> N1 style INPUTS fill:#e1f5ff style EQ fill:#ffe1e1 style OUTPUT fill:#d4f4dd ``` ## 4.4 Decision Tree — Speed Control Method Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Need speed above or below base speed?} Q1 -->|Below base speed| Q2{Is efficiency critical?} Q1 -->|Above base speed| A1[Use Field Weakening - Reduce Phi] Q2 -->|Yes| A2[Armature Voltage Control - Ward Leonard / Chopper] Q2 -->|No, cost critical| A3[Rheostatic Control - Add Ra in series] A1 --> OUT1[Speed Increases - Constant Power Region] A2 --> OUT2[Speed Varies Linearly with V - Constant Torque] A3 --> OUT3[Speed Decreases - High I2R Losses] style Q1 fill:#fff4e1 style A1 fill:#d4f4dd style A2 fill:#d4f4dd style A3 fill:#f4d4d4 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **State and explain the speed equation of a DC motor. Why is the no-load speed of a DC shunt motor finite, while that of a DC series motor is dangerously high?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The speed equation of a DC motor is: $$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi} \cdot \frac{60 A}{P Z}$$ It shows that speed is **directly proportional** to the net voltage $(V - I_a R_a)$ across the armature and **inversely proportional** to the flux per pole $\Phi$. **Why the difference at no-load:** - In a **shunt motor**, the field winding is connected in parallel; even at no-load, the field current $I_f = V/R_f$ produces a finite, well-defined flux $\Phi$. Hence the no-load speed $N_0 = 60AV/(P\Phi Z)$ is **finite and safe**. - In a **series motor**, the field winding is in series with the armature; at no-load $I_a \to 0$, so $\Phi \to 0$. From the speed equation, $N \to \infty$ (in practice, it becomes destructively high). Therefore, a series motor is **never belt-connected** and must always be loaded. **(1 Mark for the difference, 1 Mark for shunt finite, 1 Mark for series infinite.)** ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **A 4-pole DC motor with 600 conductors, lap-connected, runs at 1000 rpm on full load. If the flux per pole is 50 mWb and the armature current is 40 A, calculate the back EMF and the supply voltage, given $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Given:** $P = 4$, $Z = 600$, $A = P = 4$ (lap), $N = 1000$ rpm, $\Phi = 50 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb, $I_a = 40$ A, $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$. **Step 1:** Back EMF $$E_b = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A} = \frac{4 \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 \times 600}{60 \times 4}$$ $$E_b = \frac{120000}{240} = 500 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2:** Supply voltage $$V = E_b + I_a R_a = 500 + (40)(0.5) = 500 + 20 = 520 \text{ V}$$ **Answer:** $E_b = 500$ V, $V = 520$ V. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]** **(a)** Starting from the basic principles of Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, **derive the speed equation of a DC motor**. Clearly state the assumptions and define each term in the final expression. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A 250 V, 4-pole, wave-wound DC shunt motor has 1000 armature conductors. The armature resistance is 0.1 Ω and the field current is 2 A at no-load. The full-load armature current is 80 A and the flux per pole at full load is 25 mWb. The armature reaction weakens the flux by 4% at full load. Calculate: **(i)** the **no-load speed**, and **(ii)** the **full-load speed**. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution to Question A** #### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks) **Assumption:** Wave/lap winding, constant brush position, uniform air-gap flux, linear magnetic circuit. **Step 1: EMF in a single conductor per revolution** A conductor cuts a flux $P \Phi$ per revolution (passing under $P$ poles, each of flux $\Phi$). **(1 Mark)** **Step 2: Time per revolution** $t = \dfrac{60}{N}$ seconds. **(1 Mark)** **Step 3: Average EMF per conductor** $$e_{avg} = \frac{P \Phi}{t} = \frac{P \Phi N}{60}$$ **(1 Mark)** **Step 4: Conductors in series per parallel path** There are $A$ parallel paths, so conductors in series per path $= Z/A$. **(1 Mark)** **Step 5: Total armature EMF** $$E_b = e_{avg} \times \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{P \Phi N Z}{60 A}$$ **(1 Mark)** **Step 6: Voltage equation of the armature** By KVL, $V = E_b + I_a R_a$, so $E_b = V - I_a R_a$. **(1 Mark)** **Step 7: Equating and solving for N** $$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi} \cdot \frac{60 A}{P Z}$$ **(1 Mark)** #### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks) **Given:** $V = 250$ V, $P = 4$, $A = 2$ (wave), $Z = 1000$, $R_a = 0.1\ \Omega$, $I_{f,NL} = 2$ A, $I_{a,FL} = 80$ A, $\Phi_{NL}$ (assumed same as unloaded) corresponding to $I_f = 2$ A. **Step 1: No-load flux** At no-load, the field current is 2 A (no armature reaction weakening). $$\Phi_{NL} = 25 \text{ mWb (reference value)}$$ But we must determine it from the **no-load condition**. Since the motor runs unloaded, $I_a \approx 0$ (assume ideal). The flux at no load is given by the field current of 2 A. We are told the flux per pole at full load (before weakening) is 25 mWb. Assume this is the no-load flux as well (since field current is the same): $$\Phi_{NL} = 25 \text{ mWb} = 25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}$$ **Step 2: No-load speed (i)** At no-load, $E_b \approx V = 250$ V. $$N_0 = \frac{V \times 60 A}{P \Phi_{NL} Z} = \frac{250 \times 60 \times 2}{4 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000}$$ $$N_0 = \frac{30000}{100} = 300 \text{ rpm}$$ **Step 3: Full-load flux (with 4% weakening)** $$\Phi_{FL} = 25 \times 10^{-3} \times (1 - 0.04) = 24 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Wb}$$ **Step 4: Full-load back EMF** $$E_{b,FL} = V - I_{a,FL} R_a = 250 - (80)(0.1) = 250 - 8 = 242 \text{ V}$$ **Step 5: Full-load speed (ii)** $$N_{FL} = \frac{E_{b,FL} \times 60 A}{P \Phi_{FL} Z} = \frac{242 \times 60 \times 2}{4 \times 24 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000}$$ $$N_{FL} = \frac{29040}{96} = 302.5 \text{ rpm}$$ **Answers:** $N_0 = 300$ rpm, $N_{FL} = 302.5$ rpm. > **Speed Regulation:** > $$\text{Reg} = \frac{300 - 302.5}{302.5} \times 100\% = -0.83\%$$ > (Negative regulation — speed rises with load due to flux weakening by armature reaction.) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]** **(a)** With the help of the **speed equation**, explain **three methods of speed control of a DC shunt motor**. Compare their advantages and disadvantages in a tabular form. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A DC series motor runs at 600 rpm when drawing a current of 80 A from a 220 V supply. The armature resistance is 0.1 Ω and the series field resistance is 0.05 Ω. Assuming the flux is proportional to the armature current, calculate the speed when the current drops to 30 A. Also, calculate the percentage change in speed. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution to Question B** #### Part (a) — Speed Control Methods (7 Marks) The speed equation: $$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi} \cdot \frac{60 A}{P Z}$$ shows three controllable variables: $V$, $R_a$, $\Phi$. **1. Armature Voltage Control (V is varied):** Used **below base speed**. A variable DC source (Ward-Leonard generator, chopper, or PWM inverter) supplies the armature. Speed varies **linearly** with $V$. **Efficient** but **expensive**. **(2 Marks)** **2. Field Flux Control (Φ is varied):** Used **above base speed**. A field rheostat in series with the shunt field reduces the field current, weakening $\Phi$. Speed is **inversely proportional** to $\Phi$. **Cheap and efficient** but limited range. **(2 Marks)** **3. Rheostatic Armature Control ($R_a$ is varied):** External resistance is added in series with the armature. Speed **decreases** as resistance increases. **Cheap** but **highly inefficient** (large $I^2R$ loss); used only in intermittent-duty applications (e.g., crane hoists). **(2 Marks)** **Comparison Table (1 Mark):** | Feature | Armature Voltage | Field Flux | Rheostatic | |---------|------------------|------------|------------| | Region | Below base speed | Above base speed | Below base speed | | Efficiency | High | High | Low | | Cost | High | Low | Low | | Speed range | Wide | Limited (~2:1) | Wide | | Torque | Constant | Reduces | Reduces | #### Part (b) — Series Motor Numerical (7 Marks) **Given:** $N_1 = 600$ rpm at $I_1 = 80$ A, $V = 220$ V, $R_a = 0.1\ \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.05\ \Omega$, $I_2 = 30$ A. **Step 1: Total resistance** $R_t = R_a + R_{se} = 0.1 + 0.05 = 0.15\ \Omega$ **Step 2: Back EMF at $I_1 = 80$ A** $$E_{b1} = V - I_1 R_t = 220 - 80(0.15) = 220 - 12 = 208 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3: Apply Speed Equation at $I_1$ and $I_2$** Since $\Phi \propto I_a$ (given), let $\Phi = k_1 I_a$. Then: $$N = \frac{V - I_a R_t}{k_1 I_a} \cdot \frac{60 A}{P Z} = \frac{K (V - I_a R_t)}{I_a}$$ where $K = \dfrac{60 A}{k_1 P Z}$ is a constant. **Step 4: Set up the ratio** $$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{(V - I_2 R_t) / I_2}{(V - I_1 R_t) / I_1}$$ $$N_2 = N_1 \cdot \frac{I_1 (V - I_2 R_t)}{I_2 (V - I_1 R_t)}$$ **Step 5: Substitute values** $$E_{b2} = V - I_2 R_t = 220 - 30(0.15) = 220 - 4.5 = 215.5 \text{ V}$$ $$N_2 = 600 \times \frac{80 \times 215.5}{30 \times 208} = 600 \times \frac{17240}{6240}$$ $$N_2 = 600 \times 2.7628 = 1657.7 \text{ rpm}$$ **Step 6: Percentage change in speed** $$\%\Delta N = \frac{1657.7 - 600}{600} \times 100\% = 176.3\%$$ **Answers:** $N_2 \approx 1658$ rpm; speed increases by approximately **176.3%**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** > 1. **Forgetting the series field resistance** $R_{se}$ — many students use only $R_a$ in the voltage equation. This causes an error of 3–4 V in $E_b$ and a wrong final answer. **(Lose 1–2 marks.)** > 2. **Mixing up the linear proportionality** — in a series motor, $\Phi$ is proportional to $I_a$ only in the **unsaturated (linear) region** of the magnetization curve. The problem statement must explicitly assume this. If $\Phi$ is given as a constant, use the **shunt** motor formula. > 3. **Sign convention in speed regulation** — examiners deduct marks if the regulation is reported as positive when it should be negative (or vice versa). Always write: $\text{Reg} = (N_0 - N_{fl})/N_{fl}$. > 4. **Units of flux** — flux must always be in **Weber** in the formula. Forgetting to convert mWb to Wb ($1$ mWb $= 10^{-3}$ Wb) leads to answers off by a factor of 1000. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Speed Equation** - ⚡ **Master Formula:** $$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi} \cdot \frac{60 A}{P Z}$$ - ⚡ **Three Pillars:** Back EMF ($E_b$), Voltage equation ($V = E_b + I_a R_a$), and Faraday's law ($E_b = P\Phi NZ / 60A$). - ⚡ **Proportionality Rules (memorize!):** - $N \propto (V - I_a R_a)$ — **direct** proportionality - $N \propto 1/\Phi$ — **inverse** proportionality - $N \propto 1/I_a$ — for series motor (low-load region) - ⚡ **Winding Identities:** - **Lap winding:** $A = P$ (always) — high current, low voltage machines - **Wave winding:** $A = 2$ (always) — high voltage, low current machines - ⚡ **No-Load vs. Standstill:** - No-load: $N = N_0$, $E_b \approx V$ - Standstill: $N = 0$, $E_b = 0$, $I_{st} = V/R_a$ → must use starter! - ⚡ **Speed Control Hierarchy:** - **Below base speed:** Armature voltage control (efficient) or rheostatic (lossy) - **Above base speed:** Field weakening only - **Combined:** Ward-Leonard for the widest range - ⚡ **Series Motor Caveats:** - Never start on no-load → will "run away" - $\Phi \propto I_a$ assumption valid only **below saturation knee** - High starting torque — ideal for traction and cranes - ⚡ **Common KTU Numerical Format:** - Always: **Given → Formula → Substitution → Step-by-step arithmetic → Boxed answer with units**. - Carrying **units** in every step earns **bonus partial credit**. - ⚡ **Real-World Linkage:** - EV drives, robotics, CNC machines, paper mills, elevators, traction systems — all rely fundamentally on this equation. - ⚡ **Key Pitfall:** - Do NOT confuse the **DC motor speed equation** (above) with the **synchronous speed** of AC machines: $N_s = 120f/P$. They are **different** equations for **different** machines.

topics.title: Torques, Performance Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT412 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS MEASUREMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Torques, Performance Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Defining Torques in Electromechanical Indicating Instruments

A measuring instrument is a device that converts a physical quantity (electrical or non-electrical) into a readable form. Every **deflecting-type indicating instrument** (analog meter) operates on the fundamental principle of equilibrium between multiple mechanical torques acting on a moving system. Without these torques, the pointer would either spin uncontrollably, stay frozen at zero, or vibrate forever.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Interpretation):**
> The *deflecting torque* produces a rotation of the pointer in proportion to the measurand. The *controlling torque* opposes the deflecting torque and grows with pointer deflection, establishing a steady reading. The *damping torque* acts only during motion to suppress oscillations and bring the pointer to rest without affecting the final steady-state deflection.

The **four fundamental torques** governing the moving system of an indicating instrument are:

| S.No | Torque | Symbol | Role |
|------|--------|--------|------|
| 1 | Deflecting Torque | $T_d$ | Drives the pointer from zero in proportion to measurand |
| 2 | Controlling Torque | $T_c$ | Opposes $T_d$, provides restoring action, defines scale |
| 3 | Damping Torque | $T_{D}$ | Suppresses pointer oscillations, no effect at final position |
| 4 | Friction Torque | $T_f$ | Undesirable, opposes motion, reduces accuracy |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Steering Wheel Analogy:**
> Imagine you are driving a car (the pointer) along a curved road (the scale of the meter).
> - **Deflecting Torque ($T_d$)** is the **engine power** pushing the car forward — stronger measurand = harder push.
> - **Controlling Torque ($T_c$)** is the **steering and brakes** that pull the car back toward the centerline based on how far you've turned. Without it, the car would accelerate forever.
> - **Damping Torque ($T_D$)** is the **shock absorbers**. When the car hits a bump, they absorb energy and stop the bouncing. At rest (steady reading), they stop applying force.
> - **Friction Torque ($T_f$)** is the **road roughness** — a nuisance that resists motion, wastes energy, and makes the pointer sluggish.

At the **final steady-state indication**, three torques balance:
$$T_d - T_c - T_f = 0$$
Since $T_f$ is small and ideally neglected, the working equation becomes:
$$T_d = T_c$$

### 1.3 Standardized Units and Conventions

- All torques are expressed in **Newton·meters (N·m)** or **dyne·cm** (CGS).
- The **deflection angle** $\theta$ is in **radians (rad)** or **degrees**.
- **Sensitivity** $S$ has units of **rad/unit of measurand** or **mm/A** for ammeters.
- **Damping ratio** $\xi$ is **dimensionless**.
- The **natural frequency** $\omega_n$ is in **rad/sec**.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Torque

#### 2.1.1 Deflecting Torque ($T_d$)
The deflecting torque is generated by the **deflecting system** of the instrument. The mechanism depends on the type of instrument:

- **PMMC (Permanent Magnet Moving Coil):** Magnetic field of permanent magnet interacts with current-carrying coil. $T_d \propto I$ (linear)
- **Moving Iron (MI):** Soft iron vane is attracted/repelled by current-carrying coil. $T_d \propto I^2$ (quadratic, hence AC/DC usable)
- **Electrodynamometer:** Interaction between two current-carrying coils. $T_d \propto I_1 I_2 \cos\phi$
- **Electrostatic:** Force between charged plates. $T_d \propto V^2$
- **Induction (Shaded-pole, Watt-hour meter):** Interaction between alternating fluxes. $T_d \propto \phi_1 \phi_2 \sin\alpha$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** The *shape* of the scale depends entirely on the relationship between $T_d$ and the measurand. If $T_d \propto$ (measurand)$^n$, the scale is uniform only when $n=1$. For $n=2$ (MI, electrostatic), the scale is **square-law** (cramped at low end, open at high end).

#### 2.1.2 Controlling Torque ($T_c$)
The controlling torque must grow with deflection $\theta$ to balance $T_d$ at a unique steady position. There are **two methods** of providing $T_c$:

**Method A — Spring Control (Gravity Control is obsolete for portable instruments):**
- Two helical phosphor-bronze springs (one on either side of the spindle) wound in opposite directions to cancel temperature effects.
- $T_c \propto \theta$ i.e., $T_c = K_s \theta$, where $K_s$ is the **spring constant** in N·m/rad.
- **Advantages:** Portable, uniform torque on both sides, no leveling required, can be used in any orientation.
- **Disadvantages:** Affected by temperature (spring constant changes), elastic fatigue over time.

**Method B — Gravity Control:**
- A small weight (usually 2–5 gm) attached to the spindle. As the spindle rotates, the weight's moment arm changes.
- $T_c = W \cdot L \cdot \sin\theta$, where $W$ is weight, $L$ is distance from pivot.
- **Advantages:** Cheaper, temperature-insensitive.
- **Disadvantages:** Must be used **only in vertical (upright) position**, produces **non-uniform (cramped) scale** at low end, less portable.

#### 2.1.3 Damping Torque ($T_D$)
Damping torque exists **only during pointer motion** and is designed to be zero at steady state. There are three common damping systems:

| Damping System | Mechanism | $T_D$ Expression | Disadvantage |
|----------------|-----------|------------------|--------------|
| Air Friction | Piston in air chamber | $T_D = D \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}$ | Needs airtight chamber |
| Fluid Friction | Vane immersed in oil | $T_D = D \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}$ | Oil leaks, temperature dependent |
| Eddy Current | Aluminum disc moving in PM field | $T_D \propto \frac{d\theta}{dt}$ (independent of $I$) | Strongest, most common |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Students often forget that eddy-current damping provides a torque **proportional to angular velocity, not to current**. It works only for AC or transient DC — pure DC produces no eddy currents and **no damping** in an eddy-current system.

The mathematical model is:
$$T_D = D \cdot \omega = D \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$
where $D$ is the **damping coefficient** (N·m·s/rad).

#### 2.1.4 Friction Torque ($T_f$)
$T_f$ is an **undesirable** torque at the pivot bearings. The **figure of merit** of an instrument (goodness) is defined as:
$$\text{Figure of Merit} = \frac{T_d}{T_f} = 10 \text{ (minimum acceptable)}$$
A higher figure of merit means the instrument is more accurate because friction has less relative effect on the reading.

### 2.2 Performance Characteristics — Static

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static characteristics** describe instrument behavior when the measurand is **constant or slowly varying** (steady-state).

- **Accuracy:** Closeness of reading to true value. Expressed as % of full-scale deflection (FSD) or % of true value.
- **Precision:** Repeatability of readings. An instrument can be precise without being accurate.
- **Sensitivity ($S$):** Ratio of change in output to change in input. $S = \frac{d\theta}{dI}$ for an ammeter.
- **Resolution:** Smallest measurable change in input. Determined by smallest scale division.
- **Dead Zone / Dead Band:** Range of input for which the output does not change. Caused by friction.
- **Linearity:** How closely the calibration curve follows a straight line. $\% \text{ Linearity} = \frac{(\text{Max deviation})}{\text{FSD}} \times 100$
- **Drift:** Slow change of output with time at constant input. Zero drift, span drift.
- **Reproducibility / Repeatability:** Ability to produce same reading for same input under same conditions over time.
- **Threshold:** Smallest input that produces detectable output.
- **Span / Range:** Difference between maximum and minimum measurable values.

### 2.3 Performance Characteristics — Dynamic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic characteristics** describe instrument behavior when the measurand **changes rapidly with time**.

- **Speed of Response:** How quickly the instrument reaches its final steady value.
- **Lag:** Delay between input change and output response.
- **Fidelity:** Ability to reproduce the input signal faithfully (without distortion) at output.
- **Dynamic Error:** Difference between true value of measurand (at any instant) and the indicated value, when the measurand is varying with time.
- **Time Constant ($\tau$):** For a first-order system, the time to reach **63.2%** of final value.
- **Settling Time:** Time to reach and stay within $\pm 2\%$ (or $\pm 5\%$) of final value.
- **Rise Time:** Time to go from 10% to 90% of final value.
- **Overshoot:** Amount by which the response **exceeds** the steady-state value, expressed as % of FSD.
- **Damping Ratio ($\xi$):** Controls the nature of the response.

### 2.4 Instrument Dynamic Modeling

The moving system of an indicating instrument can be modeled as a **second-order mechanical rotational system**:

$$J \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + D \frac{d\theta}{dt} + K_s \theta = T_d(t)$$

where:
- $J$ = moment of inertia of moving system (kg·m²)
- $D$ = damping coefficient (N·m·s/rad)
- $K_s$ = spring constant (N·m/rad)
- $T_d(t)$ = input deflecting torque (function of measurand)

This is the **standard second-order differential equation**, identical in form to RLC electrical circuits and mass-spring-damper mechanical systems.

**Natural (undamped) frequency:**
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{J}} \quad \text{rad/sec}$$

**Damping ratio:**
$$\xi = \frac{D}{2\sqrt{K_s J}} = \frac{D}{2 \sqrt{J \cdot K_s}}$$

### 2.5 The Four Response Regimes (Damping Cases)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** The damping ratio $\xi$ classifies the dynamic response into **four regimes**. Examiner frequently asks: *Classify and sketch the step response for each case.*

| Case | Damping Ratio | Response Type | Pointer Behavior |
|------|--------------|---------------|------------------|
| Underdamped | $0 \le \xi < 1$ | Oscillatory | Pointer oscillates around true value, gradually settling |
| Critically damped | $\xi = 1$ | Non-oscillatory, fastest | Reaches true value in **minimum time** without overshoot |
| Overdamped | $\xi > 1$ | Non-oscillatory, sluggish | Reaches true value slowly, no overshoot |
| Undamped | $\xi = 0$ | Pure oscillation | Pointer oscillates forever (theoretical) |

For an **indicating instrument**, the desired response is **slightly underdamped** ($\xi \approx 0.6$ to $0.8$) so that the pointer reaches the final value quickly with a small overshoot that the user barely notices. This is the **engineering compromise**.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Following Table Summarizes All Formulas Required for the Topic "Torques & Performance Characteristics" — No Symbol Conflicts.**

| # | Concept | Equation | Units | Condition |
|---|---------|----------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | Torque balance (steady) | $T_d = T_c$ | N·m | Ideal, friction neglected |
| 2 | Spring control | $T_c = K_s \theta$ | N·m | Spiral springs |
| 3 | Gravity control | $T_c = W L \sin\theta$ | N·m | Vertical only |
| 4 | Eddy current damping | $T_D = D \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}$ | N·m | Moving aluminum vane |
| 5 | Equation of motion | $J \ddot{\theta} + D \dot{\theta} + K_s \theta = T_d(t)$ | — | Standard 2nd order |
| 6 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_s / J}$ | rad/s | Undamped oscillation freq. |
| 7 | Damping ratio | $\xi = \dfrac{D}{2 \sqrt{J K_s}}$ | dimensionless | Always $\ge 0$ |
| 8 | Damped freq. | $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \xi^2}$ | rad/s | Underdamped only |
| 9 | Figure of merit | $FOM = T_d / T_f$ | — | Min 10 (good) |
| 10 | Sensitivity | $S = d\theta / dX$ | rad/unit | Higher = better |
| 11 | Linearity error | $\%L = \dfrac{\Delta_{\max}}{FSD} \times 100$ | % | Closer to 0 = better |
| 12 | Time constant (1st order) | $\tau = J / D$ | s | First order approx. |
| 13 | Response eq. (critically damped) | $\theta(t) = \theta_f \left(1 - e^{-\omega_n t}(1+\omega_n t)\right)$ | rad | $\xi = 1$ |
| 14 | Response eq. (underdamped) | $\theta(t) = \theta_f \left[1 - \dfrac{e^{-\xi \omega_n t}}{\sqrt{1-\xi^2}} \sin(\omega_d t + \phi)\right]$ | rad | $\xi < 1$ |
| 15 | Phase angle (underdamped) | $\phi = \cos^{-1}(\xi)$ | rad | At time $t=0$ |
| 16 | Dead zone | $DZ = \dfrac{2 T_f}{S}$ | input units | Friction effect |
| 17 | PMMC deflecting torque | $T_d = BINA$ | N·m | $N$ turns, $A$ area, $B$ flux density |
| 18 | MI deflecting torque | $T_d \propto I^2$ | N·m | Attraction/repulsion type |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Meter Design:** The choice of $\xi \approx 0.7$ ensures that a domestic energy-meter analog pointer settles in under 4 seconds, allowing fast reading by technicians.
- **Watt-Hour Meters:** These use an **Aluminum disc with permanent magnet** for eddy-current damping, and **brake magnets** for the **braking torque** that exactly balances the **driving torque** at constant speed.
- **Aircraft Instruments:** Aircraft panel meters are critically damped to prevent reading errors during maneuvers.
- **Industrial Recorders:** Chart recorders are designed as **first-order systems** (deflection type galvanometer with low inertia) for fast response in process control.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pointer Deflection from Torque Equilibrium

Consider a moving system of a PMMC instrument. Let $N$ = number of turns, $I$ = current, $A$ = coil area, $B$ = flux density in the air gap. The force on one side of the coil is:
$$F_1 = B I N l$$
where $l$ is the active length of the coil side.

Since there are **two active sides**, the total force on the coil is:
$$F = 2 \cdot B I N l$$

The deflecting torque is force $\times$ perpendicular distance from pivot (which is the coil width $b$):
$$T_d = F \cdot b = 2 B I N l b = B I N A$$
where $A = l \cdot b$ is the coil area.

At steady state, $T_d = T_c$. With spring control $T_c = K_s \theta$:
$$B I N A = K_s \theta$$

Solving for deflection $\theta$:
$$\theta = \frac{B N A}{K_s} \cdot I$$

The quantity $\frac{B N A}{K_s}$ is defined as the **sensitivity constant** $K_\theta$:

$$\boxed{\theta = K_\theta \cdot I \quad \text{where} \quad K_\theta = \frac{B N A}{K_s}}$$

This proves the **linear scale** of PMMC instruments: deflection $\theta$ is **directly proportional** to current $I$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Second-Order Dynamic Response for Step Input

The equation of motion is:
$$J \frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + D \frac{d\theta}{dt} + K_s \theta = T_d(t)$$

Apply a **step input** $T_d(t) = T_{d0} \cdot u(t)$, where $u(t)$ is the unit step. The final steady deflection is:
$$\theta_f = \frac{T_{d0}}{K_s}$$

Divide the entire equation by $J$:
$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + \frac{D}{J} \frac{d\theta}{dt} + \frac{K_s}{J} \theta = \frac{T_{d0}}{J} u(t)$$

Substitute $\omega_n^2 = K_s / J$ and $2 \xi \omega_n = D / J$:
$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + 2 \xi \omega_n \frac{d\theta}{dt} + \omega_n^2 \theta = \omega_n^2 \theta_f \cdot u(t)$$

Define the **error** $e(t) = \theta_f - \theta(t)$:
$$\frac{d^2 e}{dt^2} + 2 \xi \omega_n \frac{de}{dt} + \omega_n^2 e = 0$$
with initial conditions $e(0) = \theta_f$ and $\dot{e}(0) = 0$.

**Case A: Underdamped ($0 \le \xi < 1$):**

The characteristic roots are $s_{1,2} = -\xi \omega_n \pm j \omega_d$, where:
$$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \xi^2}$$

The general solution is:
$$e(t) = e^{-\xi \omega_n t} \left( A \cos \omega_d t + B \sin \omega_d t \right)$$

Applying $e(0) = \theta_f$ gives $A = \theta_f$. Applying $\dot{e}(0) = 0$ gives:
$$0 = -\xi \omega_n \theta_f + B \omega_d \Rightarrow B = \frac{\xi \omega_n \theta_f}{\omega_d} = \frac{\xi \theta_f}{\sqrt{1-\xi^2}}$$

Therefore:
$$e(t) = \theta_f \, e^{-\xi \omega_n t} \left[ \cos \omega_d t + \frac{\xi}{\sqrt{1-\xi^2}} \sin \omega_d t \right]$$

Using the identity $\cos \phi = \xi$, $\sin \phi = \sqrt{1-\xi^2}$ where $\phi = \cos^{-1}\xi$:
$$e(t) = \frac{\theta_f}{\sqrt{1-\xi^2}} \, e^{-\xi \omega_n t} \cos\left( \omega_d t - \cos^{-1}\xi \right)$$

Final underdamped response:
$$\boxed{\theta(t) = \theta_f \left[ 1 - \frac{e^{-\xi \omega_n t}}{\sqrt{1-\xi^2}} \cos\left( \omega_d t - \cos^{-1}\xi \right) \right]}$$

**Case B: Critically Damped ($\xi = 1$):**

The roots are **repeated** at $s = -\omega_n$. The general solution is:
$$e(t) = (A + B t) e^{-\omega_n t}$$

Applying $e(0) = \theta_f$ gives $A = \theta_f$. Applying $\dot{e}(0) = 0$ gives $B = \omega_n \theta_f$. Therefore:
$$e(t) = \theta_f (1 + \omega_n t) e^{-\omega_n t}$$

Final critically damped response:
$$\boxed{\theta(t) = \theta_f \left[ 1 - (1 + \omega_n t) e^{-\omega_n t} \right]}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Settling Time and Overshoot Formulas

**Maximum Overshoot** (only for underdamped response):
$$M_p = e^{-\xi \pi / \sqrt{1 - \xi^2}}$$
Expressed as a percentage:
$$\boxed{\%M_p = 100 \cdot e^{-\xi \pi / \sqrt{1 - \xi^2}}}$$

**Time to First Peak (peak time):**
Setting $\frac{d\theta}{dt} = 0$ at the first peak, we find:
$$\boxed{t_p = \frac{\pi}{\omega_d} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_n \sqrt{1 - \xi^2}}}$$

**Settling Time (2% criterion):**
The envelope $1 \pm e^{-\xi \omega_n t}/\sqrt{1-\xi^2}$ reaches 2% of final value when $e^{-\xi \omega_n t_s} \approx 0.02$:
$$\boxed{t_s \approx \frac{4}{\xi \omega_n}}$$

**Rise Time (10% to 90%):**
For underdamped systems, a useful approximation is:
$$\boxed{t_r \approx \frac{\pi - \phi}{\omega_d} = \frac{\pi - \cos^{-1}\xi}{\omega_n \sqrt{1-\xi^2}}}$$

### 3.4 Numerical Example — Worked Out (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A moving-coil instrument has $J = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ kg·m², $D = 8 \times 10^{-4}$ N·m·s/rad, and $K_s = 16 \times 10^{-4}$ N·m/rad. Determine (a) natural frequency, (b) damping ratio, (c) type of damping, (d) first peak overshoot, (e) settling time (2%).

**Step 1 — Natural frequency:**
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_s}{J}} = \sqrt{\frac{16 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Damping ratio:**
$$\xi = \frac{D}{2\sqrt{K_s J}} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-4}}{2\sqrt{16 \times 10^{-4} \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

Compute the denominator: $K_s J = 64 \times 10^{-10}$, $\sqrt{K_s J} = 8 \times 10^{-5}$, $2\sqrt{K_s J} = 16 \times 10^{-5} = 1.6 \times 10^{-4}$.

$$\xi = \frac{8 \times 10^{-4}}{1.6 \times 10^{-4}} = 5$$

**Step 3 — Type of damping:** Since $\xi = 5 > 1$, the system is **overdamped**.

**Step 4 — Overshoot:** None, because $\xi > 1$. The system does not overshoot.

**Step 5 — Settling time:** For overdamped system, $t_s \approx \frac{4}{\xi \omega_n} = \frac{4}{5 \times 20} = \frac{4}{100} = 0.04$ s. (Note: this approximation is conservative for overdamped; true settling is slightly longer.)

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Torques by Instrument Type

| Instrument | $T_d$ Generation | $T_c$ Method | $T_D$ Method | Scale Shape |
|------------|------------------|--------------|--------------|-------------|
| PMMC | Coil in PM field | Spring | Eddy current | Linear |
| MI (Attraction) | Iron vane attracted | Spring/Gravity | Air friction | Square law ($I^2$) |
| MI (Repulsion) | Iron vanes repel | Spring | Air/Eddy | Square law |
| Electrodynamometer | Two coils | Spring | Air friction | Non-uniform (varies) |
| Electrostatic | Charged plates | Spring/Gravity | Air friction/Eddy | Square law ($V^2$) |
| Induction (Watt-hour) | Two AC fluxes | Magnetic brake (eddy) | Inherent braking | Linear (energy) |
| Rectifier | PMMC + diodes | Spring | Eddy current | Linear (AC rectified) |

### 3.6 Derivation 4 — Damping Torque Energy Dissipation

The damping torque does **negative work** equal to the energy dissipated during the transient oscillation. For eddy-current damping, the EMF induced in the aluminum disc is:
$$e = B l v = B l \dot{x}$$
where $B$ is flux density, $l$ is the radial length cutting the field, and $v = \dot{x}$ is the linear velocity at radius $x$ from the spindle.

The induced current (assuming low resistance $R$):
$$i = \frac{e}{R} = \frac{B l \dot{x}}{R}$$

The retarding force on the disc:
$$F = B l i = \frac{B^2 l^2 \dot{x}}{R}$$

Convert to torque using lever arm $x$:
$$T_D = F \cdot x = \frac{B^2 l^2 x^2 \dot{x}}{R}$$

For a **complete disc** integrating across radius, this simplifies to a torque proportional to angular velocity:
$$T_D = D \dot{\theta}$$
with $D = \dfrac{B^2 l^2}{R} \int x^2 \, dx$, which is a constant of the construction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The damping coefficient $D$ is **independent of the measurand** and depends only on **disc geometry, magnet strength, and disc material resistivity**. This is why eddy-current damping is the most predictable and preferred in precision instruments.
