## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Details of an SRM (8/6 Configuration)

**Stator:**
- Made of **silicon steel laminations** (0.35 mm or 0.5 mm thick) to minimize eddy current losses.
- Has **8 salient inward-projecting poles**, each carrying a **concentrated (tooth) winding**.
- Diametrically opposite stator poles are connected in *series* to form one **phase**. Hence 8/6 SRM has **4 phases (A, B, C, D)**.

**Rotor:**
- Made of **silicon steel laminations** (no conductors, no magnets, no cage).
- Has **6 salient outward-projecting poles** (teeth) and 6 interpolar slots.
- Mechanically simple → **the most rugged rotor in the entire electrical machine family**.

**Power Electronic Converter (Asymmetric Half-Bridge):**
- Each phase requires **two power switches and two freewheeling diodes**.
- Switches are typically **IGBT or MOSFET** modules.
- The converter is **bidirectional** in torque (motoring and generating).

**Rotor Position Sensor:**
- An **optical encoder or Hall-effect sensor** is mounted on the non-drive end of the shaft.
- Resolution: at least **10-bit** for smooth commutation.
- Sensorless control (sliding mode observers, AI estimators) is an active KTU research topic.

### 2.2 Principle of Operation – Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Magnetic Circuit:** When phase A is energized, a magnetic flux $\phi$ is established across the air gap between stator pole pair A-A' and the rotor.
2. **Reluctance Variation:** As the rotor turns, the *air-gap length* between the energized stator pole and the rotor pole changes cyclically. Reluctance $\mathcal{R}(\theta)$ is maximum when the rotor pole is *aligned* with the *stator interpolar slot* (unaligned position) and minimum when they are directly *aligned* (aligned position).
3. **Minimum Reluctance Seeker:** The rotor is always pulled toward the **minimum reluctance position** because the co-energy stored in the magnetic field, $W'_{co} = \tfrac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2$, is maximum there.
4. **Sequential Phase Excitation:** The controller reads rotor position, then fires phase A → phase B → phase C → phase D → phase A… Each commutation advances the rotor by **15°** (for 8/6 SRM), giving a smooth rotation.
5. **Pulse Timing:** A phase is energized only when the *rate of change of inductance* with respect to rotor position ($dL/d\theta$) is **positive** for motoring, and **negative** for regenerative braking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why rotation direction is independent of current polarity:** Torque is proportional to $i^2$, so reversing current *does not* reverse torque. To reverse rotation, you must **advance or retard the commutation angle** — this is a 100% guaranteed KTU question.

### 2.3 Inductance Profile of an SRM

The phase inductance $L(\theta)$ as a function of rotor position $\theta$ is the **heart of SRM analysis**. It has three characteristic regions:

| Region | Position | Inductance | $dL/d\theta$ | Operation |
|--------|----------|------------|--------------|-----------|
| Unaligned | Rotor pole centered with stator slot | $L_{min}$ (low) | $\approx 0$ | No useful torque |
| Rising / Aligning | Rotor pole approaching stator pole | $L_{min} \to L_{max}$ | $> 0$ (positive) | **Motoring torque** |
| Aligned | Rotor pole fully aligned with stator pole | $L_{max}$ (high) | $\approx 0$ | No useful torque |
| Falling / Un-aligning | Rotor pole leaving stator pole | $L_{max} \to L_{min}$ | $< 0$ (negative) | **Generating torque** |

Mathematically, the simplified trapezoidal model is:

$$L(\theta) = L_{min} + (L_{max} - L_{min}) \cdot f(\theta)$$

where $f(\theta)$ is a periodic function with period equal to the rotor pole pitch $\tau_r = \dfrac{360°}{N_r}$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|-------------------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $T_e = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot i^2 \cdot \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | Instantaneous electromagnetic torque (reluctance torque) | N·m |
| 2 | $W'_{co} = \dfrac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2$ | Magnetic co-energy | Joules (J) |
| 3 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{N_r}$ | Synchronous / no-load speed (SRM step rate) | RPM |
| 4 | $\theta_{step} = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$ | Rotor step angle per commutation | degrees |
| 5 | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{l_g}{\mu_0 A_g}$ | Air-gap reluctance (linear) | AT/Wb |
| 6 | $\phi = \dfrac{N i}{\mathcal{R}}$ | Flux per pole (Ohm's law for magnetic circuits) | Weber (Wb) |
| 7 | $L = \dfrac{N^2}{\mathcal{R}} = \dfrac{N \phi}{i}$ | Phase inductance | Henry (H) |
| 8 | $P_{out} = T_e \cdot \omega_m$ | Mechanical output power | Watts (W) |
| 9 | $V = R i + \dfrac{d(L i)}{dt}$ | Phase voltage equation (per phase) | Volts (V) |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{Cu} + P_{core} + P_{mech}}$ | Efficiency of SRM drive (typically 85–92%) | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **NEVER write the torque equation with absolute value pipes inside markdown tables.** Use the syntax `dL/dtheta` or $\dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ to avoid breaking the table parser.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of SRM

- **Electric vehicles (EVs):** Tesla, Renault, and Tata Motors have all patented SRM-based traction drives because of the fault-tolerant operation (if one phase fails, the motor still runs on the remaining phases).
- **Aerospace actuators:** SRMs operate in harsh environments without rare-earth magnets.
- **High-speed industrial drives:** Blowers, compressors, and pumps in the 10 kW – 1 MW range.
- **Domestic appliances:** Washing machines (LG, Whirlpool), vacuum cleaners, mixers (variable-speed high-torque operation).
- **Mining and traction:** Mining locomotives and subway propulsion where robustness trumps efficiency.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Instantaneous Reluctance Torque Equation

**Starting Point:** The torque produced by any electromagnetic system can be expressed as the partial derivative of magnetic co-energy with respect to angular displacement, at constant current.

$$T_e = \left. \frac{\partial W'_{co}(i, \theta)}{\partial \theta} \right|_{i = const}$$

**Step 1:** Express the co-energy stored in a single magnetic phase. For a linear magnetic system (constant permeability, no saturation), the co-energy of one phase is:

$$W'_{co} = \int_{0}^{i} \phi(i', \theta) \, di' = \int_{0}^{i} L(\theta) i' \, di'$$

**Step 2:** Since $L(\theta)$ is independent of the dummy current variable $i'$, we pull it out of the integral:

$$W'_{co} = L(\theta) \int_{0}^{i} i' \, di'$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral of $i'$ from $0$ to $i$. The integral of $i'$ is $\frac{i'^2}{2}$, evaluated between 0 and $i$:

$$W'_{co} = L(\theta) \cdot \frac{i^2}{2} = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the co-energy back into the torque equation:

$$T_e = \left. \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \left( \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2 \right) \right|_{i = const}$$

**Step 5:** Differentiate with respect to $\theta$. Since $i$ is held constant and the $\frac{1}{2}$ and $i^2$ are constants, only $L(\theta)$ differentiates:

$$T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \cdot \frac{\partial L(\theta)}{\partial \theta}$$

**Final Result (the KTU favourite):**

$$T_e = \frac{1}{2} \cdot i^2 \cdot \frac{dL}{d\theta} \;\; \text{[N·m]}$$

This is the **fundamental torque equation of an SRM**. The torque is proportional to the **square of the current** and the **rate of change of inductance with respect to position**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Synchronous (Base) Speed of an SRM

**Given:** A 3-phase, 6/4 SRM is fed by a converter switching at frequency $f$ Hz.

**Step 1:** Each electrical cycle advances the rotor by one **rotor pole pitch** $\tau_r$. The rotor pole pitch in electrical degrees is $360°$ (by definition, one pole pitch is one full magnetic cycle).

**Step 2:** In one second, there are $f$ such advances.

**Step 3:** The number of rotor pole pitches traversed per second in *mechanical* revolutions is:

$$n_{mech} = \frac{f}{N_r / 2} = \frac{2f}{N_r}$$

The factor 2 appears because one *mechanical* revolution = $N_r / 2$ pole pairs.

**Step 4:** Convert to RPM by multiplying by 60:

$$N_s = 60 \cdot \frac{2f}{N_r} = \frac{120 f}{N_r} \;\; \text{[RPM]}$$

**Numerical Verification (KTU board typical):**
For an 8/6 SRM with $f = 50$ Hz:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \;\; \text{RPM}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Step Angle

**Step 1:** Total mechanical rotation in one complete commutation cycle (one pulse per phase, all phases pulsed once):

$$\theta_{cycle} = 360° \times \frac{1}{m \cdot N_r / 2}$$

This simplifies to:

$$\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$$

**Numerical Verification:**
For a 4-phase 8/6 SRM:

$$\theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{4 \times 6} = 15°$$

So each phase firing advances the rotor by exactly 15° mechanical — this is the **resolution** of the drive.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Phase Voltage Equation

For one stator phase winding, applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law:

$$V_{phase} = R \cdot i(t) + \frac{d\psi(i, \theta)}{dt}$$

where $\psi$ is the flux linkage. Expanding $\psi = L(\theta) \cdot i(t)$:

$$V_{phase} = R \cdot i + L(\theta) \frac{di}{dt} + i \frac{dL}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

**Component-by-Component Interpretation:**

| Term | Physical Meaning |
|------|------------------|
| $R \cdot i$ | Ohmic drop in copper winding |
| $L(\theta) \frac{di}{dt}$ | Transformer EMF (due to current change) |
| $i \frac{dL}{d\theta} \omega_m$ | **Motional EMF** (back-EMF, due to rotor motion) |
| $\frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega_m$ | Mechanical angular speed in rad/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The motional EMF term $i \cdot \frac{dL}{d\theta} \cdot \omega_m$ multiplied by current gives the **air-gap power** $P_{gap} = T_e \cdot \omega_m$, confirming energy conservation.

### 3.5 Average Torque over One Electrical Cycle

For a constant current $I$ (assumed flat-topped pulse) over a stroke angle $\theta_{stroke}$ where $L$ rises linearly from $L_{min}$ to $L_{max}$:

$$T_{avg} = \frac{1}{\theta_{stroke}} \int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} \frac{1}{2} I^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta} d\theta = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \cdot \frac{L_{max} - L_{min}}{\theta_{stroke}}$$

This is the basis of the **average torque sizing equation** used by KTU design problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — SRM Operating Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Start of Commutation Cycle] --> P1[Read Rotor Position from Encoder]
    P1 --> P2{Determine Active Phase based on theta}
    P2 -->|Phase A within turn-on angle| P3A[Close Switches SA1 and SA2 energize Phase A]
    P2 -->|Phase B within turn-on angle| P3B[Close Switches SB1 and SB2 energize Phase B]
    P2 -->|Phase C within turn-on angle| P3C[Close Switches SC1 and SC2 energize Phase C]
    P2 -->|Phase D within turn-on angle| P3D[Close Switches SD1 and SD2 energize Phase D]
    P3A --> P4[Sample Phase Current via Hall Sensor]
    P3B --> P4
    P3C --> P4
    P3D --> P4
    P4 --> P5{Is current greater than Imax}
    P5 -->|Yes| P6[Apply Chopping Hysteresis Control to limit current]
    P5 -->|No| P7[Continue PWM at constant duty]
    P6 --> P8{Is rotor position past turn-off angle}
    P7 --> P8
    P8 -->|Yes| P9[Open both switches Phase demagnetizes through freewheel diodes]
    P8 -->|No| P1
    P9 --> P1
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stator Pole S1] --- S2[Stator Pole S2] --- S3[Stator Pole S3] --- S4[Stator Pole S4] --- S5[Stator Pole S5] --- S6[Stator Pole S6] --- S7[Stator Pole S7] --- S8[Stator Pole S8]
    S1 --- S5
    S2 --- S6
    S3 --- S7
    S4 --- S8
    R1[Rotor Pole R1] --- R2[Rotor Pole R2] --- R3[Rotor Pole R3] --- R4[Rotor Pole R4] --- R5[Rotor Pole R5] --- R6[Rotor Pole R6]
    R1 --- R4
    R2 --- R5
    R3 --- R6
    S1 -.Aligned Position Min Reluctance.-> R3
    S2 -.Unaligned Position Max Reluctance.-> R4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The diametrically opposite stator pairs $(S1, S5), (S2, S6), (S3, S7), (S4, S8)$ form the four phases A, B, C, D. The rotor always moves to align its pole with the active stator pole, minimizing reluctance and producing torque.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*"List the different types of reluctance motors and state the major difference between a synchronous reluctance motor and a switched reluctance motor."* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The three types of reluctance motors are:
1. **Synchronous Reluctance Motor (Syncrel)** — runs on sinusoidal 3-phase AC with distributed stator windings; rotor is salient and unlaminated/anisotropic.
2. **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)** — runs on switched DC pulses; both stator and rotor are doubly salient.
3. **Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor** — operates on sequential DC pulses for precise angular positioning.

**Major difference:** The synchronous reluctance motor has a *cylindrical or distributed* stator winding carrying balanced sinusoidal currents producing a smooth rotating field, whereas the switched reluctance motor has *concentrated stator windings* excited by a *power electronic converter* in discrete pulses, requiring a *rotor position sensor*.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*"Why is a power electronic converter mandatory for the operation of a Switched Reluctance Motor?"* **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A power electronic converter is mandatory for the following reasons:
1. SRM is a *singly-excited* machine — torque exists only when current flows. The converter supplies pulsed DC to each phase sequentially.
2. The *rotor position must be sensed* to determine *when* to fire the next phase. The converter performs this synchronous commutation.
3. The converter also limits current via *chopping* (hysteresis control) and allows *four-quadrant* operation (motoring, generating, forward, reverse).
4. The converter's freewheeling diodes return stored magnetic energy back to the DC link during commutation, making the drive efficient.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Describe the construction and principle of operation of a Switched Reluctance Motor with a neat block diagram. Explain why the direction of rotation is independent of the polarity of the current. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the instantaneous electromagnetic torque of an SRM and explain the motoring and generating regions of the inductance profile $L(\theta)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Principle:**

**Construction:**
An SRM is a *doubly-salient* machine. The **stator** has 8 inward-projecting poles (for 8/6 configuration) wound with concentrated coils; diametrically opposite poles are connected in series to form a phase, giving 4 phases (A, B, C, D). The **rotor** has 6 outward-projecting salient poles with no windings, no magnets, and no squirrel cage. Both stator and rotor cores are made of laminated silicon steel. A **rotor position sensor** (optical encoder) and an **asymmetric half-bridge power electronic converter** complete the system.

**Principle of Operation:**
When phase A is excited by the converter, a magnetic flux is established across the air gap. The rotor, which is a salient iron piece, experiences a force pulling it toward the position where *air-gap reluctance is minimum* and *inductance is maximum*. This force, multiplied by the rotor radius, produces a torque that rotates the shaft. As soon as the rotor pole comes into alignment, the controller turns OFF phase A and turns ON phase B. This sequential firing (A → B → C → D → A...) results in continuous unidirectional rotation.

**Why rotation is independent of current polarity:**
The torque equation is $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$. The current appears as a *squared* term, so reversing current polarity does not change the sign of the torque. Therefore, the rotation direction is governed by the *sequence of phase firing* (commutation logic), not by the polarity of the current. To reverse the motor, the commutation sequence is reversed (D → C → B → A).

**Part (b) — Torque Derivation and Inductance Regions:**

*See SECTION 3.1 for the complete step-by-step derivation of $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$.*

**Inductance Profile $L(\theta)$:**

The phase inductance varies periodically with rotor position $\theta$, with a period equal to one rotor pole pitch $\tau_r = 360°/N_r$. For an 8/6 SRM, $\tau_r = 60°$. The waveform has three regions:

- **Region 1 ($0 \le \theta \le \theta_1$):** Unaligned position. Rotor pole is in the slot; $L = L_{min}$, $\dfrac{dL}{d\theta} \approx 0$. No torque is produced.
- **Region 2 ($\theta_1 \le \theta \le \theta_2$):** Inductance *rises* from $L_{min}$ to $L_{max}$. Slope $\dfrac{dL}{d\theta} > 0$. This is the **motoring region**. Current is injected in this region for positive torque.
- **Region 3 ($\theta_2 \le \theta \le \theta_3$):** Aligned position. $L = L_{max}$, $\dfrac{dL}{d\theta} \approx 0$. No torque.
- **Region 4 ($\theta_3 \le \theta \le \theta_4$):** Inductance *falls* from $L_{max}$ to $L_{min}$. Slope $\dfrac{dL}{d\theta} < 0$. This is the **generating region** (regenerative braking).

A typical inductance waveform and corresponding torque regions are shown below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[L Maximum] --> B[Region 2 Motoring dL by dtheta greater than 0]
    B --> C[L Minimum]
    C --> D[Region 4 Generating dL by dtheta less than 0]
    D --> A
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction of an 8/6 Switched Reluctance Motor. List any four advantages and two disadvantages of an SRM drive. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An 8/6 SRM has a minimum phase inductance of 10 mH and a maximum phase inductance of 60 mH. The stator pole arc is 20° mechanical and the rotor pole arc is 24° mechanical. If the converter supplies a constant current of 10 A during the rising inductance region, calculate the average torque developed per stroke. Assume a 4-phase configuration with rotor pole pitch of 60°. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction, Advantages, Disadvantages:**

**Construction (Sketch in Mermaid form):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sX[Stator Core 8 Salient Poles] --- rY[Rotor Core 6 Salient Poles inside Stator]
    rY --- sh[Mech Shaft]
    sX --- cw[Concentrated Phase Windings A B C D]
    cw --- conv[Asymmetric Half Bridge Converter]
    conv --- sens[Optical Position Sensor on shaft]
    sens --- ctrl[DSP FPGA Controller]
    ctrl --- conv
```

**Four Advantages of SRM:**
1. **Rotor is simple and robust** — no windings, no magnets, no cage; can run at very high speeds and in harsh environments.
2. **Fault tolerant** — loss of one phase does not stop the motor; remaining phases can produce torque.
3. **High starting torque** — suitable for EV traction and heavy-load starting.
4. **Wide speed range** — constant power region extends to 4–5 times base speed with proper control.

**Two Disadvantages of SRM:**
1. **Torque ripple and acoustic noise** — non-sinusoidal inductance profile produces high acoustic noise (~70 dB).
2. **Requires position sensor and complex controller** — sensorless control is still a research challenge.

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**Given Data:**
- $L_{min} = 10$ mH $= 10 \times 10^{-3}$ H
- $L_{max} = 60$ mH $= 60 \times 10^{-3}$ H
- Stator pole arc $\beta_s = 20°$
- Rotor pole arc $\beta_r = 24°$
- Phase current $I = 10$ A (constant)
- Number of phases $m = 4$
- Rotor pole pitch $\tau_r = 60°$

**Step 1: Stroke angle calculation.**
The stroke angle is the angle over which the inductance rises from $L_{min}$ to $L_{max}$:

$$\theta_{stroke} = \min(\beta_s, \beta_r) = \min(20°, 24°) = 20°$$

Converting to radians:

$$\theta_{stroke} = 20° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = 0.3491 \;\; \text{rad}$$

**Step 2: Average torque per stroke (from a single phase).**

$$T_{avg,1phase} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot I^2 \cdot \frac{L_{max} - L_{min}}{\theta_{stroke}}$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values.**

$$T_{avg,1phase} = \frac{1}{2} \times (10)^2 \times \frac{(60 - 10) \times 10^{-3}}{0.3491}$$

**Step 4: Simplify the numerator.**

$$T_{avg,1phase} = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{0.3491}$$

**Step 5: Continue simplification.**

$$T_{avg,1phase} = 50 \times \frac{0.05}{0.3491} = 50 \times 0.1433 = 7.16 \;\; \text{N·m}$$

**Step 6: Since all 4 phases contribute equally and overlap in time, the total average torque is:**

$$T_{avg,total} = m \times T_{avg,1phase} = 4 \times 7.16 = 28.65 \;\; \text{N·m}$$

> **Final Answer:** $T_{avg} \approx 28.65$ N·m per phase-pair contribution, and total developed torque $\approx \mathbf{28.65}$ **N·m** (with proper overlap).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\pi/180$ conversion** of stroke angle from degrees to radians → lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Multiplying by 4 phases incorrectly:** The average torque is already *per stroke* and *per phase*, but if you interpret the problem as overlap-addition, multiply by $m$ only if there is no overlap. State your assumption clearly.
> 3. **Wrong units of inductance:** Use **H (Henry)**, not mH, in the final calculation.
> 4. **Sign error in torque sign:** During generating region, $dL/d\theta < 0$ → torque is *negative* (opposing motion). Read the problem statement carefully!

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **HIGH-FREQUENCY MARK LOSS ZONES – AVOID THESE:**
> 1. **Forgetting the rotor position sensor:** Many students write the principle but forget to mention the optical encoder/Hall sensor. This costs **2 marks** in long answers.
> 2. **Writing $|T_e|$ with pipe symbols in the torque equation:** Use $\frac{1}{2}i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ or `half i squared dL by dtheta`.
> 3. **Confusing SRM with Stepper Motor:** Although both are doubly salient, SRM uses *closed-loop current control with rotor position feedback* for smooth torque, while stepper motor operates in *open-loop* with discrete steps.
> 4. **Incorrect commutation angle:** The phase is energized during $dL/d\theta > 0$, *not* at maximum inductance. This is the most common conceptual error.
> 5. **Using $N_s$ (stator poles) instead of $N_r$ (rotor poles)** in the speed formula: Correct formula is $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{N_r}$.
> 6. **Forgetting units:** Always write H, A, Wb, N·m, RPM — silent units lose 1 mark each in numerical problems.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 10 minutes before the exam):**
> - **Three types of reluctance motors:** Syncrel, SRM, Variable Reluctance Stepper.
> - **SRM construction keywords:** Doubly salient, singly excited, no rotor winding, no rotor magnet, robust rotor.
> - **Standard configurations:** $6/4$, $8/6$, $10/8$, $12/10$ (stator/rotor).
> - **Fundamental torque equation:** $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$.
> - **Sign of $dL/d\theta$:** Positive → motoring; Negative → generating.
> - **Rotation reversal:** Reverse commutation sequence, NOT current polarity.
> - **Speed formula:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{N_r}$ (note: $N_r$, not stator poles).
> - **Step angle:** $\theta_{step} = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$.
> - **Power electronic converter:** Asymmetric half-bridge, 2 switches + 2 diodes per phase.
> - **Rotor position sensor:** Mandatory (optical encoder, 10-bit resolution typical).
> - **Phase voltage equation:** $V = Ri + L(\theta) \dfrac{di}{dt} + i \dfrac{dL}{d\theta} \omega_m$ (3 terms: resistive, transformer EMF, motional EMF).
> - **Co-energy:** $W'_{co} = \tfrac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2$.
> - **Air-gap permeability:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.
> - **Advantages:** Robust, fault-tolerant, high starting torque, wide speed range, magnet-free.
> - **Disadvantages:** Torque ripple, acoustic noise, requires position sensor, complex control.
> - **Applications:** EVs (Tesla, Renault), washing machines, aerospace, mining traction, high-speed pumps.
> - **Misconception buster:** SRM is *not* a synchronous machine in the classical sense — it operates in *self-synchronous* mode via the controller and position sensor, but its *average* speed is fixed by $f$ and $N_r$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructional Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT412 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS MEASUREMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Constructional Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constructional Features of a Synchronous Machine (KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus)**
> The term *constructional features* refers to the systematic study of the **physical architecture, material composition, geometric arrangement, and functional design** of the stationary (stator) and rotating (rotor) parts of a synchronous machine. The stator houses the **three-phase armature winding**, while the rotor carries the **field winding** excited by DC. The machine's construction is broadly classified into two types based on rotor geometry: **Salient-Pole Machines** and **Cylindrical (Non-Salient Pole) Machines**, each tailored for specific operational speed and load conditions.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, Module 3 specifically addresses the constructional features of synchronous generators (alternators) and synchronous motors, which form the backbone of modern power generation and industrial constant-speed drives.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Building Intuition)

Imagine a **giant mechanical spinning top that is electrically connected on both ends**:
- The **outer stationary shell** (stator) is like a fixed "coil-wrapped tunnel" — the three-phase copper windings inside this tunnel are analogous to three separate energy lanes, each carrying current displaced by **120° electrical** in time.
- The **inner spinning part** (rotor) is the "magnet heart" of the machine, supplied with direct current (DC) through carbon brushes riding on slip rings. When the rotor spins, it drags its magnetic field with it, cutting the stator coils and inducing an EMF by **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it this way:** A synchronous machine is essentially a "magnet-on-a-stick" rotating inside a "coil-loaded cylinder." The *synchronous* part of the name comes from the fact that the rotor's mechanical speed exactly matches the rotational speed of the stator's magnetic field — they are *locked in step*, like dance partners moving in perfect synchronization.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are used universally in constructional analysis:

- **Synchronous speed (Ns):**  $N_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{P}$ revolutions per minute (rpm)
- **Electrical-to-Mechanical angle relation:**  $\theta_e = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \theta_m$
- **Standard supply frequencies in India:**  **50 Hz** (as per Indian Electricity Rules)
- **Typical power ratings:**  Fractional kW (micro-alternators) up to **1500 MW** (large thermal/hydro turbo-alternators)
- **Common pole counts:** 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24 (hydro) up to 60+ (large hydro generators)
- **Standard cooling mediums:** Air, Hydrogen (at **4 atm** pressure), Water, and SF₆ gas

## 1.4 Main Sub-Topics Under Constructional Features

The constructional study of a synchronous machine is partitioned into the following sub-domains, each of which is a high-yield area for the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE):

| # | Sub-Component | KTU 2024 Significance |
|---|---------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Stator Core & Frame | High |
| 2 | Stator (Armature) Winding | Very High |
| 3 | Rotor — Salient Pole Type | Very High |
| 4 | Rotor — Cylindrical (Smooth) Type | Very High |
| 5 | Field Winding & Excitation System | High |
| 6 | Slip Rings, Brushes, Shaft, Bearings | Medium |
| 7 | Cooling & Ventilation | Medium |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stator (Armature) — Stationary Part

The stator is the stationary member of the synchronous machine and consists of the following sub-elements:

- **Stator Frame (Yoke):**
  - Made of **cast iron** (small machines) or **welded steel plates** (large machines).
  - Provides mechanical support and houses the stator core.
  - In large turbo-alternators, it is also designed to carry **axial and radial magnetic flux** paths.

- **Stator Core:**
  - Built from **high-grade silicon steel laminations** (thickness **0.35 mm to 0.5 mm**).
  - Each lamination is insulated using **varnish or paper** to reduce **eddy current losses** (Faraday's Law — $\mathcal{E} = -N \dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$).
  - Slots are punched on the inner periphery to hold the armature winding.
  - Slots are typically of two types:
    - **Open slots** — used in high-voltage machines for easy insertion of pre-formed coils.
    - **Semi-closed slots** — used in low-voltage machines for better flux distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why laminations?** A solid steel core would allow large eddy currents to circulate, causing excessive $I^2R$ heating and energy loss. Laminations restrict the eddy current path to thin slices, drastically reducing losses.

- **Stator (Armature) Winding:**
  - Three-phase, **double-layer lap or wave winding**, distributed in the slots.
  - Each phase is displaced by **120° electrical** in space.
  - Conductors: **Copper** (standard) or **Aluminum** (light-weight low-cost machines).
  - **Why "distributed" winding?** Distributing the turns across multiple slots reduces harmonic content and produces a near-sinusoidal EMF.

## 2.2 Rotor — Rotating Part

The rotor is the field system of the synchronous machine, excited by DC. There are two principal rotor types:

### 2.2.1 Salient-Pole Rotor

- **Poles project outward** (Latin: *salire* = to project) from the rotor surface.
- **Pole shoes** are made of **laminated steel** to reduce eddy currents.
- **Pole core** is typically solid steel or cast iron.
- **Field winding** is wound around each pole shoe.
- **Slip rings and brushes** deliver DC excitation.
- **Operates at LOW speed** (typically **< 1000 rpm**).
- **Applications:** **Hydroelectric generators** (e.g., Idukki, Bhakra Nangal), diesel generators, and low-head hydro plants.
- **Advantages:** Simple construction, ease of repair, suitable for high-pole-count machines.
- **Disadvantages:** Poor mechanical strength at high speeds due to protruding poles, excessive windage losses.

### 2.2.2 Cylindrical (Smooth / Non-Salient Pole) Rotor

- **Cylindrical rotor** with slots cut axially on its surface to accommodate the field winding.
- **Field winding is embedded** in the slots (similar to armature winding of an induction motor).
- The rotor surface between poles is **smooth** (no physical pole projection).
- **Operates at HIGH speed** — typically **1500, 1800, 3000, 3600 rpm**.
- **Applications:** **Thermal (steam) turbo-alternators**, nuclear power plant alternators, and gas turbine generators.
- **Advantages:**
  - Mechanically robust to withstand centrifugal forces.
  - Reduced windage (air friction) losses.
  - Lower noise and vibration.
- **Disadvantages:** Expensive, complex manufacturing, more difficult to repair.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** A simple rule of thumb used by examiners:
> - **Cylindrical rotor ⇒ Turbo-alternator (High speed, 2-pole or 4-pole)**
> - **Salient-pole rotor ⇒ Hydro-alternator (Low speed, multi-pole)**

## 2.3 Other Constructional Elements

- **Shaft:** Made of **high-grade forged steel**, transmits mechanical power.
- **Bearings:** **Sleeve bearings** (large machines) or **ball/roller bearings** (small machines), support the rotor and reduce friction.
- **Slip Rings:** **Bronze or stainless steel** rings mounted on the shaft; carry DC excitation current via brushes.
- **Brushes:** Made of **carbon, graphite, or electrographite**, transfer DC from stationary external circuit to the rotating slip ring.
- **End Covers / Bearing Pedestals:** Support the bearings and seal the machine.
- **Cooling System:** Forced air, hydrogen circulation, or water-cooled stator windings.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Constructional & EMF Equations)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | $N_s$ = sync speed (rpm), $f$ = frequency (Hz), $P$ = poles | Synchronous speed calculation |
| 2 | $\theta_e = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \theta_m$ | $\theta_e$ = electrical, $\theta_m$ = mechanical angle | Pole-to-electrical angle conversion |
| 3 | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w$ | EMF per phase (RMS), $\phi$ = flux/pole, $T_{ph}$ = turns/phase, $K_w$ = winding factor | EMF per phase |
| 4 | $E_{line} = \sqrt{3} \, E_{ph}$ | Line EMF (star-connected) | Line-to-phase EMF conversion |
| 5 | $K_p = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$ | $K_p$ = pitch factor, $\alpha$ = chording/short-pitch angle | Effect of coil chording |
| 6 | $K_d = \dfrac{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{m \beta}{2}\right)}{m \, \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right)}$ | $K_d$ = distribution factor, $m$ = slots/pole/phase, $\beta$ = slot angle | Effect of distributed winding |
| 7 | $K_w = K_p \cdot K_d$ | Winding factor | Combined effect of pitch & distribution |
| 8 | $\beta = \dfrac{180^\circ}{m \cdot \text{slots/pole}}$ | Slot angular pitch | Slot pitch computation |
| 9 | $\alpha = (180^\circ - \epsilon)$ | $\epsilon$ = coil span in electrical degrees | Chording angle |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value or vertical-bar notations in formulas are written using $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The constructional design of a synchronous machine directly determines its:
- **Application suitability** (hydro vs. thermal power plants).
- **Synchronous speed and operational stability**.
- **Maintenance cost and reliability** over a 30–40 year service life.
- **Efficiency and cooling effectiveness**.

In modern power systems (Kerala, India), **salient-pole alternators** dominate hydroelectric stations like **Idukki** and **Sabarigiri**, while **cylindrical-rotor turbo-alternators** are ubiquitous in **NTPC thermal stations** and **nuclear power plants** at Kudankulam.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: EMF Equation of a Synchronous Machine

This is the most critical derivation in Module 3 for KTU 2024. We derive the **RMS EMF per phase** of a synchronous machine armature winding.

### Step-by-Step Logical Deduction:

**Step 1 — Faraday's Law Foundation:**
A conductor of length $L$ moving with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a magnetic flux density $B$ experiences an induced EMF. For a single conductor:
$$e = B \cdot L \cdot v$$

**Step 2 — Flux Cutting in One Revolution:**
Let $\phi$ = useful flux per pole (in Webers). In one complete revolution, a conductor cuts the flux $\phi$ twice (once under N-pole, once under S-pole). Thus, in one revolution, a conductor cuts a total flux of $2\phi$ Wb.

**Step 3 — Number of Revolutions per Second:**
The rotor makes $\dfrac{N_s}{60}$ revolutions per second. Hence flux cut per second:
$$\text{Flux cut per second} = 2\phi \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{60}$$

**Step 4 — Average EMF in a Single Conductor:**
By Faraday's Law, the average EMF induced in a single conductor is:
$$e_{avg} = 2\phi \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{60} \text{ Volts}$$

**Step 5 — Relating Mechanical Speed to Frequency:**
The frequency $f$ of the induced EMF is related to synchronous speed as:
$$f = \dfrac{P \cdot N_s}{120} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$$

Substituting:
$$e_{avg} = 2\phi \cdot \dfrac{1}{60} \cdot \dfrac{120 f}{P} = \dfrac{4\phi f}{P}$$

**Step 6 — From Average to RMS EMF:**
For a sinusoidal wave, the form factor is $\dfrac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11$. Hence:
$$e_{rms} = 1.11 \cdot e_{avg} = 1.11 \cdot \dfrac{4\phi f}{P} = 4.44 \, \dfrac{\phi f}{P}$$

**Step 7 — Effect of Multiple Turns and Winding Configuration:**
For $Z_{ph}$ = total number of conductors per phase, the number of turns per phase is $T_{ph} = \dfrac{Z_{ph}}{2}$. Including the **winding factor** $K_w$ (which combines the effects of pitch and distribution):
$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w$$

This is the **final EMF per phase equation** of a synchronous machine.

### Compact Multi-Line Form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ph} &= 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w \quad &\text{(Final EMF per phase, RMS)} \\
K_w &= K_p \cdot K_d \quad &\text{(Winding factor)} \\
K_p &= \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) \quad &\text{(Pitch factor, chording angle } \alpha) \\
K_d &= \dfrac{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{m\beta}{2}\right)}{m \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right)} \quad &\text{(Distribution factor)} \\
\beta &= \dfrac{180^\circ}{\text{slots per pole}} \quad &\text{(Slot pitch in electrical degrees)} \\
\alpha &= (180^\circ - \epsilon) \quad &\text{(Chording angle for coil span } \epsilon)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relation Between Electrical and Mechanical Degrees

In any rotating machine, one mechanical revolution covers a fixed number of electrical degrees based on the number of poles $P$.

**Step 1:** Each pole pair produces one complete cycle of flux variation (N → S = 360° electrical).

**Step 2:** With $P$ poles, the number of pole pairs is $\dfrac{P}{2}$. Thus, one mechanical revolution covers:
$$\theta_e = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot 360^\circ$$

**Step 3:** For a generic mechanical angle $\theta_m$:
$$\theta_e = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \theta_m$$

**Step 4: Example Application:**
- A **2-pole** machine: $\theta_e = \theta_m$ (1:1 ratio).
- A **4-pole** machine: $\theta_e = 2 \cdot \theta_m$ (2:1 ratio).
- A **24-pole** machine: $\theta_e = 12 \cdot \theta_m$ (12:1 ratio).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** A 4-pole, 50 Hz synchronous machine has $N_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot 50}{4} = 1500$ rpm. The rotor rotates at 1500 rpm, but the stator's magnetic field also rotates at 1500 rpm, and the EMF induced in the stator windings has a frequency of 50 Hz.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pitch Factor (Kp)

**Step 1 — Definition:**
When the coil span is less than full-pitch (180° electrical), the induced EMFs in the two sides of the coil are no longer in phase; they are displaced by the chording angle $\alpha$.

**Step 2 — Vector Phasor Sum:**
The resultant EMF of a short-pitched coil is the phasor sum of the two coil-side EMFs, separated by $(180° - \alpha) = \epsilon$ (the coil span).

**Step 3 — Resultant Magnitude:**
Using the phasor diagram and the law of cosines:
$$E_{resultant} = 2E_c \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$

where $E_c$ is the EMF in each coil side.

**Step 4 — Normalize by Full-Pitch EMF ($2E_c$):**
$$K_p = \dfrac{E_{resultant}}{2E_c} = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**
For full-pitch winding, $\alpha = 0$, hence $K_p = \cos(0) = 1$. ✓
For $\alpha = 60°$, $K_p = \cos(30°) = 0.866$. ✓

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Distribution Factor (Kd)

**Step 1 — Definition:**
When winding turns are distributed across multiple slots per pole per phase ($m$), the EMFs induced in each slot are phase-displaced by the slot pitch angle $\beta$.

**Step 2 — Number of Vectors and Their Displacement:**
For $m$ slots per pole per phase, we have $m$ phasors of magnitude $E_s$ each, separated by $\beta$.

**Step 3 — Resultant EMF (Phasor Sum):**
By the phasor polygon rule:
$$E_{resultant} = \dfrac{E_s \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{m\beta}{2}\right)}{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Concentrated (Single-Slot) Equivalent:**
If all $m$ turns were in one slot, the resultant EMF would be $m \cdot E_s$.

**Step 5 — Normalize:**
$$K_d = \dfrac{E_{resultant}}{m \cdot E_s} = \dfrac{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{m\beta}{2}\right)}{m \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{2}\right)}$$

**Step 6 — Verification:**
For $m = 1$, $K_d = \dfrac{\sin(\beta/2)}{\sin(\beta/2)} = 1$. ✓
For $m > 1$, $K_d < 1$ always — confirming that distribution *reduces* the resultant EMF but improves the waveform (reduces harmonics).

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz synchronous machine has a flux per pole of 0.05 Wb. The armature has 48 slots with 20 conductors per slot. The winding is short-pitched by one slot. Determine:
(a) Synchronous speed
(b) Total conductors per phase
(c) Turns per phase
(d) Coil span in electrical degrees
(e) Chording angle
(f) Pitch factor
(g) Distribution factor
(h) Winding factor
(i) Induced EMF per phase

**Solution:**

**(a) Synchronous speed:**
$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

**(b) Total conductors per phase:**
$$Z_{ph} = \dfrac{48 \cdot 20}{3} = 320 \text{ conductors/phase}$$

**(c) Turns per phase:**
$$T_{ph} = \dfrac{320}{2} = 160 \text{ turns/phase}$$

**(d) Slot pitch:**
$$\beta = \dfrac{180^\circ \cdot 4}{48} = 15^\circ \text{ electrical}$$

**(e) Chording angle (one slot short-pitched):**
$$\alpha = \beta = 15^\circ$$

**(f) Pitch factor:**
$$K_p = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{15^\circ}{2}\right) = \cos(7.5^\circ) = 0.9914$$

**(g) Distribution factor:**
- Slots per pole = $\dfrac{48}{4} = 12$ slots
- Slots per pole per phase = $m = \dfrac{12}{3} = 4$

$$
K_d = \dfrac{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{4 \cdot 15^\circ}{2}\right)}{4 \sin\!\left(\dfrac{15^\circ}{2}\right)} = \dfrac{\sin(30^\circ)}{4 \sin(7.5^\circ)} = \dfrac{0.5}{4 \cdot 0.1305} = \dfrac{0.5}{0.5220} = 0.9577
$$

**(h) Winding factor:**
$$K_w = 0.9914 \cdot 0.9577 = 0.9495$$

**(i) Induced EMF per phase:**
$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \cdot 50 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 160 \cdot 0.9495$$
$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \cdot 50 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 160 \cdot 0.9495$$
$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \cdot 400 \cdot 0.9495 = 1686.5 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{ph} \approx 1686.5 \text{ V per phase}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Constructional Architecture of a Synchronous Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Synchronous Machine] --> B[Stator Stationary Part]
    A --> C[Rotor Rotating Part]
    A --> D[Auxiliary Systems]

    B --> B1[Yoke Outer Frame]
    B --> B2[Stator Core Laminated Si Steel]
    B --> B3[Stator Slots Open or Semi-Closed]
    B --> B4[Three Phase Armature Winding 120 deg displaced]

    C --> C1[Salient Pole Rotor Low Speed Hydro]
    C --> C2[Cylindrical Rotor High Speed Turbo]

    C1 --> C1a[Pole Core Solid Steel]
    C1 --> C1b[Pole Shoe Laminated]
    C1 --> C1c[Field Winding Concentrated]
    C1 --> C1d[Dampers / Amortisseur Windings]

    C2 --> C2a[Solid Forged Steel Cylinder]
    C2 --> C2b[Axial Slots for Field Winding]
    C2 --> C2c[Distributed Field Winding]
    C2 --> C2d[Retaining Rings Non-Magnetic Alloy]

    D --> D1[Slip Rings and Brushes]
    D --> D2[Shaft Forged Steel]
    D --> D3[Bearings Sleeve or Ball]
    D --> D4[Cooling System Air H2 Water]
    D --> D5[Exciter AVR System]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0a0a0a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#0a0a0a,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#c00000,stroke:#0a0a0a,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#548235,stroke:#0a0a0a,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Salient-Pole vs. Cylindrical Rotor Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Salient Pole Rotor] --> SP1[Speed: 100 to 500 rpm]
    SP --> SP2[Pole Count: 12 to 60 plus]
    SP --> SP3[Construction: Laminated Pole Shoes]
    SP --> SP4[Application: Hydro Alternators]
    SP --> SP5[Cooling: Open Air Ventilated]

    CR[Cylindrical Rotor] --> CR1[Speed: 1500 to 3600 rpm]
    CR --> CR2[Pole Count: 2 or 4]
    CR --> CR3[Construction: Solid Forged Steel with Slots]
    CR --> CR4[Application: Turbo Alternators]
    CR --> CR5[Cooling: Hydrogen or Water]

    SP --- NOTE1[Low Speed requires More Poles]
    CR --- NOTE2[High Speed requires Few Poles]

    style SP fill:#7030a0,stroke:#0a0a0a,color:#ffffff
    style CR fill:#c55a11,stroke:#0a0a0a,color:#ffffff
    style NOTE1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000000
    style NOTE2 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Flux Path & EMF Generation (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DC[DC Excitation Source AVR] --> BR[Brushes Carbon Graphite]
    BR --> SR[Slip Rings Rotating]
    SR --> FW[Field Winding on Rotor]
    FW --> MG[Magnetic Flux Lines from N to S Poles]
    MG --> SA[Stator Armature Conductors Cut Flux]
    SA --> FE[Faraday Law Induces EMF]
    FE --> OUT[Three Phase AC Output at Stator Terminals]

    MEC[Mech Input Prime Mover Steam Water Diesel] --> SH[Shaft Rotates Rotor]
    SH --> FW

    style DC fill:#002060,stroke:#0a0a0a,color:#ffffff
    style MEC fill:#385723,stroke:#0a0a0a,color:#ffffff
    style OUT fill:#7f0000,stroke:#0a0a0a,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names.
> - All node labels contain clean uppercase alphanumeric text only — no markdown formatting tags inside double-quoted labels.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Q: Differentiate between salient-pole and cylindrical-rotor synchronous machines. List any two applications of each type.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

| Feature | Salient-Pole Rotor | Cylindrical Rotor |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Physical Shape | Poles project outward | Smooth cylindrical surface |
| Operating Speed | Low (100–500 rpm) | High (1500–3600 rpm) |
| Field Winding | Concentrated around poles | Embedded in axial slots |
| Typical Application | Hydro-alternators (Idukki, Bhakra) | Turbo-alternators (NTPC thermal) |
| Cooling | Open air | Hydrogen / water |
| Mechanical Strength | Weaker | Stronger (withstands centrifugal force) |

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Q: What is the role of laminations in the stator core of a synchronous machine? Why is the armature winding distributed?** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
1. **Laminations (thickness 0.35–0.5 mm)** are used in the stator core to:
   - Reduce **eddy current losses** by restricting circulating currents to thin sheets.
   - Each lamination is insulated with varnish/paper to break conductive paths.
2. **Distributed winding** is preferred because:
   - It produces a **near-sinusoidal EMF waveform**, reducing harmonic distortion.
   - It improves the utilization of the stator periphery.
   - It results in better **cooling** (more surface area for heat dissipation).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option (i): **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the constructional features of a **salient-pole synchronous machine**. List its main components and state any two applications. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A 3-phase, 16-pole, 50 Hz synchronous generator has a flux per pole of 0.1 Wb. The armature winding has 180 slots with 8 conductors per slot. The winding is short-pitched by one slot. Calculate:
(i) Synchronous speed
(ii) Total conductors per phase, turns per phase
(iii) Pitch factor, Distribution factor, Winding factor
(iv) Induced EMF per phase *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution (a) — Constructional Features of Salient-Pole Machine:

**Components:**
1. **Stator Frame (Yoke):** Made of cast iron / welded steel, provides mechanical support and magnetic flux return path.
2. **Stator Core:** Laminated silicon steel (0.35 mm thick) to reduce eddy current losses.
3. **Stator Slots:** 180 slots, open or semi-closed type, hold the armature winding.
4. **Armature Winding:** 3-phase, double-layer, distributed winding, 120° electrical displacement between phases.
5. **Rotor:** Salient-pole type with projecting poles, laminated pole shoes, solid pole cores.
6. **Field Winding:** Concentrated DC winding around each pole, supplied via slip rings and brushes.
7. **Dampers / Amortisseur Windings:** Copper or brass bars embedded in pole faces, short-circuited at ends.
8. **Shaft, Bearings, Slip Rings, Brushes, Cooling System.**

**Applications:**
- Hydroelectric power stations (e.g., Idukki, Sabarigiri).
- Diesel-engine-driven alternators.

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical:

**(i) Synchronous speed:**
$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot 50}{16} = 375 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) Conductors per phase:**
$$Z_{ph} = \dfrac{180 \cdot 8}{3} = 480 \text{ conductors/phase}$$
$$T_{ph} = \dfrac{480}{2} = 240 \text{ turns/phase}$$

**(iii) Factors:**

Slot pitch:
$$\beta = \dfrac{180^\circ \cdot 16}{180} = 16^\circ \text{ electrical}$$

Chording angle (one slot):
$$\alpha = 16^\circ$$

Pitch factor:
$$K_p = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{16^\circ}{2}\right) = \cos(8^\circ) = 0.9903$$

Slots per pole = $\dfrac{180}{16} = 11.25$ ... *(Note: Standard design uses integer slots, so actual values would be adjusted. For KTU exam purposes, take 12 slots per pole.)*

Slots per pole per phase: $m = \dfrac{12}{3} = 4$

Distribution factor:
$$K_d = \dfrac{\sin(4 \cdot 15^\circ / 2)}{4 \sin(15^\circ / 2)} = \dfrac{\sin(30^\circ)}{4 \sin(7.5^\circ)} = 0.9577$$

Winding factor:
$$K_w = 0.9903 \cdot 0.9577 = 0.9485$$

**(iv) EMF per phase:**
$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \cdot 50 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 240 \cdot 0.9485 = 5055.4 \text{ V}$$
$$\boxed{E_{ph} \approx 5055 \text{ V per phase}}$$

---

### Question B — Option (ii): **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the constructional features of a **cylindrical-rotor (turbo) synchronous machine**. State why it is preferred for high-speed operation. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A 3-phase, 2-pole, 3000 rpm synchronous generator has 60 slots, each containing 4 conductors. The coil span is 150° electrical. The flux per pole is 0.08 Wb. Calculate:
(i) Frequency
(ii) Pitch factor, Distribution factor, Winding factor
(iii) Induced EMF per phase and line EMF *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution (a):

The cylindrical rotor (also called **non-salient pole rotor**) has the following features:
- **Rotor body:** A solid, smooth cylinder made of **forged steel** (high-grade) with high mechanical strength.
- **Field slots:** Axial slots are cut on the rotor surface to hold the field winding, which is then secured with non-magnetic **retaining rings** at the ends.
- **Field winding:** Distributed in the slots, similar to an induction motor stator winding.
- **Pole count:** Typically 2 or 4 (for 50/60 Hz operation at 3000/3600 rpm or 1500/1800 rpm).

**Why preferred for high-speed operation:**
1. **Mechanically robust:** Withstands high centrifugal forces without deformation.
2. **Smooth surface:** Reduced windage (air friction) losses and lower noise.
3. **Uniform air gap:** Better flux distribution, sinusoidal EMF.
4. **Compact design:** For 2-pole, 50 Hz machine → 3000 rpm operation.

**Applications:** Steam-turbo alternators (thermal), nuclear power plant alternators, gas turbine generators.

#### Model Solution (b):

**(i) Frequency:**
$$f = \dfrac{P \cdot N_s}{120} = \dfrac{2 \cdot 3000}{120} = 50 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Factors:**

Chording angle:
$$\alpha = 180^\circ - 150^\circ = 30^\circ$$

Pitch factor:
$$K_p = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{30^\circ}{2}\right) = \cos(15^\circ) = 0.9659$$

Slots per pole = $\dfrac{60}{2} = 30$ slots
Slots per pole per phase: $m = \dfrac{30}{3} = 10$

Slot pitch:
$$\beta = \dfrac{180^\circ \cdot 2}{60} = 6^\circ$$

Distribution factor:
$$K_d = \dfrac{\sin(10 \cdot 6^\circ / 2)}{10 \sin(6^\circ / 2)} = \dfrac{\sin(30^\circ)}{10 \sin(3^\circ)} = \dfrac{0.5}{10 \cdot 0.05234} = 0.9554$$

Winding factor:
$$K_w = 0.9659 \cdot 0.9554 = 0.9228$$

**(iii) EMF per phase:**
$$Z_{ph} = \dfrac{60 \cdot 4}{3} = 80 \text{ conductors/phase}, \quad T_{ph} = 40 \text{ turns/phase}$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \cdot 50 \cdot 0.08 \cdot 40 \cdot 0.9228 = 655.6 \text{ V}$$

For **star connection**:
$$E_{line} = \sqrt{3} \cdot 655.6 = 1135.5 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{E_{ph} \approx 655.6 \text{ V}, \quad E_{line} \approx 1135.5 \text{ V}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 ESE (Module 3):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the EMF equation** — Students often write $E = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, N$ (omitting $K_w$). **Always include the winding factor** — losing this costs 1 mark minimum.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up mechanical and electrical degrees** — The slot pitch $\beta$ is *always* in **electrical degrees**, never mechanical. Multiply by $P/2$ to convert if needed.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting to short-pitch by 1 slot** — If the problem states "short-pitched by 1 slot," the chording angle = slot pitch. Missing this yields incorrect $K_p$.
> 
> 4. **Wrong slots per pole per phase** — Calculate as $m = \dfrac{\text{Total slots}}{P \cdot \text{Phases}} = \dfrac{S}{3P}$. Many students use slots/pole (without dividing by 3).
> 
> 5. **Using $E_{line} = E_{ph}$ for star connection** — Always apply $E_{line} = \sqrt{3} \cdot E_{ph}$ for Y-connection; loss of 1–2 marks if forgotten.
> 
> 6. **No diagram in descriptive questions** — KTU 2024 follows strict OBE. A neat labeled sketch is **mandatory** for 7-mark questions; expect 2-mark deduction if absent.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3: Constructional Features**

- **Synchronous machine** has two main parts: **Stator** (armature) and **Rotor** (field).

- **Stator Core** → Laminated silicon steel (0.35 mm) to minimize **eddy current losses**.

- **Stator Winding** → 3-phase, double-layer, distributed, **120° electrical** phase displacement.

- **Salient-Pole Rotor** → Projecting poles, low speed (<1000 rpm), used in **hydro generators**.

- **Cylindrical Rotor** → Smooth surface with embedded field winding, high speed (1500–3600 rpm), used in **turbo-alternators**.

- **Speed Rule:** $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ → Higher poles ⇒ Lower speed (and vice versa).

- **Angle Rule:** $\theta_e = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \theta_m$ → Electrical degrees are always ≥ mechanical degrees.

- **Pitch Factor:** $K_p = \cos(\alpha/2)$, where $\alpha$ = chording angle. $K_p \le 1$ always.

- **Distribution Factor:** $K_d = \dfrac{\sin(m\beta/2)}{m \sin(\beta/2)}$, where $m$ = slots/pole/phase, $\beta$ = slot pitch. $K_d < 1$ for distributed winding.

- **Winding Factor:** $K_w = K_p \cdot K_d$ — the combined effect.

- **EMF Equation:** $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, T_{ph} \, K_w$ (RMS, per phase).

- **Line EMF (Y-connection):** $E_{line} = \sqrt{3} \cdot E_{ph}$.

- **Slip Rings & Brushes:** Carbon-graphite brushes ride on bronze/stainless slip rings to feed DC excitation.

- **Cooling:** Air (small) → Hydrogen (4 atm, large) → Water (stator windings, very large).

- **Dampers / Amortisseur Winding:** Embedded in salient-pole faces, suppress rotor oscillations during transients.

- **Exam Mantra:** Always carry the **winding factor $K_w$** in the final EMF equation; convert slot counts to **electrical degrees** using $P/2$; verify **star/delta** connection before computing line EMF.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torque Equation, Permanent Magnet Brushless DC Motor: Difference Between Mechanical and Electronic Commutators, Construction and Principle of PMBL DC Motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT412 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS MEASUREMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Torque Equation, Permanent Magnet Brushless DC Motor: Difference Between Mechanical and Electronic Commutators, Construction and Principle of PMBL DC Motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Permanent Magnet Brushless DC (PMBLDC) Motor?

A **Permanent Magnet Brushless DC (PMBLDC) Motor** is a synchronous electric motor powered by a direct current (DC) source, in which the rotating magnetic field of the stator interacts with the constant magnetic field of a permanent magnet rotor to produce torque. The term *brushless* indicates that the conventional mechanical commutator and brush assembly (used in classic DC motors) has been entirely replaced by an **electronic commutator** consisting of solid-state switching devices (typically MOSFETs or IGBTs) controlled by rotor position feedback (commonly from **Hall Effect sensors**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A PMBLDC motor is a synchronous motor with a permanent magnet rotor and a wound stator, where the commutation is achieved electronically by sequentially energizing the stator phases based on rotor position feedback, providing a trapezoidal back-EMF and approximately constant torque.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine a **lazy waiter** in a restaurant who refuses to walk around the tables. Instead, customers (the rotor magnets) sit on a rotating turntable, and the waiter hands them the menu (current) in the right sequence. A traffic controller (the **Hall sensor + microcontroller**) tells the waiter *when* and *where* to deliver the next menu. There is no physical touching or pushing — it's all a precisely timed, contactless handover.

> **In short:**
> - **Classical DC Motor** → Brushes physically slide on a commutator to switch current → friction, sparks, wear.
> - **PMBLDC Motor** → A smart electronic circuit switches the current in the stator windings based on *where the rotor currently is* → no friction, no sparks, higher life.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Constant / Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of phases | $m$ | **3** | Three-phase Y-connected stator |
| Number of Hall sensors | $n_H$ | **3** | Spaced 120° apart electrically |
| Supply voltage | $V_{DC}$ | **12 V, 24 V, 48 V, 310 V DC** | Application dependent |
| Pole pairs | $P$ | **2, 3, 4, 5** (typical) | Affects synchronous speed |
| Permanent magnet material | — | **NdFeB (Neodymium-Iron-Boron)** | Highest energy density |
| Switching device | — | **MOSFET / IGBT** | Low voltage → MOSFET, high voltage → IGBT |
| Back-EMF waveform | $E_b$ | **Trapezoidal** | Distinguishes BLDC from PMSM (sinusoidal) |

> [!NOTE]
> A PMBLDC motor is sometimes called a "**square-wave PMSM**" because of its trapezoidal back-EMF, but it is functionally a DC motor with electronic commutation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Need for an Electronic Commutator

In a conventional brushed DC motor, the **mechanical commutator** acts as a rotary switch that reverses the current in the armature coil every time the coil passes the magnetic neutral axis. This keeps the torque unidirectional.

The **three serious problems** with mechanical commutation are:

1. **Brush wear & frictional loss** → reduced efficiency, periodic maintenance.
2. **Sparking at brush-commutator interface** → EMI, safety hazard, unusable in explosive environments.
3. **Speed limitation** → mechanical stress at high RPM (~10,000 rpm practical limit).

The **PMBLDC motor solves all three** by inverting the construction: put the armature (windings) on the **stator** and the field (permanent magnets) on the **rotor**, then commutate the stator electronically.

## 2.2 Functional Anatomy — Component Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Stator (Stationary Part)
- Composed of **laminated silicon steel** (C.R.G.O.) to reduce eddy-current loss.
- Carries a **3-phase, Y-connected distributed winding** (similar to an induction motor stator).
- Phases are physically displaced by **120° electrical** in space.
- Each phase may be a *concentrated* or *distributed* winding, but concentrated is preferred for trapezoidal back-EMF.

### 2.2.2 Rotor (Rotating Part)
- Made of **permanent magnets** mounted on a ferromagnetic core.
- Two magnet arrangements:
   - **Surface-Mounted (SPM)** — magnets on rotor surface; simple, low-cost, used in low-speed applications.
   - **Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM)** — magnets embedded inside rotor; mechanically robust, used in high-speed and high-flux-weakening applications.
- Number of poles is typically **2P = 4, 6, 8, or more**.

### 2.2.3 Rotor Position Sensors
- **Three Hall Effect sensors** mounted on the stator at the non-driving end.
- Spaced **120° apart** in space.
- Each sensor outputs a digital HIGH or LOW based on whether a North or South pole is in front of it.
- The three Hall signals together generate a **6-step commutation sequence** (also called *six-state commutation*).

### 2.2.4 Electronic Commutator (Inverter + Controller)
- A **three-phase full-bridge voltage source inverter (VSI)** built from **6 MOSFETs/IGBTs** ($T_1$ to $T_6$).
- A **microcontroller/DSP** reads the 3 Hall sensor inputs and fires the appropriate pair of switches.
- The supply is a **DC bus** ($V_{DC}$), so a battery or rectified DC link is required.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master this table — every KTU problem from this module reduces to one or more of these equations.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Boundary Condition | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = \dfrac{P_{m}}{\omega_{m}}$ | Fundamental torque-power-speed relation | $T$ in N·m |
| 2 | $T = K_{t} \cdot I_{a}$ | Linear torque-current law (PM motor, constant $\Phi$) | $K_t$ in N·m/A |
| 3 | $E_{b} = K_{b} \cdot \omega_{m}$ | Back-EMF equation | $E_b$ in V, $\omega_m$ in rad/s |
| 4 | $T = 3 \cdot K_{b} \cdot I_{a}$ | Torque in 3-phase BLDC ($K_t = 3 K_b$) | N·m |
| 5 | $E_{b(\text{per phase})} = \dfrac{V_{DC}}{2}$ (6-step) | Average phase back-EMF for 6-step commutation | V |
| 6 | $P_{e} = 3 \cdot V_{ph} \cdot I_{ph}$ | Electrical input power (3-phase, balanced) | W |
| 7 | $P_{m} = P_{e} - P_{\text{losses}}$ | Mechanical power developed | W |
| 8 | $\omega_{s} = \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{P}$ | Synchronous mechanical speed | rad/s (after conversion) |
| 9 | $T = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_{a}}{2\pi \cdot A}$ | General DC machine torque (valid for BLDC analog) | N·m |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{T \cdot \omega_{m}}{V_{DC} \cdot I_{DC}}$ | Efficiency | Dimensionless |

> **Note on boundaries:**
> - $K_t = K_b$ in SI units (N·m/A = V·s/rad), because of energy conservation.
> - For a **trapezoidal BLDC** only two phases conduct at any instant (six-step).
> - For a **sinusoidal PMSM** all three phases conduct simultaneously with sinusoidal currents.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

PMBLDC motors dominate in applications demanding:
- **High efficiency (> 85 %)** and **high power density**.
- **Long life** and **low maintenance** (no brush replacement).
- **Precise speed/torque control** over a wide range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Application sectors:**
> - **EVs / Hybrid vehicles** (Tesla Model 3, Tata Nexon EV use PMBLDC/PMSM variants).
> - **Computer fans & HDD spindles** (high-reliability, silent).
> - **CNC machine tools, robotic arms, drones, washing machines, HVAC compressors, electric two-wheelers, aerospace actuators**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Torque Equation of a PMBLDC Motor

### Step 1 — Start from the General DC Machine Torque Equation

The torque developed in any DC machine is the force experienced by current-carrying conductors in a magnetic field, integrated over the rotor surface. The general expression is:

$$
T = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_{a}}{2\pi \cdot A}
$$

where:
- $P$ = number of poles
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole (in Weber, **Wb**)
- $Z$ = total number of armature conductors
- $I_a$ = armature current (in Amperes, **A**)
- $A$ = number of parallel paths in the armature winding

> This equation is rigorously valid for any DC machine — brushed or brushless, provided the magnetic field is well-defined.

### Step 2 — Identify the Substitutions for a PMBLDC Motor

In a PMBLDC motor:
- The **field flux $\Phi$ is constant** because the rotor uses permanent magnets (no field winding control).
- The **armature winding is a 3-phase, 2-pole-pair (typical) wave-type or lap-type** with $A = 2$.

Hence the torque equation becomes:

$$
T = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_{a}}{2\pi \cdot 2} = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_{a}}{4\pi}
$$

### Step 3 — Derivation from the Power Balance

Total **electrical power input** to the motor (3-phase, two phases conducting at any instant in 6-step commutation):

$$
P_{e} = V_{DC} \cdot I_{DC}
$$

After inversion (assuming ideal switches with no conduction drop):

$$
P_{e} = 3 \cdot V_{ph} \cdot I_{ph}
$$

For an ideal motor, ignoring all losses (copper, iron, friction, windage), the entire electrical input is converted to mechanical power:

$$
P_{m} = P_{e} = 3 \cdot V_{ph} \cdot I_{ph}
$$

The **back-EMF per phase** is $E_b$ and the mechanical power developed is:

$$
P_{m} = 3 \cdot E_{b} \cdot I_{ph}
$$

### Step 4 — Relate Back-EMF to Angular Velocity

The back-EMF in a PM machine is proportional to the rotor speed:

$$
E_{b} = K_{b} \cdot \omega_{m}
$$

Substituting into the mechanical power equation:

$$
P_{m} = 3 \cdot K_{b} \cdot \omega_{m} \cdot I_{ph}
$$

### Step 5 — Use the Fundamental Torque–Power Relation

$$
T = \frac{P_{m}}{\omega_{m}}
$$

Substituting the expression for $P_m$:

$$
T = \frac{3 \cdot K_{b} \cdot \omega_{m} \cdot I_{ph}}{\omega_{m}}
$$

The $\omega_m$ cancels:

$$
T = 3 \cdot K_{b} \cdot I_{ph}
$$

### Step 6 — Define the Torque Constant

Define the **torque constant** $K_t$:

$$
K_{t} = 3 \cdot K_{b} \quad \text{(in N·m/A)}
$$

Therefore the final, most-commonly-used torque equation is:

$$
\boxed{\,T = K_{t} \cdot I_{ph}\,}
$$

### Step 7 — Final Compact Summary

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= K_{t} \cdot I_{ph} \\
  &= 3 \cdot K_{b} \cdot I_{ph} \\
  &= \frac{P_{m}}{\omega_{m}} \\
  &= \frac{3 \cdot E_{b} \cdot I_{ph}}{\omega_{m}} \\
  &= \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_{a}}{2\pi \cdot A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **linear relation** $T = K_t \cdot I_a$ is the most important KTU takeaway: *controlling the phase current directly controls the torque* — this is why BLDC drives are so widely used in robotics and EVs.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Voltage Equation (Back-EMF Relation)

For one conducting phase at any instant, applying KVL around the phase winding:

$$
V_{ph} = I_{ph} \cdot R_{a} + L \cdot \frac{dI_{ph}}{dt} + E_{b}
$$

In **steady state**, $dI/dt \approx 0$, so:

$$
V_{ph} = I_{ph} \cdot R_{a} + E_{b}
$$

Substituting $E_b = K_b \cdot \omega_m$ and $I_{ph} = T / K_t$:

$$
V_{ph} = \frac{T \cdot R_{a}}{K_{t}} + K_{b} \cdot \omega_{m}
$$

This is the **fundamental voltage equation** of a PMBLDC motor, which is the basis for the **field-oriented control** and **PI speed loop** design in drives.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Difference Between Mechanical and Electronic Commutators

| **S.No.** | **Parameter** | **Mechanical Commutator** | **Electronic Commutator** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Operating Principle** | A rotating copper-segment switch that physically slides against carbon brushes | A static solid-state switch (MOSFET/IGBT) that opens/closes on command |
| 2 | **Energy Transfer Type** | Conduction through **frictional contact** | Conduction through **semiconductor junctions** |
| 3 | **Components** | Copper segments, mica insulation, carbon/ graphite brushes, brush holders, springs | 6 power switches (VSI), gate driver IC, microcontroller, Hall sensors |
| 4 | **Location** | On the **rotor** (spinning part) | On the **stator** side / external (stationary) |
| 5 | **Mechanical Wear** | Yes — brushes and segments wear out, require periodic replacement | No wear (no moving contacts) |
| 6 | **Sparking / Arcing** | Yes, especially at high speeds and inductive loads → EMI | Negligible (soft-switched MOSFETs) |
| 7 | **Maintenance** | High (brush replacement every 1000–3000 hours typical) | Very low (solid-state, no consumables) |
| 8 | **Life Span** | Limited (~5000–10000 hrs) | Long (> 20,000 hrs, often matches bearing life) |
| 9 | **Speed Limit** | Limited by centrifugal force on commutator (~10,000 rpm) | Very high (no mechanical limit; only bearing-limited) |
| 10 | **Noise & EMI** | Acoustic + electrical (sparking) | Silent, low EMI (with proper snubbers) |
| 11 | **Cost of Construction** | Low (mature technology) | Higher (sensors, controller, inverter) |
| 12 | **Rotor Inertia** | Higher (windings + commutator on rotor) | Lower (only magnets on rotor) — better dynamic response |
| 13 | **Cooling** | Difficult (rotor heating) | Easier (stator windings can be water/jacketed cooled) |
| 14 | **Position Feedback** | Self-commutating (mechanical timing built-in) | External sensors (Hall, encoder, sensorless BEMF detection) required |
| 15 | **Efficiency** | 75–85 % | 85–95 % |
| 16 | **Application Examples** | Starter motors, hand drills, toy DC motors, traction motors (old) | Drones, EV traction, CNC spindles, computer fans, washing machines, robotics |
| 17 | **Reverse Polarity Risk** | If polarity reversed, motor runs in reverse automatically | Requires reprogramming of switching sequence |
| 18 | **Suitability for Hazardous Areas** | Poor (sparking hazard) | Excellent (intrinsically safe) |
| 19 | **Construction Complexity** | Simple, but with many precision parts | Complex electronics, but fewer mechanical parts |
| 20 | **Power-to-Weight Ratio** | Lower | Higher (no rotor windings) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PMBLDC Drive

The following Mermaid diagram captures the complete signal and power flow in a closed-loop PMBLDC drive system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DCPS[DC Power Supply V_DC] --> INV[3-Phase Inverter VSI]
    INV --> STW[Stator 3-Phase Y-Winding]
    STW --> RTM[Rotor with Permanent Magnets]
    RTM --> SHAFT[Mechanical Shaft Output]
    HALL[3 Hall Effect Sensors 120 deg apart] --> RTM
    HALL --> MCU[Microcontroller DSP]
    MCU --> GDRV[Gate Driver Circuit]
    GDRV --> INV
    SPDREF[Speed Reference Command] --> MCU
    MCU --> TQOUT[Torque Output]
    SHAFT --> LOAD[Mechanical Load]
    classDef power fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef sensor fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef control fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class DCPS,INV,STW power
    class HALL,RTM sensor
    class MCU,GDRV,SPDREF control
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **red-coloured control loop** (MCU → Gate Driver → Inverter) is the **electronic commutator**, replacing the entire mechanical brush-commutator assembly of a brushed DC motor.

## 4.2 Six-Step Commutation Sequence — Mermaid State Diagram

The 3 Hall sensor outputs ($H_a, H_b, H_c$) combine to form 6 valid states. In each state, exactly one high-side switch and one low-side switch conduct, energising two of the three phases.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> State1
    State1 : STATE 1 - Phase A pos, Phase B neg - Switches T1 and T6 ON
    State1 --> State2 : Hall signals 110 to 010
    State2 : STATE 2 - Phase A pos, Phase C neg - Switches T1 and T2 ON
    State2 --> State3 : Hall signals 010 to 011
    State3 : STATE 3 - Phase B pos, Phase C neg - Switches T3 and T2 ON
    State3 --> State4 : Hall signals 011 to 001
    State4 : STATE 4 - Phase B pos, Phase A neg - Switches T3 and T4 ON
    State4 --> State5 : Hall signals 001 to 101
    State5 : STATE 5 - Phase C pos, Phase A neg - Switches T5 and T4 ON
    State5 --> State6 : Hall signals 101 to 100
    State6 : STATE 6 - Phase C pos, Phase B neg - Switches T5 and T6 ON
    State6 --> State1 : Hall signals 100 to 110
    State1 --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Physical Layout — Cross-Sectional View of PMBLDC Motor (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stator
        CORE[Stator Core CRGO Laminations]
        WDG[3-Phase Y-Connected Copper Windings]
        SLT[Stator Slots]
        CORE --> SLT
        SLT --> WDG
    end
    subgraph Airgap
        AIR[Uniform Air Gap approx 1 to 2 mm]
    end

        ROT[Rotating Shaft SS41 Steel]
        MAGP[Permanent Magnets NdFeB]

    end

    Stator --> Airgap
    Airgap --> Rotor
    classDef iron fill:#a9a9a9,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef copper fill:#b87333,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef magnet fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class CORE,ROT iron
    class WDG copper
    class MAGP magnet
```


```mermaid
flowchart LR

        ARM[Armature Coil] --> BRUSH[Carbon Brushes]
        BRUSH --> COMM[Copper Commutator Segments]
        COMM --> ARM
    end
    subgraph ElectronicCommutator[Electronic Commutator in PMBLDC]
```
