# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a PMBLDC motor. List any two advantages it has over a conventional brushed DC motor. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
A Permanent Magnet Brushless DC (PMBLDC) motor is a synchronous motor in which the field is produced by permanent magnets on the rotor and the armature windings are placed on the stator. Commutation of the stator phases is performed electronically using a solid-state inverter and rotor position feedback (typically from Hall sensors), in place of a mechanical brush-commutator assembly.

**Advantages (1 Mark — any two):**
1. **Higher efficiency** (85–95 %) because rotor has no copper losses and no brush friction.
2. **Longer life and lower maintenance** since there are no brushes to wear out.
3. **Higher speed capability** limited only by the bearings, not the commutator.
4. **Better heat dissipation** because the stator (with the windings) can be externally cooled.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between mechanical and electronic commutation. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Mechanical Commutation** | **Electronic Commutation** |
|---|---|
| Performed by a rotating copper commutator and stationary brushes | Performed by static solid-state switches (MOSFETs/IGBTs) |
| Causes sparking, friction, and brush wear | No sparking, no wear, no contact |
| Requires periodic maintenance | Practically maintenance-free |

The electronic commutator thus provides **higher efficiency, longer life, silent operation, and suitability for hazardous environments** as compared to a mechanical commutator.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In torque-equation derivations, **never** skip the step showing $T = P_m / \omega_m$ — most students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 2. When listing Hall-sensor states, **memorize the 6-step commutation table**; the wrong sequence is the most common cause of -3 to -4 marks.
> 3. In construction diagrams, **label every part** (stator core, windings, rotor magnets, Hall sensors, inverter) — unmarked diagrams fetch zero marks.
> 4. For comparison questions, use a **table format** with at least 8–10 parameters; bullet points alone are insufficient for full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`(a)` Derive the torque equation of a Permanent Magnet Brushless DC (PMBLDC) motor, starting from the general DC machine torque expression. State all assumptions. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Write the general DC machine torque equation (1 Mark):**

$$
T = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_{a}}{2\pi \cdot A}
$$

**Step 2 — State the assumptions (1 Mark):**
1. Magnetic field is uniform in the air gap.
2. The flux per pole $\Phi$ is **constant** (since it is produced by permanent magnets, not a field winding).
3. Armature reaction is neglected.
4. The motor operates in the linear region of the magnet's B-H curve.
5. The number of parallel paths $A = 2$ (standard wave winding analog).

**Step 3 — Substitute to get the simplified PMBLDC torque expression (1 Mark):**

$$
T = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_{a}}{4\pi}
$$

Since $\Phi$ is constant, we can group all constants as $K_1$:

$$
T = K_1 \cdot I_{a}, \quad \text{where} \quad K_1 = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z}{4\pi}
$$

**Step 4 — Derive from the power balance (2 Marks):**
Electrical input power: $P_e = V_{DC} \cdot I_{DC} = 3 \cdot V_{ph} \cdot I_{ph}$ (after inversion)

Ignoring losses, mechanical power developed: $P_m = P_e = 3 \cdot E_b \cdot I_{ph}$, where $E_b$ is the per-phase back-EMF.

Back-EMF relation: $E_b = K_b \cdot \omega_m$

Hence: $P_m = 3 \cdot K_b \cdot \omega_m \cdot I_{ph}$

**Step 5 — Use $T = P_m / \omega_m$ to get the final equation (2 Marks):**

$$
T = \frac{3 \cdot K_b \cdot \omega_m \cdot I_{ph}}{\omega_m} = 3 K_b \cdot I_{ph}
$$

Defining $K_t = 3 K_b$:

$$
\boxed{\,T = K_{t} \cdot I_{ph} = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_{a}}{2\pi \cdot A}\,}
$$

---

**`(b)` With a neat diagram, explain the construction and principle of operation of a PMBLDC motor. Discuss the role of Hall sensors in electronic commutation. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Model Solution:

**Construction (3 Marks):**

A PMBLDC motor consists of the following parts:

1. **Stator (1 Mark):**
   - Laminated **C.R.G.O. silicon steel** core with slots.
   - Carries a **3-phase, Y-connected** distributed winding.
   - The three phases (A, B, C) are displaced by **120° electrical** in space.
   - Slots may be skewed to reduce cogging torque.

2. **Rotor (1 Mark):**
   - Made of permanent magnets (**NdFeB** typical) mounted on a ferromagnetic shaft.
   - Either **Surface-Mounted (SPM)** or **Interior Permanent Magnet (IPM)** type.
   - Number of poles is typically **4, 6, or 8** (2, 3, or 4 pole-pairs).
   - No windings and no commutator on the rotor → low inertia, high speed capability.

3. **Position Sensors (0.5 Mark):**
   - **Three Hall Effect sensors** mounted on the stator at the non-driving end.
   - They sense the polarity of the rotor magnet passing by and produce digital HIGH/LOW signals.

4. **Electronic Commutator / Inverter (0.5 Mark):**
   - **Three-phase VSI** with 6 MOSFETs/IGBTs ($T_1$–$T_6$).
   - A **microcontroller/DSP** reads the Hall signals and fires the appropriate switches.

**Principle of Operation (2 Marks):**

1. A **DC supply** ($V_{DC}$) is fed to the inverter.
2. The **Hall sensors** detect the instantaneous rotor position and output a 3-bit code.
3. Based on this code, the controller energises a specific pair of stator phases (always two at a time in 6-step commutation).
4. The current-carrying phase creates a **stationary magnetic field vector**; as the rotor magnet passes, the next Hall state triggers, the controller commutates to the next phase, and the **stator field vector jumps by 60°**.
5. The continuous six-step switching creates a **rotating magnetic field** that drags the permanent-magnet rotor along — producing **continuous unidirectional torque**.
6. The motor thus runs at a synchronous speed $n_s = 120f / P$ where $f$ is the electrical switching frequency.

**Role of Hall Sensors (2 Marks):**

- Provide **real-time rotor position feedback** to the controller.
- Generate the **6-step commutation sequence** ($001, 010, 011, 100, 101, 110$) corresponding to 60° electrical intervals.
- Allow **precise torque control** because the controller always knows which phase to energise.
- Enable **closed-loop speed control** when combined with a speed sensor.
- Alternatives: **sensorless commutation** (Back-EMF zero-crossing detection) for cost-sensitive applications.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**`(a)` Compare mechanical and electronic commutators in detail, with at least ten distinguishing points. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### Model Solution:

A **commutator** is a device that reverses the direction of current in the armature winding of a DC machine to keep the torque unidirectional. It can be of two types:

**Mechanical Commutator (in Brushed DC Motors):**
- A cylindrical assembly of copper segments mounted on the rotor shaft, insulated from each other by mica.
- Stationary carbon brushes slide on the rotating commutator and reverse the armature current at the correct instant.

**Electronic Commutator (in PMBLDC Motors):**
- A static solid-state circuit (Voltage Source Inverter) that performs the same current-reversal function using MOSFETs/IGBTs.
- Triggering is based on rotor position feedback (Hall sensors or sensorless BEMF detection).

**Comparison Table (6 Marks):**

| **S.No.** | **Parameter** | **Mechanical Commutator** | **Electronic Commutator** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Working principle | Physical sliding contact | Solid-state switching |
| 2 | Components | Copper segments, carbon brushes, springs | MOSFETs/IGBTs, gate drivers, microcontroller |
| 3 | Location | On rotor | Stator side (external) |
| 4 | Wear & tear | Yes, due to friction | No moving parts, negligible wear |
| 5 | Sparking | Yes, at high speeds/inductive loads | Negligible |
| 6 | Maintenance | High (brush replacement) | Very low |
| 7 | Life | 5000–10000 hrs | > 20,000 hrs |
| 8 | Speed limit | ~10,000 rpm | Limited only by bearings |
| 9 | Efficiency | 75–85 % | 85–95 % |
| 10 | Rotor inertia | High (windings + commutator) | Low (only magnets) |
| 11 | Heat dissipation | Poor (rotor heating) | Good (stator externally cooled) |
| 12 | Position feedback | Self-commutating (mechanical) | External (Hall / sensorless) |
| 13 | EMI | High (sparking) | Low (with proper snubbers) |
| 14 | Suitability for hazardous areas | Poor | Excellent |
| 15 | Cost | Low initial, high lifetime | Higher initial, low lifetime |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
The **electronic commutator** is vastly superior in efficiency, life, maintenance, and speed capability, at the cost of increased electronic complexity. It has enabled modern applications such as EVs, drones, and CNC machines where brushed DC motors are unsuitable.

---

**`(b)` With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a complete PMBLDC drive system. Discuss the role of the inverter, controller, and Hall sensors. (7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### Model Solution:

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DC Supply} \rightarrow &\text{DC Link Filter (Capacitor)} \rightarrow \text{3-Phase VSI Inverter} \\
\rightarrow &\text{3-Phase Stator Windings} \rightarrow \text{Rotor (PM)} \rightarrow \text{Mechanical Load}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Feedback Loop:**

$$
\text{Hall Sensors on Stator} \rightarrow \text{Microcontroller (DSP)} \rightarrow \text{Gate Drivers} \rightarrow \text{Inverter}
$$

The same diagram is shown in **Section 4.1** (Mermaid flowchart).

**Working Description (4 Marks):**

1. **DC Power Supply & Filter (0.5 Mark):**
   - A DC bus voltage $V_{DC}$ is obtained either from a battery, a rectified AC mains, or a DC-DC converter.
   - A large filter capacitor smooths the DC link and absorbs regenerative energy.

2. **3-Phase Voltage Source Inverter (1 Mark):**
   - Built with **6 switches** (MOSFETs for low voltage, IGBTs for high voltage) arranged as 3 half-bridge legs.
   - Each switch pair is anti-paralleled by a **freewheeling diode**.
   - The inverter converts the DC bus into a 3-phase quasi-square-wave AC voltage applied to the stator windings.

3. **Controller (Microcontroller/DSP) (1 Mark):**
   - Reads the **3 Hall sensor signals** (digital 3-bit code).
   - Implements the **6-step commutation table** to determine which two switches must be ON at any instant.
   - Generates the **PWM gate signals** to control the switches and hence the **phase voltage magnitude** (speed control).
   - May implement a **PI speed loop**, where the error between reference and actual speed adjusts the PWM duty cycle.

4. **Hall Sensors (0.5 Mark):**
   - Mounted 120° apart on the stator, sensing the rotor's magnetic field.
   - Produce 6 unique digital states (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6) for every electrical revolution.
   - Provide **synchronous, position-locked** switching — essential for proper commutation.

5. **PMBLDC Motor (0.5 Mark):**
   - The stator windings produce a **stepped rotating field** (60° jumps).
   - The rotor (permanent magnets) synchronously follows the field.
   - The shaft delivers **torque and speed** to the mechanical load.

6. **Closed-Loop Operation (0.5 Mark):**
   - Reference speed → controller → PWM duty cycle → inverter → motor speed → Hall sensors → controller.
   - The speed error drives the duty cycle → **precise closed-loop speed control**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

- ✅ A **PMBLDC motor** = permanent-magnet rotor + wound stator + electronic commutator.
- ✅ The **back-EMF is trapezoidal** in a true BLDC (sinusoidal = PMSM).
- ✅ The **stator has 3-phase Y-connected windings**, 120° apart in space.
- ✅ The **rotor has permanent magnets** (NdFeB), no windings, no commutator.
- ✅ **3 Hall sensors**, 120° apart, give 6 digital states for 6-step commutation.
- ✅ The **electronic commutator is a 3-phase VSI** with 6 switches (MOSFET/IGBT) + freewheeling diodes.
- ✅ **Torque equation: $T = K_t \cdot I_a$** — current directly controls torque.
- ✅ **Back-EMF: $E_b = K_b \cdot \omega_m$** — speed directly controls voltage.
- ✅ $K_t = 3 K_b$ for a 3-phase BLDC with 6-step commutation.
- ✅ **Mechanical commutator**: rotating copper + brushes → friction, sparks, wear, low life, low speed.
- ✅ **Electronic commutator**: static solid-state → no wear, no sparks, high life, high speed, high efficiency.
- ✅ PMBLDC **efficiency = 85–95 %** vs brushed = 75–85 %.
- ✅ **General torque equation**: $T = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a}{2\pi \cdot A}$ — valid for any DC machine.
- ✅ **Voltage equation**: $V_{ph} = I_{ph} R_a + E_b$ (steady state).
- ✅ **Synchronous speed**: $n_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ rpm.
- ✅ **6-step sequence**: $T_1T_6 \rightarrow T_1T_2 \rightarrow T_3T_2 \rightarrow T_3T_4 \rightarrow T_5T_4 \rightarrow T_5T_6$ (memorize the order).
- ✅ Applications: **EVs, drones, CNC, robotics, computer fans, washing machines, HVAC**.
- ✅ **Two phases conduct at any instant** in 6-step BLDC; this is the key distinction from PMSM (3 phases simultaneously).
- ✅ The **air-gap flux is constant** (no field control possible) — hence BLDC is *not* a constant-power, field-weakening friendly drive; IPM variants are used for field-weakening.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torque and EMF Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT412 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS MEASUREMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Torque and EMF Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **EMF Equation** of a DC machine establishes the mathematical relationship between the EMF induced in the armature winding ($E_g$ or $E_b$), the magnetic flux per pole ($\Phi$), the speed of rotation ($N$), and the constructional parameters (number of poles $P$, total armature conductors $Z$, and number of parallel paths $A$). It is a fundamental governing equation that quantifies the electromechanical energy conversion within the armature.

The **Torque Equation** expresses the rotational force developed in the armature of a DC machine as a function of the flux per pole, armature current, and the machine's geometric constants. In motor operation, this electromagnetic torque drives the mechanical load, while in generator operation, a prime mover must supply torque to overcome this electromagnetic resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every numerical on DC machines in Module 3 *will* test either the EMF equation, the torque equation, or a combined power-balance problem. Memorize the derivation steps, not just the final formula — KTU examiners award step-marks for the intermediate logical transitions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - $\pi = 3.14159\ldots$ (dimensionless)
> - $60$ (conversion factor from revolutions per minute to revolutions per second)
> - $2\pi$ (radians per mechanical revolution)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

**EMF Equation — The "Water Pump" Analogy:**
Imagine a water pump with rotating blades. The faster you spin the blades (speed $N$), the more water they push. Replace "water flow" with "magnetic flux cutting," and you have the essence of the EMF equation. Doubling the magnetic field strength ($\Phi$) is like fitting stronger magnets — you get more "push" per rotation. Adding more blades (more conductors $Z$) or arranging them in fewer parallel pipes (changing $A$) also affects the pressure (EMF) at the outlet.

**Torque Equation — The "Wrench Turning a Bolt" Analogy:**
Think of loosening a stubborn bolt. The longer the wrench handle (analogous to the machine's lever arm), the less force you need. In a DC machine, the torque is the rotational "force" trying to twist the shaft. The magnetic flux acts like the handle's rigidity, and the armature current is your applied effort. More current or stronger flux means more twisting power (torque).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Mnemonic:** **"ZAPP"** for the EMF equation numerator — **Z** (conductors), **A** (parallel paths in denominator), **P** (poles), **P** (in $60$ comes from poles $\times$ flux). Denominator always carries **60A**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Building Blocks

The EMF and Torque equations are derived from three fundamental physical principles working together:

**Step 1 — Flux Cutting Per Revolution**
As the armature rotates through the magnetic field established by the field poles, each conductor sweeps past $P$ poles per revolution. The total flux cut by one conductor in one full revolution equals $P\Phi$ webers.

**Step 2 — Rate of Flux Cutting (Faraday's Law)**
The EMF induced in a conductor is proportional to the rate of change of flux linkage. By dividing the total flux cut per revolution ($P\Phi$) by the time taken per revolution ($60/N$ seconds), we obtain the average EMF induced in a single conductor:

$$e_{\text{conductor}} = \frac{P\Phi N}{60} \text{ volts}$$

**Step 3 — Series Connection of Conductors (Winding Architecture)**
Conductors are not independent — they are connected in series within each parallel path. The number of conductors connected in series per parallel path is $Z/A$. Therefore, the EMF per parallel path (which equals the terminal EMF) is:

$$E_g = \frac{P\Phi N}{60} \times \frac{Z}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Why This Matters in Engineering**
This equation is the *design cornerstone* of every DC machine. An engineer selecting a motor for an electric vehicle uses this relationship to determine the back-EMF at a given speed, which in turn dictates the current drawn and hence the torque available for propulsion.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A}$ | EMF Equation of DC Machine (Generator/Motor) | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $e_{\text{cond}} = \dfrac{P\Phi N}{60}$ | EMF induced per single conductor | Volts (V) |
| 3 | Flux cut per revolution $= P\Phi$ | Total flux swept per turn | Weber (Wb) |
| 4 | Time per revolution $= \dfrac{60}{N}$ | Period of one rotation | seconds (s) |
| 5 | $T_g = \dfrac{P}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\Phi Z I_a}{A}$ | Gross Electromagnetic Torque | Newton-metre (N·m) |
| 6 | $T_g = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{P}{A} \cdot \Phi Z I_a$ | Alternative form of Torque Equation | N·m |
| 7 | $T_{sh} = T_g - T_{\text{mech losses}}$ | Shaft (Useful) Torque | N·m |
| 8 | $P_m = E_g \cdot I_a = T_g \cdot \omega$ | Electromechanical Power Balance | Watts (W) |
| 9 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular velocity in rad/s | rad/s |
| 10 | $A = P$ (Lap Winding), $A = 2$ (Wave Winding) | Parallel paths rule | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Sign Convention:** For a **DC Motor**, the induced EMF $E_b$ opposes the applied voltage $V$ (Lenz's Law) — hence it is called *back-EMF*. For a **DC Generator**, $E_g$ is the *generated* EMF delivered to the external circuit.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electric Vehicle Design:** Automotive engineers use the EMF equation to predict motor speed under varying battery voltage conditions.
- **Industrial Drives:** Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) controlling DC motors rely on the torque equation to deliver precise mechanical output.
- **Power Generation:** DC generators in wind turbines and backup systems use the EMF equation to size the field excitation system.
- **Traction Systems:** Indian Railways' DC traction motors (legacy systems) use these equations to compute tractive effort and acceleration curves.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the EMF Equation

Let us denote the following machine parameters (consistent with KTU 2024 notation):

- $P$ = Number of field poles (always an even integer: 2, 4, 6...)
- $\Phi$ = Useful flux per pole, in webers (Wb)
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = Speed of armature in revolutions per minute (rpm)
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths in the armature winding

**Step 1 — Flux cut by one conductor in one revolution**

As the armature rotates, one conductor passes under each of the $P$ poles once per revolution. Each pole contributes a flux $\Phi$. Therefore, the total flux cut by a single conductor in one complete revolution is:

$$\text{Flux cut per conductor per revolution} = P\Phi \text{ webers}$$

**Step 2 — Time required for one revolution**

If the armature rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, then the time taken for one revolution is:

$$\text{Time per revolution} = \frac{60}{N} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Average EMF induced in one conductor**

By Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction, the average EMF equals the rate of change of flux. Substituting:

$$\text{EMF per conductor} = \frac{\text{Flux cut}}{\text{Time taken}} = \frac{P\Phi}{\frac{60}{N}}$$

$$e_{\text{avg}} = \frac{P\Phi N}{60} \text{ volts}$$

**Step 4 — Conductors per parallel path**

The total armature conductors $Z$ are distributed among $A$ parallel paths. Hence the number of conductors in series within each parallel path is:

$$\text{Conductors in series per path} = \frac{Z}{A}$$

**Step 5 — Total EMF per parallel path**

Since the conductors in a single path are connected in series, their EMFs add up. The EMF across any one parallel path (which is the terminal EMF of the machine) is:

$$E_g = e_{\text{avg}} \times \frac{Z}{A}$$

$$E_g = \frac{P\Phi N}{60} \times \frac{Z}{A}$$

$$\boxed{E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A} \text{ volts}}$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Torque Equation

**Step 1 — Mechanical power developed in the armature**

The electromagnetic power converted from electrical to mechanical form in the armature equals the product of induced EMF and armature current:

$$P_m = E_g \times I_a \text{ watts}$$

where $I_a$ is the total armature current flowing through the machine.

**Step 2 — Substitute the EMF equation**

Substituting $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A}$:

$$P_m = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A} \times I_a$$

**Step 3 — Express power in terms of torque and angular velocity**

Mechanical power is also given by torque times angular velocity:

$$P_m = T_g \times \omega$$

where $\omega$ is the angular velocity in rad/s:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for torque**

Setting the two expressions for $P_m$ equal:

$$T_g \times \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A} \times I_a$$

Cancel $N$ from both sides (valid for $N \neq 0$):

$$T_g \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = \frac{\Phi Z P}{60\,A} \times I_a$$

Cancel $60$ from both sides:

$$T_g \times 2\pi = \frac{\Phi Z P}{A} \times I_a$$

Solving for $T_g$:

$$T_g = \frac{1}{2\pi} \times \frac{P}{A} \times \Phi Z I_a$$

$$\boxed{T_g = \frac{P}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\Phi Z I_a}{A} \text{ newton-metres (N·m)}}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | EMF Equation | Torque Equation |
|-----------|--------------|-----------------|
| Depends on speed $N$? | **Yes** (directly proportional) | **No** (cancelled out) |
| Depends on current $I_a$? | **No** | **Yes** (directly proportional) |
| Role in Motor | Determines back-EMF $E_b$ | Determines mechanical output torque |
| Role in Generator | Determines terminal voltage | Represents opposition torque |
| Proportional to $\Phi$ | Linearly | Linearly |
| Proportional to $Z$ | Linearly | Linearly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often confuse which quantity the EMF depends on. Remember: EMF $\propto N$ but Torque does *not* depend on $N$ in its final form — the speed cancels out algebraically.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (For Practice)

**Problem:** A 4-pole, wave-wound DC generator has 480 armature conductors and a flux per pole of 0.05 Wb. It runs at 1000 rpm. Calculate (i) the generated EMF, and (ii) the electromagnetic torque if the armature current is 200 A.

**Solution:**
Given: $P = 4$, $A = 2$ (wave), $Z = 480$, $\Phi = 0.05$ Wb, $N = 1000$ rpm, $I_a = 200$ A.

(i) EMF:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A} = \frac{0.05 \times 480 \times 1000 \times 4}{60 \times 2}$$

$$E_g = \frac{96000}{120} = 800 \text{ V}$$

(ii) Torque:

$$T_g = \frac{P}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\Phi Z I_a}{A} = \frac{4}{2\pi} \times \frac{0.05 \times 480 \times 200}{2}$$

$$T_g = \frac{4}{2\pi} \times \frac{4800}{2} = \frac{4 \times 2400}{2\pi} = \frac{9600}{2\pi}$$

$$T_g \approx \frac{9600}{6.2832} \approx 1527.89 \text{ N·m}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — EMF and Torque in a DC Machine

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the cause-and-effect chain that links the input electrical/mechanical quantities to the output EMF and Torque inside a DC machine.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["DC Supply V"] --> motorA["Armature Winding Z"]
    inputB["Field Supply If"] --> fieldA["Field Poles - Flux Phi"]
    fieldA --> airgapA["Air Gap Flux Density B"]
    motorA --> airgapA
    airgapA --> emfA["Induced EMF Eg"]
    emfA --> powerA["Electromechanical Power Pm = Eg x Ia"]
    motorA --> currentA["Armature Current Ia"]
    currentA --> torqueA["Torque Tg = f(Phi, Z, Ia, P, A)"]
    airgapA --> torqueA
    torqueA --> shaftA["Mechanical Shaft Output"]
    powerA --> shaftA
    emfA -.feedback.-> motorA

    classDef inputStyle fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#0D47A1
    classDef fluxStyle fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#E65100
    classDef outputStyle fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#1B5E20
    class inputA,inputB,motorA,currentA inputStyle
    class fieldA,airgapA fluxStyle
    class emfA,powerA,torqueA,shaftA outputStyle
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The **DC Supply** energizes the armature winding.
- The **Field Supply** establishes magnetic flux $\Phi$ in the poles.
- The interaction in the **air gap** simultaneously produces **induced EMF** (electromagnetic induction) and **torque** (force on current-carrying conductors).
- The feedback loop (dotted line) indicates that in motor mode, the induced EMF opposes the applied voltage.

---

### 4.2 Conceptual Mapping of Formula Variables

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Constructional Params"] --> nodeB["P - Number of Poles"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Z - Total Conductors"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["A - Parallel Paths"]
    nodeE["Operating Params"] --> nodeF["Phi - Flux per Pole"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["N - Speed in rpm"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Ia - Armature Current"]
    nodeB --> nodeI["EMF Equation Eg"]
    nodeC --> nodeI
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeB --> nodeJ["Torque Equation Tg"]
    nodeC --> nodeJ
    nodeD --> nodeJ
    nodeF --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeK["Power Developed Pm"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram carefully:** Notice that speed $N$ appears *only* in the EMF equation branch, while current $I_a$ appears *only* in the torque branch. This visual separation reinforces the analytical matrix presented in Section 3.3.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State and explain the EMF equation of a DC machine. List the factors affecting the generated EMF.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The EMF equation of a DC machine is:

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A} \text{ volts}$$

**Explanation of terms:**
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (Wb)
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = Speed of armature (rpm)
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths ($A = P$ for lap, $A = 2$ for wave)

**Factors affecting generated EMF:**
1. Magnetic flux per pole ($\Phi$) — proportional
2. Speed of rotation ($N$) — proportional
3. Number of armature conductors ($Z$) — proportional
4. Number of parallel paths ($A$) — inversely proportional
5. Winding type (lap/wave) — affects $A$

*(Allocation: 1 mark — formula, 1 mark — term definitions, 1 mark — factors list)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between the EMF equation and the torque equation of a DC machine in terms of their dependence on speed and current.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | EMF Equation | Torque Equation |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|
| Speed dependence | Directly proportional to $N$ | Independent of $N$ (speed cancels) |
| Current dependence | Independent of $I_a$ | Directly proportional to $I_a$ |
| Functional role | Determines induced voltage | Determines mechanical turning effort |
| Power linkage | $P_m = E_g I_a$ | $P_m = T_g \omega$ |

*(Allocation: 1 mark — speed, 1 mark — current, 1 mark — functional role table)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Derive the EMF equation of a DC generator starting from Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction. Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-pole, lap-connected DC shunt generator has 720 armature conductors. The flux per pole is 40 mWb and the armature runs at 1200 rpm. Calculate: (i) the generated EMF, and (ii) the electromagnetic torque developed when the armature current is 150 A. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q3(A):

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — State Faraday's Law:**
"Induced EMF is proportional to the rate of change of flux linkage."

**Step 2 — Flux cut per conductor per revolution:**
Each conductor passes under $P$ poles per revolution, so flux cut $= P\Phi$ Wb.

**Step 3 — Time per revolution:**
$\text{Time} = \dfrac{60}{N}$ seconds.

**Step 4 — EMF per conductor:**
$e = \dfrac{P\Phi N}{60}$ V.

**Step 5 — Conductors per path:**
Number of conductors in series per parallel path $= \dfrac{Z}{A}$.

**Step 6 — Total EMF (terminal):**

$$E_g = \frac{P\Phi N}{60} \times \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A} \text{ volts}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Armature winding is symmetrically placed.
2. Flux per pole is uniform (no fringing).
3. Brushes are placed at the magnetic neutral axis.
4. Winding is full-pitched.

*(Mark split: Step 1 = 1, Step 2-4 = 3, Step 5-6 = 2, Assumptions = 1)*

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $P = 6$, Lap winding $\Rightarrow A = P = 6$, $Z = 720$, $\Phi = 40 \text{ mWb} = 0.04$ Wb, $N = 1200$ rpm, $I_a = 150$ A.

**(i) Generated EMF:**

$$E_g = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A} = \frac{0.04 \times 720 \times 1200 \times 6}{60 \times 6}$$

$$E_g = \frac{0.04 \times 720 \times 1200}{60} = \frac{34560}{60} = 576 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Electromagnetic Torque:**

$$T_g = \frac{P}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\Phi Z I_a}{A} = \frac{6}{2\pi} \times \frac{0.04 \times 720 \times 150}{6}$$

$$T_g = \frac{1}{2\pi} \times 0.04 \times 720 \times 150 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \times 4320$$

$$T_g = \frac{4320}{2\pi} = \frac{4320}{6.2832} \approx 687.55 \text{ N·m}$$

*(Mark split: Substitution = 2, EMF answer = 1.5, Torque substitution = 2, Torque answer = 1.5)*

---

**Q3 (B). [KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]**
**(a)** Starting from the power balance equation ($P_m = E_g I_a = T_g \omega$), derive the electromagnetic torque equation of a DC machine. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole, wave-wound DC motor has 600 armature conductors. The flux per pole is 25 mWb and the motor draws an armature current of 80 A from a 250 V DC supply. Calculate: (i) the back-EMF at a speed of 1500 rpm, and (ii) the electromagnetic torque developed. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q3(B):

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Mechanical power developed:**
$P_m = E_g \cdot I_a$

**Step 2 — Substitute EMF equation:**
$P_m = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A} \cdot I_a$

**Step 3 — Power in mechanical form:**
$P_m = T_g \cdot \omega = T_g \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$

**Step 4 — Equate:**

$$T_g \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A} \cdot I_a$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $N$ and $60$:**

$$T_g \cdot 2\pi = \frac{\Phi Z P \cdot I_a}{A}$$

**Step 6 — Final torque equation:**

$$T_g = \frac{P}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\Phi Z I_a}{A} \text{ N·m}$$

*(Mark split: Step 1 = 1, Step 2 = 1.5, Step 3 = 1.5, Step 4-5 = 2, Step 6 = 1)*

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $P = 4$, Wave $\Rightarrow A = 2$, $Z = 600$, $\Phi = 25 \text{ mWb} = 0.025$ Wb, $I_a = 80$ A, $V = 250$ V, $N = 1500$ rpm.

**(i) Back-EMF:**

$$E_b = \frac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A} = \frac{0.025 \times 600 \times 1500 \times 4}{60 \times 2}$$

$$E_b = \frac{0.025 \times 600 \times 1500 \times 4}{120} = \frac{90000}{120} = 750 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $E_b = 750$ V > $V = 250$ V, this operating point is **physically impossible** for a motor at steady state. This indicates the motor is acting as a **generator** (negative slip), or the given data is inconsistent. For a motor: $E_b < V$. A KTU examiner would expect the student to detect this anomaly and comment accordingly for full marks.

**(ii) Electromagnetic Torque:**

$$T_g = \frac{P}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{\Phi Z I_a}{A} = \frac{4}{2\pi} \times \frac{0.025 \times 600 \times 80}{2}$$

$$T_g = \frac{4}{2\pi} \times \frac{1200}{2} = \frac{4 \times 600}{2\pi} = \frac{2400}{2\pi} \approx 381.97 \text{ N·m}$$

*(Mark split: Substitution = 2, EMF answer = 1.5, Anomaly detection = 1, Torque calculation = 2.5)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in EMF/Torque Questions:**
>
> 1. **Wrong parallel paths ($A$):** Students frequently write $A = P$ even for wave windings. **Rule:** Lap $\Rightarrow A = P$; Wave $\Rightarrow A = 2$. Mixing these costs 1 full mark.
>
> 2. **Unit mismatch on $\Phi$:** If flux is given in mWb (milliwebers), students forget to convert to Wb before substitution. This produces an EMF off by a factor of $1000$. **Always check units first.**
>
> 3. **Confusing EMF and Torque formulas:** Students sometimes write $E_g$ in the torque equation slot or vice versa. KTU examiners specifically test this confusion.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the $60$ in the denominator:** A common slip — the time conversion from minutes to seconds is skipped. This invalidates the entire numerical.
>
> 5. **Not commenting on physical anomalies:** If your calculated $E_b > V$ for a motor, do **not** blindly write the answer. Flag the inconsistency — KTU's 2024 OBE-based marking scheme rewards critical thinking.
>
> 6. **Shaft torque vs. electromagnetic torque:** If the question asks for *shaft torque*, you must subtract mechanical (friction + windage) losses. Using $T_g$ directly is half-marks only.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **EMF Equation:** $E_g = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60\,A}$ — speed-dependent, current-independent.
- **Torque Equation:** $T_g = \dfrac{P}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\Phi Z I_a}{A}$ — current-dependent, speed-independent.
- **Parallel paths rule:** Lap = $P$; Wave = $2$.
- **Power balance identity:** $E_g I_a = T_g \omega$ — this single equation connects the entire derivation chain.
- **Unit conversions:** mWb → Wb (multiply by $10^{-3}$); rpm → rad/s (multiply by $2\pi/60$).
- **Gross vs. Shaft Torque:** $T_{sh} = T_g - T_{\text{mechanical losses}}$.
- **Direction of EMF:** Generator — supports current; Motor — opposes applied voltage (back-EMF).
- **KTU Weightage:** This topic typically carries 7–14 marks per exam paper and is the foundation for DC machine, transformer, and induction machine analyses.
- **Cross-topic linkage:** The same derivation template extends to synchronous machines (with $\sqrt{2}$ factor for RMS) and is conceptually similar to transformer EMF equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torque-Speed Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT412 - ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS MEASUREMENTS & MACHINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Torque-Speed Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torque-Speed Characteristic** of an electric machine is the functional graphical/analytical relationship between the **electromagnetic torque (T)** developed by the machine and its **rotor speed (N or ω)**, expressed mathematically as $T = f(N)$ or $T = f(\omega)$.

In a polyphase induction motor, because rotor speed $N = N_s(1 - s)$ (where $s$ is slip and $N_s$ is synchronous speed), the characteristic is more commonly expressed as the **Torque–Slip Characteristic**, i.e., $T = f(s)$.

The KTU 2024 Scheme treats this topic as a fundamental bridge between machine theory and load–machine interaction, since any operating point of a motor–load system is the **intersection** of the motor's torque-speed curve and the load's torque-speed curve.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Paddle Wheel on a River"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a paddle wheel spinning on a flowing river. The water current represents the **rotating magnetic field** (synchronous speed), and the paddle wheel represents the **rotor**.

- If the paddle wheel is held still (slip = 100%, s = 1) → maximum relative motion → maximum induced current → strong push but no useful rotation. This is the **Starting Torque** condition.
- As the wheel starts spinning, it begins to "catch up" with the water flow. The relative speed drops, the induced EMF drops, and the torque changes.
- At one specific speed (slip ≈ 5–10% for typical motors), the torque reaches its **maximum value** (Pull-out Torque). This is where the wheel is most efficient at converting the flow into push.
- If the wheel could spin exactly at water speed (slip = 0, synchronous speed) → no relative motion → no current → **zero torque**. The wheel would coast.

This is precisely why an induction motor **cannot run at synchronous speed under load** — it needs slip to generate torque, just as the paddle wheel needs relative motion to push against the current.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Definitions (Mandatory Vocabulary)

| Term | Symbol | Standard Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Synchronous Speed | $N_s$ | Speed of rotating magnetic field = $\dfrac{120f}{P}$ rpm |
| Rotor Speed | $N$ | Actual mechanical speed of rotor in rpm |
| Slip | $s$ | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N}{N_s}$; dimensionless, often expressed in **%** |
| Starting Torque | $T_{st}$ | Torque developed at $s = 1$ (rotor at standstill) |
| Maximum / Pull-out Torque | $T_{max}$ | Peak value of torque-slip curve |
| Full-load Torque | $T_{fl}$ | Torque at rated load (typically $s \approx 0.03$ to $0.05$) |
| Pull-up Torque | $T_{pu}$ | Minimum torque during acceleration from standstill to breakdown (in cage motors) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard reference values** typically used in KTU numericals: $f = \mathbf{50\,Hz}$ (Indian supply standard), motor rating range $\mathbf{1\,kW}$ to $\mathbf{100\,kW}$, and full-load slip usually $\mathbf{3\%}$ to $\mathbf{5\%}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Derivation of Torque Equation

The torque developed by a 3-phase induction motor arises from the interaction between the stator rotating field and the rotor induced currents. The derivation proceeds through five rigorous stages:

**Stage 1 — Rotor EMF at slip *s*:**
When the rotor runs at slip $s$, the relative speed between rotating field and rotor conductors is $sN_s$. Hence the frequency of rotor EMF is $sf$, and the rotor EMF per phase is:
$$E_{2s} = sE_2$$
where $E_2$ is the standstill rotor EMF per phase.

**Stage 2 — Rotor Impedance:**
The rotor circuit has resistance $R_2$ and standstill reactance $X_2$. At slip $s$, reactance becomes $sX_2$.
$$Z_2 = R_2 + jsX_2$$

**Stage 3 — Rotor Current per Phase:**
$$I_2 = \frac{sE_2}{\sqrt{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}$$

**Stage 4 — Rotor Input (Air-gap Power):**
The rotor input is the total power transferred across the air gap.
$$P_g = 3 \cdot I_2^2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{s} = \frac{3 s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$$

**Stage 5 — Mechanical Power Developed:**
Out of the air-gap power, a fraction $(1-s)$ is converted to mechanical power, and the remainder $s \cdot P_g$ is lost as rotor copper loss.
$$P_m = (1 - s) P_g = \frac{3 s E_2^2 R_2 (1 - s)}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$$

Dividing by rotor mechanical speed $\omega_r = \omega_s(1 - s) = \dfrac{2\pi N_s}{60}(1 - s)$:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}}$$

This is the **master torque equation** of the 3-phase induction motor.

## 2.2 Condition for Maximum Torque — The "Magic Slip"

Differentiating $T$ with respect to $s$ and equating to zero:

$$\frac{dT}{ds} = 0 \implies R_2^2 - s^2 X_2^2 = 0$$

$$\boxed{s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}}$$

Substituting back into the master torque equation:

$$\boxed{T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Golden Results for KTU Exams:**
> 1. Slip at maximum torque is **directly proportional to rotor resistance** ($s_{mT} \propto R_2$).
> 2. Maximum torque itself is **independent of rotor resistance** ($T_{max} \not\propto R_2$). This is the theoretical basis for rotor-resistance starting (used in slip-ring induction motors).

## 2.3 Starting Torque Expression

Substituting $s = 1$ into the master equation:

$$\boxed{T_{st} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}}$$

**Ratio of Starting Torque to Maximum Torque:**

$$\frac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \frac{2 R_2 X_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2} = \frac{2a}{1 + a^2}$$

where $a = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ is a design ratio.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | rpm | $P$ = number of poles (must be even) |
| 2 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N}{N_s}$ | pu or % | Always between 0 and 1 for motoring |
| 3 | Rotor frequency | $f_r = sf$ | Hz | |
| 4 | Master torque equation | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$ | N·m | The "go-to" formula |
| 5 | Slip at max torque | $s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ | pu | Independent of load |
| 6 | Maximum torque | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}$ | N·m | Independent of $R_2$ |
| 7 | Starting torque | $T_{st} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$ | N·m | At $s = 1$ |
| 8 | Torque ratio | $\dfrac{T_{st}}{T_{max}} = \dfrac{2 R_2 X_2}{R_2^2 + X_2^2}$ | pu | Used for design analysis |
| 9 | DC Shunt Motor torque | $T = \dfrac{k\Phi V_a}{R_a} - \dfrac{k^2\Phi^2}{R_a}\omega$ | N·m | Linear-drooping characteristic |
| 10 | DC Series Motor speed | $\omega \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{T}}$ (linear region) | rad/s | Inverse-square type curve |
| 11 | Synchronous Motor | $\omega = \omega_s$ (constant) | rad/s | Pure vertical line characteristic |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Lift & Hoist Design:** $T_{st} > T_{load}$ is essential. Selection of slip-ring vs squirrel-cage motor depends on this ratio.
> - **Electric Vehicle (EV) Traction:** Modern EVs use induction motors deliberately operated in the **constant-torque region** (low speed, high torque) and **constant-power region** (field-weakening, high speed, lower torque).
> - **Conveyor Belts & Crushers:** Operate near $T_{max}$ region, so the **breakdown torque margin** is a critical specification.
> - **Pump & Fan Loads:** Operate in the **stable region** (low slip, $s < s_{mT}$), where $T \propto s$ approximately.
> - **Wind Turbine Generators:** Operate on the **generating mode** of the same curve (negative slip region).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Slip at Maximum Torque

We start from the master torque equation:

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s E_2^2 R_2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$$

Since $\dfrac{3}{\omega_s}$ and $E_2$ are constants for a given motor, define:

$$T' = s E_2^2 R_2 / (R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2)$$

Maximum torque occurs when $\dfrac{dT'}{ds} = 0$.

Using the quotient rule with numerator $u = s E_2^2 R_2$ and denominator $v = R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2$:

$$\frac{dT'}{ds} = \frac{u'v - uv'}{v^2}$$

Compute the derivatives:

$$u' = E_2^2 R_2$$
$$v' = 2s X_2^2$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{dT'}{ds} = \frac{(E_2^2 R_2)(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2) - (s E_2^2 R_2)(2s X_2^2)}{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2)^2}$$

Factor $E_2^2 R_2$ from numerator:

$$\frac{dT'}{ds} = \frac{E_2^2 R_2 \left[ R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2 - 2s^2 X_2^2 \right]}{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2)^2}$$

$$\frac{dT'}{ds} = \frac{E_2^2 R_2 \left[ R_2^2 - s^2 X_2^2 \right]}{(R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2)^2}$$

Setting this equal to zero:

$$R_2^2 - s^2 X_2^2 = 0$$

$$s^2 X_2^2 = R_2^2$$

$$\boxed{s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation II — Maximum Torque Value

Substitute $s_{mT} = \dfrac{R_2}{X_2}$ into the master torque equation:

First, compute the denominator at $s = s_{mT}$:

$$R_2^2 + (s_{mT} X_2)^2 = R_2^2 + \left(\frac{R_2}{X_2} \cdot X_2\right)^2 = R_2^2 + R_2^2 = 2R_2^2$$

Next, the numerator term $s R_2$:

$$s_{mT} R_2 = \frac{R_2}{X_2} \cdot R_2 = \frac{R_2^2}{X_2}$$

Substituting back:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{E_2^2 \cdot (R_2^2 / X_2)}{2R_2^2}$$

The $R_2^2$ terms cancel:

$$\boxed{T_{max} = \frac{3 E_2^2}{2 \omega_s X_2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical observation:** $T_{max}$ does **not** contain $R_2$. This means adding external rotor resistance (in slip-ring motors via slip rings and brushes) shifts the curve along the slip axis without changing the peak height. The curve "slides" rightward on the slip axis.

## 3.3 Derivation III — DC Shunt Motor Torque-Speed Equation

The back-EMF equation is:
$$E_b = k\Phi\omega$$
The armature circuit equation (with $V_a$ supply and $R_a$ armature resistance):
$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a \implies I_a = \frac{V_a - k\Phi\omega}{R_a}$$

Torque developed:
$$T = k\Phi I_a = \frac{k\Phi V_a}{R_a} - \frac{k^2\Phi^2}{R_a}\omega$$

This is a straight line with:
- **No-load intercept (T = 0):** $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V_a}{k\Phi}$
- **Slope:** $-\dfrac{k^2\Phi^2}{R_a}$ (negative slope → drooping characteristic)

At stall ($\omega = 0$):
$$T_{stall} = \frac{k\Phi V_a}{R_a}$$

## 3.4 Derivation IV — DC Series Motor Inverse-Speed Relation

In the linear (unsaturated) region:
$$\Phi \propto I_a \implies \Phi = k' I_a$$

The torque is:
$$T = k\Phi I_a = k k' I_a^2 \implies I_a = \sqrt{\frac{T}{k k'}}$$

From the circuit equation:
$$V_a = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se}) = k\Phi\omega + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

Substituting $\Phi = k' I_a$:
$$V_a = k k' I_a \omega + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

Solving for $\omega$:
$$\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k k' I_a}$$

In the operating region where armature drop is small:
$$\boxed{\omega \propto \frac{1}{I_a} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}}$$

This produces the famous **inverse-square curve** of series motors — high torque at low speed, with the risk of runaway at no-load.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — All Machine Types

| Machine Type | Torque-Speed Relation | Characteristic Shape | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3-φ Induction (Cage) | $T = \dfrac{3 s E_2^2 R_2}{\omega_s (R_2^2 + s^2 X_2^2)}$ | Hyperbolic peak at $s = R_2/X_2$ | Stable in $s < s_{mT}$ region |
| 3-φ Induction (Slip-ring, with added $R_{ext}$) | Same form, but $R_2 \rightarrow R_2 + R_{ext}$ | Peak shifts right; $T_{max}$ unchanged | Used for high starting torque |
| DC Shunt Motor | $T = \dfrac{k\Phi V_a}{R_a} - \dfrac{k^2\Phi^2}{R_a}\omega$ | Straight line, slight droop | Almost constant speed |
| DC Series Motor | $\omega \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{T}}$ | Inverse-square (rectangular hyperbola) | Very high starting torque; no-load dangerous |
| DC Compound (Cumulative) | Mix of shunt + series | Drooping, but with high $T_{st}$ | Good for intermittent heavy loads |
| Synchronous Motor | $\omega = \omega_s$ (constant) | Vertical line at $N_s$ | No slip; $T$ up to pull-out then drops |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Torque-Slip Curve Regions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Input supply V, f, P] --> B[Rotating Magnetic Field at Ns]
    B --> C{Rotor Slip s}
    C -->|s = 1| D[Region 1: STANDSTILL]
    C -->|0 less than s less than smT| E[Region 2: STABLE MOTORING]
    C -->|s = smT = R2 by X2| F[Region 3: MAXIMUM TORQUE Tmax]
    C -->|s less than 0| G[Region 4: GENERATING MODE]

    D --> D1[T = Tst, starting torque]
    D1 --> D2[Used for self-starting check]

    E --> E1[T increases linearly with s approx]
    E1 --> E2[Normal operating zone for pumps and fans]
    E2 --> E3[Stable: load increase reduces speed, increases T]

    F --> F1[Breakdown or pull-out torque]
    F1 --> F2[Tmax independent of R2]
    F2 --> F3[If load exceeds Tmax, motor stalls]

    G --> G1[Negative slip: rotor runs faster than Ns]
    G1 --> G2[Motor acts as generator]
    G2 --> G3[Used in regenerative braking of EVs]
```

```mermaid
    C --> C1[High starting current risk]
    C --> C2[Low starting torque]

    E --> E2[Stable high-slip operation]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electric Machine Family] --> B[DC Machines]
    A --> C[AC Machines]

    B --> B1[DC Shunt]
    B --> B2[DC Series]
    B --> B3[DC Compound]

    B1 --> B1A[Linear droop: T = a minus b omega]
    B1A --> B1B[Use: Lathes, fans, line shafts]
```

## 4.4 Operating-Point Stability Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Load Torque Curve TL of omega] --> B{Plot Both Curves}
    B --> C[Motor T vs omega]
    B --> D[Load TL vs omega]
    C --> E[Intersection Point]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Region of Intersection?}
    F -->|s less than smT| G[STABLE: small disturbance restores balance]
    F -->|s greater than smT| H[UNSTABLE: motor will stall or accelerate to Ns]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#548235,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The stability criterion (KTU frequently asked): **A motor–load system is stable if at the intersection, the slope of motor torque is greater than the slope of load torque.** This corresponds to operation in the $s < s_{mT}$ region for induction motors.
