# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Explain the difference between the timing mode and state mode of operation of a logic analyzer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In **Timing Mode**, the logic analyzer uses its **internal asynchronous clock** to sample all input channels. The display shows the logic state of each channel versus time, making it suitable for **hardware debugging** where timing relationships and glitches must be observed. In **State Mode**, the logic analyzer uses a **synchronous external clock** from the system under test (e.g., the microprocessor clock) to sample data, and the display shows the binary/hexadecimal state of buses at each clock edge, making it suitable for **software and protocol debugging**. Timing mode is asynchronous; state mode is synchronous.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**Define Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) and state its significance in power quality analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**THD** is defined as the ratio of the RMS value of all harmonic components (excluding the fundamental) to the RMS value of the fundamental, expressed as a percentage:

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_{h,rms}^2}}{V_{1,rms}} \times 100\%$$

**Significance:** THD quantifies the **deviation of a waveform from a pure sinusoid** due to non-linear loads (VFDs, SMPS, LED drivers). High THD causes overheating of transformers, neutral conductor overload, maloperation of protection devices, and resonance. IEEE 519 limits THD-V to **5%** at the point of common coupling for low-voltage systems.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a **logic analyzer**. List any **four triggering facilities** available in a logic analyzer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A non-sinusoidal voltage waveform has the following harmonic content:
- Fundamental: 230 V RMS
- 3rd harmonic: 12 V RMS
- 5th harmonic: 8 V RMS
- 7th harmonic: 4 V RMS

Calculate (i) Total RMS voltage, (ii) THD, (iii) Crest factor if the peak of the fundamental is 325 V. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A logic analyzer consists of:

1. **Probe Unit:** Multi-channel probes (typically 16, 32, or 64 channels) with high input impedance to minimize loading.
2. **Input Comparators:** Each channel has a comparator with a user-set threshold (TTL, CMOS, ECL, user-defined) that converts analog signals to digital logic levels.
3. **Sampling Circuit:** Samples channels at a defined clock rate (real-time or equivalent-time).
4. **Acquisition Memory:** Stores captured samples for display and post-processing.
5. **Triggering Circuit:** Detects specific conditions to start/stop capture.
6. **Display & Analysis Unit:** Shows timing waveforms or decoded data.

**Four Triggering Facilities:**

1. **Edge Trigger:** Captures on a rising or falling edge of a specific channel.
2. **Pattern Trigger:** Triggers when a defined combination of channel states occurs (e.g., ch1 = 1 AND ch2 = 0).
3. **Sequential Trigger:** Triggers after a specific sequence of events on different channels.
4. **Pulse-Width (Glitch) Trigger:** Triggers if a pulse is narrower or wider than a defined duration.
5. *(Additional)* **Delay Trigger** and **Counter Trigger** are also common.

**(7 marks: 4 for diagram + explanation, 3 for trigger facilities)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $V_1 = 230$ V, $V_3 = 12$ V, $V_5 = 8$ V, $V_7 = 4$ V RMS. Peak of fundamental = **325 V**.

**(i) Total RMS Voltage:**

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + V_3^2 + V_5^2 + V_7^2}$$

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{230^2 + 12^2 + 8^2 + 4^2}$$

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{52900 + 144 + 64 + 16} = \sqrt{53124}$$

$$\boxed{V_{rms} \approx 230.49 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) THD:**

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_3^2 + V_5^2 + V_7^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{144 + 64 + 16}}{230} \times 100\% = \frac{\sqrt{224}}{230} \times 100\%$$

$$\sqrt{224} \approx 14.97$$

$$\boxed{THD \approx 6.51\%}$$

**(iii) Crest Factor:**

$$CF = \frac{V_{peak}}{V_{rms}} = \frac{325}{230.49}$$

$$\boxed{CF \approx 1.41}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **Power Quality Analyzer** with a block diagram. List any **four common power quality disturbances** it measures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture and working of a Smart Energy Meter**. Discuss **TOU (Time-of-Use) billing** and its advantages. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A Power Quality Analyzer (PQA) continuously monitors electrical parameters and identifies deviations from ideal power. The working principle:

1. **Voltage and Current Sensing:** Voltage is sensed via PT or resistive divider; current via CT or Rogowski coil.
2. **Signal Conditioning:** Anti-aliasing filters remove frequencies above the Nyquist limit; op-amps scale signals to ADC range.
3. **High-Speed ADC:** Samples V and I at high rates (typically 256 samples/cycle).
4. **DSP Processing:** Performs RMS calculation, FFT for harmonic analysis, event detection (sag, swell, transient), flicker computation.
5. **Storage:** Events and trends stored in memory card.
6. **Communication:** USB / Ethernet / WiFi to PC for analysis via software (e.g., Fluke PowerLog).

**Four Power Quality Disturbances:**

1. **Voltage Sag (Dip):** Short-duration voltage drop (10–90% nominal).
2. **Voltage Swell:** Short-duration voltage rise (110–180% nominal).
3. **Harmonics:** Distortion due to non-linear loads.
4. **Transients:** Sub-cycle overvoltage spikes.
5. *(Additional)* Flicker, Frequency Deviation, Voltage Unbalance.

**(7 marks: 4 for block diagram + working, 3 for disturbances)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

A Smart Energy Meter is a microprocessor-based meter that measures energy and communicates remotely.

**Block Diagram & Working:**

1. **Voltage & Current Sensing:** Voltage divider for V, CT for I; signals are isolated and scaled.
2. **Signal Conditioning & ADC:** Signals are filtered and digitized at high sample rate.
3. **Microcontroller / DSP:** Computes true RMS voltage, RMS current, real power (P), reactive power (Q), apparent power (S), power factor, and integrated energy (kWh).
4. **RTC (Real-Time Clock):** Provides time-stamps for TOU billing.
5. **Non-Volatile Memory:** Stores consumption history, billing data, load profile, tamper logs.
6. **Display (LCD):** Shows cumulative kWh, current power, time, alerts.
7. **Communication Module:** RF / PLC / GSM / NB-IoT for two-way data transfer with utility.
8. **Tamper Detection:** Detects magnetic interference, neutral missing, cover opening, reverse current.

**Time-of-Use (TOU) Billing:**

In TOU billing, electricity is priced differently based on time periods:
- **Peak hours** (e.g., 6 PM – 10 PM): Highest tariff
- **Normal hours:** Medium tariff
- **Off-peak hours** (e.g., 10 PM – 6 AM): Lowest tariff

The smart meter records consumption in each time slot using its RTC and sends data to the utility.

**Advantages of TOU Billing:**
1. **Demand Shifting:** Consumers shift heavy loads (washing machines, EV charging) to off-peak hours, flattening the load curve.
2. **Reduced Peak Demand:** Lowers stress on generation and transmission, deferring capacity addition.
3. **Cost Savings:** Consumers save money by running loads during cheap hours.
4. **Grid Stability:** Encourages efficient use of existing infrastructure.
5. **Environmental:** Better load factor → higher plant efficiency → lower CO₂ per kWh.

**(7 marks: 4 for block diagram + working, 3 for TOU explanation)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always write **V, A, W, VAR, VA, Hz, kWh, %** — missing units often cost 0.5–1 mark per answer.
> 2. **THD formula misuse:** Many students write $V_{rms} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots}$ as "THD." This is **RMS of total voltage**, NOT THD. THD is the ratio (in %).
> 3. **Logic Analyzer vs Oscilloscope:** Examiners expect students to clearly state that logic analyzers show *logic states* (timing relationships), not *analog waveforms*. Drawing an oscilloscope screen for a logic analyzer question = lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Smart Meter Block Diagram:** Drawing only sensors without ADC/MCU/Communication/Display = lose 2–3 marks. The "smart" part comes from communication + processing.
> 5. **Skipping the Nyquist criterion** in logic analyzer sampling problems: even in short answers, mention $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
> 6. **Confusing Sag and Swell:** Sag = voltage DECREASES; Swell = voltage INCREASES. Examiners specifically check this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Logic Analyzers — Key Points
- Multi-channel instrument for digital signal analysis; shows **logic state vs time** (not voltage vs time).
- Two operating modes: **Timing (async)** and **State (sync)**.
- Sampling governed by **Nyquist criterion**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- Key trigger modes: **Edge, Pattern, Sequential, Pulse-width, Glitch**.
- Adjustable threshold comparators (TTL, CMOS, ECL).
- Used for hardware debugging, bus analysis, protocol decoding (I²C, SPI, UART, CAN).

### Power Quality Analyzers — Key Points
- Measures THD, sag, swell, transients, flicker, unbalance, frequency deviation.
- Standards: **IEEE 519, IEEE 1159, IEC 61000-4-30, EN 50160**.
- $THD = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^\infty V_{h,rms}^2}}{V_{1,rms}} \times 100\%$.
- Pure square wave has **THD ≈ 48.35%** (a frequently asked KTU problem).
- Pure sine wave has **CF = √2 ≈ 1.414**.
- $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\sum_{h=0}^{\infty} V_{h,rms}^2}$ (orthogonal components).
- Real power is the **sum of power at each harmonic** (orthogonality principle).

### Smart Energy Meters — Key Points
- Microprocessor/DSP-based; samples V and I; computes true RMS, P, Q, S, PF, E.
- Supports **TOU billing, maximum demand tracking, tamper detection**.
- **Bidirectional communication** with utility (PLC, RF, GSM, NB-IoT, LoRa).
- Foundation of **AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure)** and **Smart Grid**.
- Standards: **IS 16444, IEC 62056, IEEE 1377**.
- Replaces electromechanical meters because it correctly registers energy under **non-linear / harmonic-rich loads**.
- Energy: $E = \int_0^T P(t)\,dt$ in kWh; Demand = E/T in kW.

### Formula Quick-Reference
- $P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi$
- $Q = V_{rms} I_{rms} \sin\phi$
- $S = V_{rms} I_{rms}$
- $PF = P/S$
- $THD = \sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^\infty (V_h/V_1)^2}$
- $CF = V_{peak}/V_{rms}$
- $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist)
- $THD_{square\,wave} \approx 48.35\%$

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Tip:** For Part A (3 marks), always structure your answer as **Definition + Key Feature + Example**. For Part B (14 marks), follow **Diagram → Theory → Numerical → Conclusion** — this is the exact structure KTU examiners reward with full marks.
